body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

**Problem**

PR #11308 constrained all chat content to 78ch across the sidebar, editor tabs, and Agent Manager. On wider panels, roughly two thirds of the screen stays empty. Users with ultrawide monitors, or anyone using an editor tab, cannot trade horizontal space for readability.

The same constraint was applied in PR #4381 (January 2026) and drew the same feedback. Issue #11380 was filed and closed as intentional behaviour, but the use case stands: when screen space is available, letting content use it should be a user preference.

**Proposed Solution**

Add a `chat.readable_width` key to `kilo.jsonc`, accepted in Global Settings and overridable per project. The value is a CSS length string: `"78ch"`, `"100ch"`, `"none"`. When set to `"none"`, the reading lane constraint is removed and content fills the available panel width. The default is `"78ch"`. Existing users see no change.

Implementation is small. The CSS custom property `--chat-readable-width` in `chat-layout.css` is the single control point. Wiring it to a config value instead of a hardcoded 78ch is a one-variable change.

**Alternatives Considered**

- Local CSS override via Custom CSS and JS Loader. Fragile: selector or property renames in future updates break it silently.
- VS Code `settings.json` key. Kilo has moved away from VS Code settings toward `kilo.jsonc` as portable config.

**Prior Art**

The `kilo.jsonc` schema already supports configurable UI dimensions. PR #11147 added `console.context_sidebar_width` and `console.diff_style` to the same schema. This proposal follows that exact pattern.

comments.body: Chiming in to say that I agree that the change that intentionally limited the width of the chat window is silly, because the user can just adjust the width of the chat window in VS Code themselves, taking a way user choice feels wrong. Restoring a method to customize it would be a good idea.

title: [FEATURE]: Add chat.readable_width config to let users set or disable the reading lane

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I've accidentally found out that currently (unless I'm wrong), Kilo injects a hardcoded identity/personality prompt (the "soul" at [src/kilocode/soul.txt](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/kilocode/soul.txt)) at the start of every system message. This prompt defines core behavioral rules like tone, formatting constraints, and tool-usage guidance. While this creates a consistent Kilo experience, it is not customizable by users, and in some parts overlaps with the agent-level prompts (even with the built-in [src\session\prompt\default.txt](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/session/prompt/default.txt)).

I’d like to request an optional config field (e.g., `soul_prompt` or `identity_prompt`) that lets users override this built-in prompt. The default value would remain the current hardcoded text, so existing behavior is unchanged. Users could then:
- Adapt the agent’s personality to their preferred workflow
- Remove or soften constraints they find restrictive
- Use Kilo with a different "voice" or role definition

Because the field would be optional with a built-in default, this is fully backward compatible. Resetting to default would simply mean removing the field from config.

**Proposed shape:**
```jsonc
{
  "soul_prompt": "You are Kilo, ..." // optional; omit to use built-in default
}
```

**Files that would need changes:**
- [src/config/config.ts](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/config/config.ts) - add the new optional field to `Config.Info`
- `apps/web/src/app/config.json/extras.ts` - mirror the new key from `Config.Info`, I assume, as per [comment](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/config/config.ts#L216)
- [src/session/system.ts](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/session/system.ts) - expose the configured soul (falling back to the file default)
- [src/session/llm/request.ts](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/ec0dd783a8b8eae279ec4f2c478a0b209413a8a3/packages/opencode/src/session/llm/request.ts) - use the configured value instead of the hardcoded `SystemPrompt.soul()` call

I think this is a low-risk, user-facing customization that aligns with Kilo’s existing pattern of making prompts and agent behavior configurable (e.g., per-agent `prompt` fields, custom commands, etc.).

---

As a possible alternative, I believe the hardcoded prompt should be heavily loosened. It should not include anything that may overlap with agent-level prompts or enforce rules that user may not need for their specific task.
title: [FEATURE]: Make the "soul" identity prompt user-configurable

body:
### Description

When the chat transcript overflows, scrolling up by a single small wheel notch snaps the viewport back to the latest message — you have to scroll hard or drag the scrollbar manually to detach. Happens whether streaming is active or stopped.

**Root cause** — `createUserActivity` (`packages/kilo-ui/src/hooks/scroll-user-activity.ts:24-27`) marks `time = performance.now()` on pointer/key/touch, but `handleWheel` calls `onWheelUp()` **without** setting `time`:

```ts
const handleWheel = (event: WheelEvent) => {
  if (event.deltaY >= 0) return
  options.onWheelUp()   // ← no time = performance.now()
}
```

So `isRecent()` is always `false` for wheel input. Meanwhile `handleScroll` (`create-auto-scroll.tsx:108`) re-attaches unconditionally near the bottom:

```ts
if (distance < threshold()) {            // 10px
  if (store.userScrolled) setStore("userScrolled", false)
  return
}
```

The race on a small upward notch:

1. `wheel` (capture) → `onWheelUp` → `stop` → `userScrolled = true`
2. Browser scrolls up a few px (overflow-anchor / virtualization reflow can leave `distanceFromBottom < 10`)
3. `scroll` → `distance < 10` → `userScrolled = false` → **snaps back**

Since `handleWheel` never set `time`, nothing protects the pause. Only a scroll large enough that `distance >= 10` skips the re-attach branch — hence "scroll hard or use the scrollbar" being the only escape. Pointer-drag/scrollbar work because those go through `mark` (which sets `time`); wheel is the one path that bypasses it.

**Proposed fix** — two small changes in `packages/kilo-ui` (Kilo-owned, no `kilocode_change` markers needed):

1. Record user-activity time on wheel-up (`scroll-user-activity.ts`):

   ```ts
   const handleWheel = (event: WheelEvent) => {
     if (event.deltaY >= 0) return
     time = performance.now()
     options.onWheelUp()
   }
   ```

2. Guard the re-attach with recency (`create-auto-scroll.tsx`):

   ```ts
   if (distance < threshold()) {
     if (store.userScrolled && !userActivity.isRecent()) setStore("userScrolled", false)
     return
   }
   ```

Existing behavior is preserved: scrolling back down to the bottom re-attaches (outside the 300ms wheel-up grace window), the "scroll to bottom" button (`MessageList.tsx:372` → `resume`) still clears the pause, and streaming doesn't yank a paused viewport. The `create-auto-scroll.test.tsx` suite should continue to pass; a new test for "wheel-up within threshold does not re-attach" would lock in the fix.

---

**Quick patch for the current version**

For anyone who needs relief before this lands in a release, [`patch-kilo-autoscroll.ps1`](https://gist.github.com/CriDos/b7cddd4662ef7cbbecc4d4e68ae7aa4a) applies the same two fixes directly to the minified webview bundles of an installed extension. It auto-detects the installed `kilocode.kilo-code-*` folder under `.vscode\extensions` / `.vscode-insiders\extensions`, patches `webview.js`, `agent-manager.js`, and `kiloclaw.js`, and writes `.bak` backups so it can restore. Run it interactively for a menu, or with `-Action apply|status|restore`. Reload the VS Code window afterward. Re-apply after each extension update.

### Plugins

7.3.50

### Kilo version

7.3.50

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open a chat session with enough messages that the transcript overflows the viewport (auto-follow is active, i.e. you're at the latest message).
2. With the mouse wheel, scroll up by a single small notch.
3. Observe the viewport jumps back to the latest message instead of staying where you scrolled.
4. Repeat with a large/fast upward scroll, or by dragging the scrollbar thumb up — both detach correctly, confirming the small-wheel path is the broken one.
5. Same behavior with streaming stopped or in progress.

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

N/A (webview scroll logic; reproduced on Windows 11 / VS Code)

### Terminal

_No response_
title: fix(vscode): chat auto-scroll won't detach from latest message on small upward wheel scroll

body:
### Description

The CLI's config **write target** and **read precedence** disagree, so a value written to the recommended file (`kilo.json` / `kilo.jsonc`) can be silently overridden at read time by a lower-recommended file (`opencode.json` / `opencode.jsonc`). The write lands where the docs tell you, but the read prefers the other file, so the change appears to do nothing.

This was triaged before by the auto-fix bot as #6742 and #7621 (both auto-closed NOT_PLANNED/stale, never fixed), and the one PR that attempted it — #6749 — was closed unmerged. I re-verified it is still present on `main`, so I'm filing a fresh, consolidated report tying the open user-facing symptoms to the root cause. I came across this while building a cross-host config adapter and needed to know which file actually wins; I'm happy to send the PR.

**Write path — kilo.* wins.** `globalConfigFile()` (`packages/opencode/src/config/config.ts:448-458`) returns the first existing of `["kilo.jsonc", "kilo.json", "opencode.jsonc", "opencode.json", "config.json"]`, and `KilocodeConfig.ALL_CONFIG_FILES` is likewise kilo-first. So `updateGlobal()` writes to `kilo.*` when it exists. The docs agree this is intended — `packages/kilo-docs/pages/automate/mcp/using-in-cli.md:16` says *"The recommended file is `kilo.json`."*

**Read path — opencode.* wins.** The global merge (`config.ts:595-601`) loads in this order with last-wins semantics:

```
config.json → kilo.json → kilo.jsonc → opencode.json → opencode.jsonc
```

`mergeConfig` is `mergeDeep(target, source)` from remeda (`config.ts:67-69`), i.e. the **later** source overrides — so `opencode.jsonc` overrides `kilo.jsonc`. The project loop (`config.ts:826`) iterates `["kilo", "opencode"] as const` (opencode last), so `opencode.*` also wins for project config. (`GLOBAL_CONFIG_FILES` at `config.ts:329` is similarly opencode-last.)

So with both files present: **writes go to `kilo.jsonc`, reads favor `opencode.jsonc`.**

### Why this matters

The two stale root-cause tickets (#6742, #7621) read like internal bot reports, but this is a real user-facing class of bug. Two open/recent issues look like direct manifestations:

- **#8858** — changing a setting (e.g. default model) in the extension Settings UI "reverts back to original value after Save" when both `kilo.json` and `opencode.jsonc` exist. That is exactly write-to-kilo / read-from-opencode.
- **#10093** (open, v7.2.40) — an MCP removed from `kilo.json` keeps loading. If the same server is also defined in `opencode.jsonc`, the read path's opencode-wins ordering would keep it alive.
- **#7079** — `kilo mcp add` writes to `opencode.jsonc` while docs recommend `kilo.json`, leaving users maintaining two files.

Because the docs steer everyone to `kilo.json`, the de-prioritized read order means the recommended file quietly loses.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Create both global config files, setting the same key to different values:
   - `~/.config/kilo/kilo.jsonc` → `{ "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4" }`
   - `~/.config/kilo/opencode.jsonc` → `{ "model": "openai/gpt-4o" }`
2. Run the CLI and observe the effective `model`. It resolves to the `opencode.jsonc` value (`openai/gpt-4o`).
3. Update the value via the CLI/UI (which writes to `kilo.jsonc`). On the next read, `opencode.jsonc` still wins — the write appears to have no effect.
   (Same effect with an `mcp` server entry, matching #10093 / #7079.)

### Proposed fix

Pick one direction so that **write target == read winner**:

- **Option A (matches #7621, lowest blast radius): make the read order kilo-last so `kilo.*` wins**, aligning reads with `globalConfigFile()` / `ALL_CONFIG_FILES`:
  ```
  // global (config.ts:595-601)
  config.json → opencode.json → opencode.jsonc → kilo.json → kilo.jsonc
  // project loop (config.ts:826)
  for (const name of ["opencode", "kilo"] as const) { ... }
  ```
- **Option B (#6742 / the unmerged #6749): make `globalConfigFile()` write to the highest-precedence existing read file.** Note #6749's reviewer flagged a delete-patch edge case with this direction; Option A avoids that.

I'd lean toward Option A and can include a `mergeConfig`-order test covering "both files present → kilo wins" plus a changeset. Edits to the shared `packages/opencode/` file would be marked with `// kilocode_change`. Happy to open the PR against an accepted direction — just let me know which you prefer (or if PR #11129's config-source seam already moves this ordering).

### Kilo version

main (verified against `packages/opencode/src/config/config.ts` at HEAD); symptoms reported on 7.2.4 (#8858) and 7.2.40 (#10093)

### Operating System

N/A (config-resolution logic; reported symptoms on Windows 11)
title: fix(cli): config read-merge order contradicts write target — opencode.json silently overrides kilo.json

body: ### Description In the TUI, the sidebar always shows $0.00 spent while I'm actively using a session, even after the model has made many paid calls. But if I quit Kilo, restart it, and reopen the same session, the spent amount suddenly appears correctly. So the cost is being saved, but the sidebar doesn't show it until I resume the session. ### Plugins None ### Kilo version 7.3.46 ### Provider OpenRouter ### Model DeepSeek V4 Flash ### Steps to reproduce 1. Run kilo to open the TUI. 2. Start a new session. 3. Send one or more messages that trigger paid model calls. 4. Observe the right-hand sidebar under Context — the spent line stays at $0.00. 5. Quit Kilo (Ctrl+C or q). 6. Run kilo again. 7. Press Ctrl+P and select the same session from the session picker. 8. Observe the sidebar — the spent value now correctly reflects the accumulated cost. ### Screenshot and/or share link - This is when starting fresh session. - This is after exit and reopen the kilo session. ### Operating System macOS (Darwin 25.5.0) ### Terminal Terminal.app (Apple_Terminal) zsh

comments.body:
The cost is persisted fine; the live sidebar just never reads the updated value. Traced it on `main`:

The `Context › spent` line reads the **session aggregate** `session().cost`:
- `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/feature-plugins/sidebar/context.tsx:17,44`

The session store's `.cost` is only patched by a `session.updated.1` event (`context/sync.tsx:462-472`) or a full session-list load (startup / resume). During a live turn no `session.updated.1` is emitted — the session cost is bumped as a **DB-only projector side-effect**, not a published event:
- `session/projectors.ts:173-196` (`MessageV2.Event.PartUpdated`) → `applyUsage()` → `SessionTable.cost = cost + …` (`projectors.ts:32-44`). No `Session.Event.Updated` is fired here.

So mid-stream the client only receives the message/part events, which update the **message** cost (`context/sync-v2.tsx:140`, `currentAssistant.cost = event.data.cost`; accumulated server-side at `session/processor.ts:717`). The session aggregate the sidebar reads stays at its loaded value — 0 for a fresh session — until the next full load, i.e. resume. That's the exact symptom.

Corroboration: the sibling `tokens` / `% used` lines in the same component **do** update live, because they're derived from the message list (`context.tsx:28-29`), not the session aggregate. The same staleness affects every other live `session.cost` readout — `component/prompt/index.tsx:351` and `routes/session/subagent-footer.tsx:61`.

Two fix directions:

1. **Client-only (surgical):** derive `spent` from the messages, mirroring how `tokens` already works in the same component — `Math.max(session().cost ?? 0, Σ assistant-message .cost)`. The `max` keeps resumed totals correct if the loaded message window is only a subset. Fixes the sidebar with no server change.
2. **Server (root, fixes all three readouts at once):** publish a `Session.Event.Updated` after `applyUsage` bumps the cost, so the existing `sync.tsx:462` handler patches the store live. One change covers the sidebar + prompt footer + subagent footer; the trade-off is an extra event per step.

Happy to open the PR for whichever direction you prefer — I'd lean client-only as the lower-risk one.
Implemented in https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/11506

title: TUI "spent" shows $0.00 during live session, but correct after resume

body:
### Description

When `kilo serve` binds to the IPv6 wildcard address `::`, it prints only the raw bind URL. The URL helper correctly derives the usable loopback URL, but the serve command prints `Local` only when a separate network URL exists.

Actual output:

```text
kilo server listening on http://[::]:43188
```

Expected output:

```text
kilo server listening on http://[::]:43188
  Local:   http://[::1]:43188
```

The server itself works and is reachable through `[::1]`, so this is limited to user-facing URL discovery. IPv4 wildcard output is unaffected.

### Kilo version

Current `main` at `be692aa53040da9108b31d082eecb792e518c197`.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run `bun run --conditions=browser ./src/index.ts serve --hostname :: --port 43188 --print-logs` from `packages/opencode`.
2. Observe that no `Local: http://[::1]:43188` line is printed.
3. Verify the local endpoint works with `curl --noproxy "*" -g http://[::1]:43188/global/health`.

### Cause

`serverUrls("::", port)` returns the correct `local` URL, but `ServeCommand` gates both Local and Network output on `urls.network`. IPv6 wildcard binds intentionally have no discovered IPv4 network URL, so Local is suppressed.

Related to #11028.
title: fix(cli): show local IPv6 URL for wildcard binds

body:
### Description

A deterministic OpenAI-compatible provider can cause Kilo Code CLI to submit duplicate tool results for a completed shell tool-call ID. The standalone reproducer builds public `@kilocode/cli@7.3.46`, starts a local mock provider, and verifies the behavior without requiring this repository or any private fuzzing harness.


### Plugins

No

### Kilo version

7.3.46

### Steps to reproduce

Prerequisites: Docker, Python 3, and the GitHub CLI (`gh`) for the clone command below. The linked reproducer is self-contained and uses only Python standard-library modules plus Docker. It installs the affected CLI version from the public package registry, starts a local mock provider, and runs the CLI in an isolated workspace. The Docker run is limited to 2 CPUs and 4 GiB RAM by default.

Complete self-contained reproducer: https://gist.github.com/N0zoM1z0/28e8b621f479534a5adc377d1b85b054

The Gist contains `kilo-code-repeated-identical-tool-loop.reproduce.py`. Download and run:

```bash
gh gist clone 28e8b621f479534a5adc377d1b85b054 kilo-code-repeated-identical-tool-loop-reproducer
cd kilo-code-repeated-identical-tool-loop-reproducer
python3 kilo-code-repeated-identical-tool-loop.reproduce.py
```

To reuse an already-built local image:

```bash
python3 kilo-code-repeated-identical-tool-loop.reproduce.py --skip-build
```

Key output from a local run against the affected version:

```text
provider_requests_at_least: observed=93 expected>=2
duplicate_tool_result repeat_loop_initial: observed=92 expected>=2
REPRODUCED
```

### Screenshot and/or share link

No

### Operating System

Linux x86_64 Docker,

### Terminal

CLI in Linux x86_64 Docker,
title: [BUG] Kilo Code CLI repeats completed shell tool results

body: ### Description **Component:** `@kilocode/cli` daemon (`.kilo`, Bun-bundled) **Version:** `7.3.44` (npm global, `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/@kilocode/cli/bin/.kilo`) **Platform:** macOS, Intel, Sonoma. VSCodium connected to a remote workspace via open-remote-ssh. Note this is a fairly old MacBook so likely I'm noticing it here more, but the 200k/s will eat cpu on other platforms to. --- ## Evidence ### 1. Rwlock call rate while idle (15 s window) ```bash sudo dtrace -n ' pid$target::pthread_rwlock_rdlock:entry { @r = count(); } pid$target::pthread_rwlock_unlock:entry { @u = count(); } tick-1s { printa("rdlock/s: %@d unlock/s: %@d\n", @r, @u); clear(@r); clear(@u); } ' -p $(pgrep -x .kilo) ``` ``` 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 226826 unlock/s: 226826 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 212693 unlock/s: 212692 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 225184 unlock/s: 225184 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 206742 unlock/s: 206743 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 224752 unlock/s: 224752 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 229177 unlock/s: 229176 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 231282 unlock/s: 231282 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 221716 unlock/s: 221717 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 165948 unlock/s: 165947 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 93340 unlock/s: 93341 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 224009 unlock/s: 224009 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 189159 unlock/s: 189159 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 209826 unlock/s: 209826 0 3558 :tick-1s rdlock/s: 92512 unlock/s: 92512 ``` Acquires and unlocks are equal across every tick. ### 2. Top call sites of `pthread_rwlock_rdlock` (5 s window) ```bash sudo dtrace -n ' pid$target::pthread_rwlock_rdlock:entry { @[ustack(8)] = count(); } tick-5s { trunc(@, 5); printa(@); exit(0); } ' -p $(pgrep -x .kilo) ``` ``` 2 27792 :tick-5s libsystem_pthread.dylib pthread_rwlock_rdlock .kilo 0x00000001048d0b90+0x2b .kilo 0x00000001049e9f40+0x126 .kilo 0x00000001054f6400+0x1623 .kilo 0x0000000103c16910+0x24 .kilo 0x00000001049c19b0+0x2ad4 libsystem_pthread.dylib _pthread_start+0x63 libsystem_pthread.dylib thread_start+0xf 623534 libsystem_pthread.dylib pthread_rwlock_rdlock .kilo 0x00000001048d0b90+0x2b .kilo 0x00000001049ea240+0xa6 .kilo 0x00000001054f6400+0x1647 .kilo 0x0000000103c16910+0x24 .kilo 0x00000001049c19b0+0x2ad4 libsystem_pthread.dylib _pthread_start+0x63 libsystem_pthread.dylib thread_start+0xf 623535 ``` Both stacks share `0x1054f6400+0x1623` / `+0x1647` as the common ancestor — two offsets 36 bytes apart in the same function. Counts on the two stacks are equal (623534 / 623535). ### 3. Offsets relative to load address `0x10383b000` | Offset (abs) | File offset | | --- | --- | | `0x1054f6400` | `+0x1cbb400` | | `0x1048d0b90` | `+0x1095b90` | | `0x1049e9f40` | `+0x11ae940` | | `0x1049ea240` | `+0x11af240` | | `0x103c16910` | `+0x3db910` | | `0x1049c19b0` | `+0x11869b0` | --- ## Expected vs actual - **Expected:** idle daemon uses ~0% CPU. - **Actual:** ~47% sustained CPU, ~200k `pthread_rwlock_rdlock`/s, no active task. ## Workaround ```bash pkill -x .kilo ``` ### Plugins vscodium extension ### Kilo version 7.3.44 ### Steps to reproduce 1. `npm i -g @kilocode/cli` → got `7.3.44`. 2. Open a remote workspace in VSCodium via open-remote-ssh. 3. Do not start any Kilo task. Leave the daemon idle. 4. After ~30 s: ```bash ps -o pid,pcpu,etime,comm -p $(pgrep -x .kilo) # observed: etime > 2 days, pcpu ~ 47 ``` 5. `kill -STOP ` → CPU drops to 0. `kill -CONT ` → resumes. The shipped `.kilo` binary is stripped — `atos` returns `+ 0` for every offset below. ### Screenshot and/or share link ### Operating System macOS Sonoma ### Terminal n/a

body:
## Summary

A collection of UX improvements and feature requests for the Kilo CLI and TUI, covering notifications, usage display accuracy, worktree session management, and process lifecycle.

---

## Requirements

### 1. Restore UI Notifications in TUI (Bell Icon)

- [ ] Restore the bell icon notifications that were previously available in the TUI
- [ ] Notifications must be visible and functional when multiple TUI tabs/sessions are open simultaneously, so users know which session requires attention
- [ ] Notifications should be attention-grabbing but non-intrusive

---

### 2. Simplify Sound Notification Settings

- [ ] Simplify sound settings to a single enable/disable toggle (consistent with VS Code extension behavior)
- [ ] Evaluate adding desktop notification support in the CLI

---

### 3. Fix Cached Tokens Display in Usage Sidebar

Currently the "Cached" field in the session usage sidebar only shows cache *read* tokens. This should be corrected via one of:

- [ ] **Option A:** Relabel the field to "Cached Read" to accurately reflect what is shown
- [ ] **Option B:** Display cache read and cache write tokens as separate fields
- [ ] **Option C:** Sum read + write cached tokens and display as a single "Cached" total

Relevant file: `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/routes/session/usage.ts`

---

### 4. Worktree Session List & New Session Button

- [ ] When selecting a worktree in the TUI, display a list of previous sessions associated with that worktree
- [ ] Add a prominent "New Session" button in the worktree selection view (in addition to the existing sidebar button)
- [ ] Goal: enable fast switching between CLI → Agent Manager → Console workflows

---

### 5. `kilo console` Process Lifecycle in Terminal

- [ ] When running `kilo console`, the process should remain attached to the terminal so users can stop it with `Ctrl+C`
- [ ] Currently there is no straightforward way to stop the console process from the terminal

---

### 6. Move/Transfer a Session from Main Branch to a Worktree

- [ ] Users sometimes start a planning session on the main branch and later decide to implement changes on a worktree — there should be a way to move or reassign an existing session to a different worktree
- [ ] Add a move/reassign session action in the TUI or CLI

---

## Additional Context

These improvements collectively aim to make multi-session and multi-worktree workflows smoother in the CLI, and to keep the TUI on par with the VS Code extension in terms of notification and settings ergonomics.
title: CLI UX Improvements & Feature Requests

body:
### What happened?

This appears to be the same root cause described in #11256, but the issue is closed while the current public release still reproduces the problem.

Kilo Code / CLI `7.3.46` tries to load/install `@kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat`, but that package is not published on the public npm registry. This causes repeated startup/session errors in the VS Code/VSCodium extension and in CLI config checks.

```text
Failed to install plugin @kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat@latest: 404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@kilocode%2fplugin-atomic-chat - Not found
```

### Reproduction

1. Use Kilo Code / CLI `7.3.46`.
2. Run:

```bash
kilo config check --print-logs
```

3. Observe the external plugin loader trying to load `@kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat` and failing with npm 404.

### Actual behavior

Logs include:

```text
service=plugin path=@kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat loading plugin
service=plugin pkg=@kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat version=latest error=404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@kilocode%2fplugin-atomic-chat - Not found failed to install plugin
```

Also:

```bash
npm view "@kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat" version
```

returns npm `E404`.

### Expected behavior

The current public release should not try to install an unpublished package on startup.

If Atomic Chat is built in, it should be loaded through the internal plugin path. If it is optional or unavailable in the packaged build, it should not be added to the external plugin origins list.

### Workaround

Running with `--pure` avoids the error, but disables all external plugins:

```bash
kilo config check --print-logs --pure
```

Logs then show:

```text
service=plugin count=3 skipping external plugins in pure mode
```

This is not a good workaround for users who rely on other external plugins.

### Environment

- Kilo Code / CLI: `7.3.46`
- Platform: macOS / darwin arm64
- Editor integration: VSCodium / VS Code extension
- npm registry: `https://registry.npmjs.org/`

### Related issue

- #11256 appears to contain a detailed root cause analysis, but it is closed while `7.3.46` still reproduces the issue.
title: 7.3.46 still tries to install unpublished @kilocode/plugin-atomic-chat package

body:
### Description

The VS Code extension's webview retains per-session unsent input state forever after the session is deleted.

In `webview-ui/src/components/chat/PromptInput.tsx` (lines 53–55) three module-level `Map`s cache unsent input scoped by session ID:

- `drafts: Map<string, string>` — unsent prompt text
- `reviewDrafts: Map<string, ReviewComment[]>` — pending review comments
- `imageDrafts: Map<string, ImageAttachment[]>` — pending image attachments

`handleSessionDeleted` in `webview-ui/src/context/session.tsx` (line 1816) cleans up the session's `pendingOptimistic`, `aborts`, `submissions`, `sessions`, `messages`, `parts`, `toolParts`, `todos`, `pages`, `agentSelections`, `questions`, `suggestions`, etc. — but **never touches these three Maps**. So when a user deletes a session, the unsent text, pending review comments, and image attachments for that session are orphaned in webview memory forever.

Image attachments are the worst offender: each `ImageAttachment` carries a base64 `dataUrl` that can be hundreds of KB per image. Over a long editing session — especially for users who attach screenshots, then delete sessions — webview memory grows without bound. The Maps are also re-imported (or in the worst case, duplicated) and have no eviction policy of any kind.

Switching sessions still preserves drafts as expected via the `createEffect(on(draftKey, …))` save/restore in `PromptInput.tsx:171–192`, so this only affects the deletion path.

### Plugins

None

### Kilo version

7.3.46

### Steps to reproduce

1. Launch the extension with `bun run extension` from `packages/kilo-vscode/`.
2. In the sidebar, click "New Session".
3. In the prompt input, type a few paragraphs of text.
4. Drag several images (or large files) into the input so they attach.
5. Optionally add a review comment.
6. **Do not send.** Right-click the session in the history and choose Delete.
7. The previous draft strings and base64 image data URLs are still retained by `(closure) → Map` retainers from `PromptInput.tsx` / `draft-store.ts`. The Maps never shrink.

**Expected behavior:** `handleSessionDeleted` should clear the per-session entries in the draft Maps alongside the rest of its cleanup. Session switching should remain unaffected.

### Screenshot and/or share link

N/A

### Operating System

Ubuntu 24.04

### Terminal

Windows Terminal
title: Webview memory leak: unsent prompt drafts, review comments, and image attachments are never freed when a session is deleted

### Description

A deterministic OpenAI-compatible provider can trigger duplicate tool-result submission for an invalid empty write tool call. The linked standalone reproducer builds the public `@kilocode/cli@7.3.46` package, starts a local mock provider, and verifies the behavior without requiring this repository or any private fuzzing harness.

### Plugins

No

### Kilo version

7.3.46

### Steps to reproduce

Prerequisites: Docker, Python 3, and the GitHub CLI (`gh`) for the clone command below. The linked reproducer is self-contained and uses only Python standard-library modules plus Docker. It builds the affected CLI version from the public package registry, starts a local mock provider, and runs the CLI in an isolated workspace. The Docker run is limited to 2 CPUs and 4 GiB RAM by default.

Complete self-contained reproducer: https://gist.github.com/N0zoM1z0/952f442863252937dcc39f1a00b4657b

The Gist contains `kilo-code-empty-write-duplicate-execution.reproduce.py`. Download and run:

```bash
gh gist clone 952f442863252937dcc39f1a00b4657b kilo-code-empty-write-duplicate-execution-reproducer
cd kilo-code-empty-write-duplicate-execution-reproducer
python3 kilo-code-empty-write-duplicate-execution.reproduce.py
```

To reuse an already-built local image:

```bash
python3 kilo-code-empty-write-duplicate-execution.reproduce.py --skip-build
```

Key output from a local run against the affected version:

```text
REPRODUCED
```

No

non-interactive shell inside Docker

body:
## Bug Description

The `write` and `edit` tools cannot create or modify files under `.kilo/plans/`, even when the user has an explicit allow rule (`"**/.kilo/plans/*.md": "allow"`) in their config.

The root cause is `ReadPermission.harden()` in `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/read.ts`, which injects a `.kilo/**` deny rule **after** all user-defined rules. With `findLast()` semantics, this hardcoded deny always wins.

Meanwhile, `ConfigProtection` in `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/config-paths.ts` explicitly exempts `plans/`:

```typescript
const EXCLUDED_SUBDIRS = ["plans/"]
```

But this exemption is not respected by the hardening logic.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Add this to `.kilo/kilo.jsonc`:
```json
{
  "permission": {
    "edit": {
      "**/.kilo/plans/*.md": "allow",
      "*": "allow"
    }
  }
}
```
2. Try to create a file using the `write` tool: `.kilo/plans/test.md`
3. The tool is blocked by `.kilo/**` deny (injected by `ReadPermission.harden()`)

## Expected Behavior

`.kilo/plans/*.md` should be writable when:
- User has an explicit allow rule for the path
- The path falls under `EXCLUDED_SUBDIRS` which is designed for this purpose

## Actual Behavior

`ReadPermission.harden()` injects `.kilo/**` deny at the end of the flattened ruleset, overriding all user config allows. The `EXCLUDED_SUBDIRS` exemption in `ConfigProtection` is not checked by the hardening logic.

## Relevant Code

- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/read.ts` — `ReadPermission.harden()`
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/config-paths.ts` — `EXCLUDED_SUBDIRS = ["plans/"]`
- `packages/core/src/permission.ts` — `evaluate()` uses `findLast()`

## Environment

- Kilo version: latest (mimo-v2.5-free model)
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
title: ReadPermission.harden() blocks write/edit tools for .kilo/plans/*.md despite EXCLUDED_SUBDIRS exemption

body:
## Description

Kilo dynamically reduces `maxOutputTokens` based on an estimate of the outgoing request context. This protects models whose advertised output limit would otherwise leave too little context for text, tool schemas, and tool results.

The current calculation uses the raw serialized request estimate. For multimodal requests, that includes base64 image data and estimates it like ordinary text. Providers decode images and account for them as model-specific vision input, so encoded transport size is not equivalent to context tokens.

This can reduce the output allowance to an unusably small value even when the provider reports ample remaining context.

## Observed failure

A real session had:

- Model context limit: `1,000,000`
- Configured output allowance: `32,000`
- Five PNG attachments with approximately `1,896,418` encoded characters
- Provider-reported input usage: `298,152` tokens
- Kilo raw request estimate: `996,194` tokens
- Outbound `max_output_tokens`: `1,758`

The reasoning model consumed all `1,758` output tokens as reasoning and produced no visible response. The session ended with:

> The model hit its output limit while reasoning and produced no actionable output.

The output allowance came from:

```text
1,000,000 context
- 996,194 estimated input
-   2,048 safety margin
=   1,758 output tokens
```

The provider usage shows that the request was not actually close to the context limit.

## Reproduction

1. Use a vision-capable model with a large context window and reasoning enabled.
2. Build a long tool turn that reads or captures several PNG images.
3. Continue the same turn so the image attachments remain in the outgoing history.
4. Inspect the next provider request.
5. Observe that encoded image size can drive `max_output_tokens` close to the minimum even though provider-reported context usage remains much lower.

## Why this needs a design decision

Kilo must choose `max_output_tokens` before the provider tokenizes the current request, so the exact image token cost is unavailable at that point. We still want to preserve the dynamic cap for text and tool schemas because it prevents legitimate context-overflow failures on smaller models.

We need to distinguish two different measurements:

- Model context usage: text tokens, tool schema tokens, and vision tokens
- Request payload size: serialized JSON and encoded media bytes

Encoded media matters for HTTP or provider payload limits, but it should not be treated as ordinary model text when calculating remaining output context.

## Options

### 1. Exclude encoded media from the output-cap estimate

Use the existing media-normalized estimate for `maxOutputTokens`, where encoded media is replaced by a placeholder.

Pros:

- Small and low-risk change
- Preserves dynamic capping for text and tool schemas
- Prevents base64 size from consuming the output allowance
- Lets existing provider overflow handling compact and retry if vision tokens cause a real overflow

Cons:

- Underestimates actual vision-token usage
- A request near the real context limit may still require a provider rejection and retry

### 2. Reserve a fixed token amount per image

Use the normalized estimate plus a conservative fixed allowance for every image.

Pros:

- Still avoids counting base64 as text
- Provides some protection against underestimating vision input
- Straightforward to implement and test

Cons:

- Any fixed number is approximate
- Image cost varies by provider, model, dimensions, and detail settings
- Can still overestimate small images or underestimate large images

### 3. Add provider-aware vision token estimation

Estimate image tokens from dimensions and provider-specific preprocessing rules.

Pros:

- Best pre-request approximation
- Can retain a precise dynamic output cap for multimodal requests

Cons:

- Significantly more implementation and maintenance complexity
- Rules differ between providers and can change
- Some provider transformations are not exposed to the client

### 4. Remove dynamic output capping

Always send the configured or model output cap and rely on provider overflow errors.

Pros:

- Simplest behavior
- Provider remains the source of truth for all tokenization

Cons:

- Reintroduces the context-overflow failures that motivated dynamic capping
- Produces avoidable failed requests and compaction retries for text-heavy requests and small-context models

## Suggested direction

As an immediate fix, use the media-normalized estimate for output capping and retain the raw measurement only for payload-size recovery. Keep dynamic capping for text and tool schemas.

Longer term, make the units explicit:

```ts
{
  estimatedContextTokens: number
  payloadBytes: number
}
```

A provider-aware or fixed per-image vision reserve can then be added without conflating payload bytes with context tokens.

## Acceptance criteria

- Base64 image length no longer directly determines `max_output_tokens`.
- Large text and tool schemas still reduce the output allowance when needed.
- Payload-size recovery continues to account for encoded media.
- Real provider context overflows still trigger compaction and retry.
- Regression coverage includes both image-heavy and text-heavy requests.
title: Encoded images can collapse the dynamic output token allowance

body:
### Description

## Summary

Sending any prompt to **Claude Opus 4.7** or **Opus 4.8** (Bedrock-hosted, or the equivalent direct Anthropic / Vertex routes, or a LiteLLM / OpenAI-compatible proxy fronting Bedrock) fails before any tokens are returned. The model rejects `thinking.type=enabled` and expects `thinking.type=adaptive` + `output_config.effort` instead, but the provider transform still emits the legacy shape on several paths.

## Reproduction

### Environment

body:
### Description ### [Feature] Custom Provider — Add Bulk "Enable Reasoning Effort" Option Instead of Manually Creating Variants per Level.

Feature Request

In Custom Provider → Edit Provider, there is currently no quick way to enable Reasoning Effort across all levels. Users must manually create a separate Variant for each level (Low, Medium, High, Extra High) one by one, which is repetitive and time-consuming. (The current reasoning toggle only has the options 'Low', 'Medium' and 'High'.)

Current Behavior

  • To support multiple Reasoning Effort levels, users must:
    1. Manually create a Variant
    2. Name it
    3. Set the Reasoning Effort level
    4. Repeat individually for each level (Low, Medium, High, Extra High)
    5. (The current reasoning toggle only has the options 'Low', 'Medium' and 'High')

This process is tedious, especially when a user simply wants all levels available without custom naming or configuration per variant.

Requested Behavior

  • Add a single "Enable Reasoning Effort" toggle/option in Edit Provider
  • When enabled, it should automatically expose all standard levels (Low, Medium, High, Extra High) for the model
  • No manual Variant creation or naming should be required for standard reasoning levels

Suggested UI

[ ] Enable Reasoning Effort
     ↳ Automatically adds: Low / Medium / High / Extra High

Use Case / Motivation

  • Most users want all reasoning levels available without custom configuration
  • Manually creating Variants one by one for each level is repetitive and unnecessary friction
  • A single toggle would significantly speed up Custom Provider setup, especially for models that support standard reasoning effort tiers

Additional Notes

  • Manual Variant creation should still remain available for users who want custom-named or custom-configured reasoning variants
  • This request is specifically for a quick bulk-enable option as a faster alternative
  • Adding
### Plugins _No response_ ### Kilo version 7.3.xx ### Steps to reproduce _No response_ ### Screenshot and/or share link The current reasoning toggle only has the options 'Low', 'Medium' and 'High'. ### Operating System Windows 11 25H2 ### Terminal _No response_

comments.body: @chrarnoldus @markijbema @marius-kilocode @lambertjosh
Problem here is they always differ. So the implementation complexity of this right now outweighs the effort of adding 4 variants. How often are you configuring this on a daily basis @zakimimit ?
@zakimimit We don't really know the right variants for custom models. They are always different.

@marius-kilocode To better illustrate the scope of this problem and explain why this feature is necessary, consider the following example. Suppose I have **4 Claude models** from the same provider. Each model supports **5 reasoning effort levels**: * Low * Medium * High * Extra High * Max To expose every option to users, I currently have to create and name a separate variant for each model/effort combination. **4 models × 5 effort levels = 20 variants.** Now imagine the same setup with **6 GPT models**, or additional model families such as **MiniMax** or **GLM**. The number of required variants grows very quickly, making configuration repetitive and difficult to maintain. This is why having a **default effort** option (for example, as a checkbox or global setting) would be valuable. ### Instead of manually creating every model/effort combination, I could enable the default effort with a single click, significantly reducing the number of variants that need to be created and managed.

title: [Feature] Custom Provider — Add Bulk "Enable Reasoning Effort" Option Instead of Manually Creating Variants per Level

body:
## Problem

The test workflow runs a root `bun install`, which installs dependencies for every workspace even though only six packages define `test:ci`. This makes the Bun package cache unnecessarily large and increases archive restore/extraction time.

Measured locally on the current monorepo:

- Full Bun cache: approximately 2.4 GB physical / 2.0 GB logical
- Filtered test-workspace cache: approximately 1.5 GB physical / 1.28 GB logical
- The filtered install removed large unused test-job dependencies such as Next.js and Storybook

Large unavoidable entries remain, particularly LanceDB's glibc and musl native binaries (about 266 MB combined), because the CLI depends on indexing.

## Proposed investigation

- Define the complete workspace set needed by `bun turbo test:ci --filter='!@kilocode/kilo-jetbrains'`, including transitive build dependencies.
- Pass matching `--filter` arguments through `.github/actions/setup-bun`'s existing `install-flags` input in the test workflow.
- Validate from a cold cache on both Linux and Windows.
- Confirm all Turbo test and prerequisite build tasks still execute successfully.
- Compare install time, cache archive size, and total job wall time before adopting it.

Avoid globally omitting optional dependencies: native packages such as LanceDB are distributed through `optionalDependencies` but are required at runtime.
title: ci: reduce test dependency cache with filtered workspace installs

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I'd like to propose adding a `model` field to the `agent_manager` tool's task definition, allowing users or the agent manager to specify which LLM model each worktree session should use.

Currently, when creating Agent Manager worktrees session via the `agent_manager` tool, all sessions inherit the model from the parent session. This limits flexibility when orchestrating parallel tasks that could benefit from different models.

## Motivation

**Use case 1: Cost optimization**
Different tasks have different complexity requirements. A task doing simple file generation could use a faster/cheaper model, while a task requiring deep architectural reasoning could use a more capable model.

**Use case 2: Model specialization**
Some models excel at specific tasks. You might want:
- One worktree using a model good at refactoring
- Another using a model optimized for test generation
- A third using a model strong at documentation

**Use case 3: Resource constraints**
When running multiple parallel worktrees, you might want to distribute load across different providers

## Proposed implementation
Add an optional `model` field to each task in the `agent_manager` tool:

## Expected behavior

- When `model` is specified, the worktree session should initialize with that model
- When `model` is omitted, it should fall back to the current session's model (backward compatible)
- The model should be configurable per-session within the worktree after creation
- Invalid model names should fail gracefully with a clear error message

## Benefits
1. **More powerful orchestration**: Agents can intelligently assign models based on task requirements
2. **Cost efficiency**: Use expensive models only where needed
3. **Flexibility**: Users can leverage different providers' strengths for different tasks
4. **Scalability**: Better resource distribution when running many parallel worktrees

## Additional context
This would be particularly valuable as Agent Manager evolves to support more autonomous multi-agent workflows, where the orchestrating agent needs fine-grained control over the execution environment of each worker.
title: [FEATURE]:Allow specifying model per Agent Manager task

body:
LM Studio integration currently appears to require models to be hardcoded or manually configured. Since LM Studio already exposes an endpoint for listing available models, Kilo Code should be able to populate the available model list dynamically.

LM Studio endpoint:
```
curl http://localhost:1234/api/v1/models \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $LM_API_TOKEN"
```
### Proposed behavior

When LM Studio is selected as the provider, Kilo Code should:
1. Call LM Studio’s model listing endpoint.
2. Populate the available model dropdown/list from the returned models.
3. Avoid requiring users to manually hardcode model names in config.
4. Gracefully handle cases where:
    - LM Studio is not running.
    - The endpoint is unavailable.
    - Authentication is required or invalid.
    - No models are currently loaded or available.


### Why this matters
This would make LM Studio integration smoother and less error-prone. **_Users often add, remove, rename, or swap local models_**, so hardcoded configuration becomes stale quickly.

Dynamic discovery would also align better with how other model providers expose available models through API endpoints.

### Documentation update
The custom model documentation should also be updated to explain:
- How LM Studio model discovery works.
- Whether users still need to manually add custom models.
- How to configure the LM Studio base URL and API token.
- What fallback behavior exists if model discovery fails.

### Related context
This was previously discussed in issue #8173, where @ricardofiorani noted that LM Studio already provides a model-listing endpoint and suggested that the integration should use it instead of relying on hardcoded config.

> Hey @marius-kilocode sorry for touching this topic again, but could you maybe please reopen the issue to make a proper implementation on this one?
> 
> Since LM Studio already provides an endpoint to list the models:
> ```
> curl http://localhost:1234/api/v1/models \
>   -H "Authorization: Bearer $LM_API_TOKEN"
> ```
> 
> I believe the list should be populated from the endpoint instead of config hardcoded for a more seamless integration.
> 
> I would drop the PR myself but I already blew my Claude quota for the day 😅  

 _Originally posted by @ricardofiorani in [#8173](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/8173#issuecomment-4177944487)_
title: Dynamically load LM Studio models from /api/v1/models instead of hardcoding

body: ### Description ### [Bug] Session Randomly Aborts with 504 Timeout Error — "The operation was aborted" After sending a prompt, the session stops abruptly and unexpectedly with the error message "The operation was aborted". The session appears to be running normally and then suddenly halts without warning. This has been observed multiple times across different sessions, indicating an intermittent/random timeout issue rather than an isolated incident. The sessions are interrupted unpredictably, causing loss of progress and a confusing, unhelpful error message with no clear recovery path **Note:** I test MiniMax 3 , Nex-N2-Pro(kilo gateway and openrouter) , GLM5.1 --- **Expected Behavior** - Sessions should run to completion without unexpected timeouts - If a timeout does occur, it should be handled gracefully with a clear, user-friendly message and an option to retry or resume - A 504 timeout should not present as an UnknownError to the user **Actual Behavior** - The session randomly aborts mid-execution ❌ - The error is surfaced as an UnknownError rather than a clear timeout message ❌ - No automatic retry or recovery mechanism is triggered ❌ - This has occurred across multiple, separate sessions — not a one-off issue ❌ --- **Possible Cause** - Backend/proxy request timing out (`504 Gateway Timeout`) on long-running operations - No retry/backoff logic in place for transient timeout errors - Error handling surfaces raw backend errors as `UnknownError` instead of a meaningful timeout message --- ### Plugins _No response_ ### Kilo version 7.3.XX ### Steps to reproduce 1. Open Kilo Code and start a session 2. Send a prompt and let the agent run 3. The session runs normally for a period of time 4. Randomly and unexpectedly, the operation stops 5. Observe the following error: ```json { "name": "UnknownError", "data": { "message": "{\"code\":504,\"message\":\"The operation was aborted\",\"metadata\":{\"error_type\":\"timeout\"}}" } } ``` ### Screenshot and/or share link ### Operating System Windows 11 25H2 ### Terminal _No response_

body:
## Summary

Add a first-class mem0 plugin for Kilo that mirrors the pattern from [mem0ai/mem0/integrations/mem0-plugin/.opencode-plugin/opencode-mem0.ts](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0/tree/main/integrations/mem0-plugin/.opencode-plugin). The plugin should let every Kilo session (CLI + VSCode extension) read and write memories through the Mem0 platform, with zero per-user setup beyond `MEM0_API_KEY` in env.

## Why

- `MEM0_API_KEY` is the only state required.
- Mem0 already has an opencode plugin in their own repo that we can port — the surface area is small and well-tested.
- Self-learning agents need persistent memory across sessions, rigs, and bead boundaries. Skills and AGENTS.md are static; mem0 is dynamic.

## Proposed hooks

- `chat.message` → on the first user turn, `search_memories(query=user_text)` and inject results into `output.parts` as a context block, so the model always knows prior context for the user / project / branch.
- `tool.execute.after` → on `bash`, `read`, `write`, `edit` tools where the result is non-trivial, call `add_memory` with redacted output (strip `sk-…`, `m0-…`, `ghp_…`, AWS keys, etc.).
- `session.idle` (via the `event` hook) → summarize the session and store as a `session_summary` memory.
- `experimental.session.compacting` → push a memory-fetch hint into the compaction context so post-compact turns stay informed.

## What ships in this PR

- `packages/plugin/src/mem0.ts` — the plugin (mirroring `opencode-mem0.ts`, adapted for Kilo's `Plugin` type from `./index.js` and `tool()` helper from `./tool.js`).
- README section in `packages/plugin/README.md` with install instructions.
- A small unit test (`packages/plugin/test/mem0.test.ts`) covering the redaction regex and the memory-shaped call.

## Out of scope (for this PR)

- TUI plugin (separate entry point).
- Multi-tenant provider registration.

## Env

- Required: `MEM0_API_KEY`
- Optional: `MEM0_USER_ID` (defaults to `os.userInfo().username`), `MEM0_APP_ID` (defaults to git remote).

## Related

- Upstream mem0 plugin we are porting: https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0/tree/main/integrations/mem0-plugin/.opencode-plugin
- Kilo plugin docs: https://kilo.ai/docs/automate/extending/plugins

comments.body: Cross-referenced in mem0ai/mem0#5606: this work will land as a PR in the mem0 integrations directory, not kilocode.

title: plugin: official mem0 client (add_memory / search_memories hooks for cross-session memory)

comments.body:
I had a similar issue directly after installing ripgrep:
`Transport error (GET https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/releases/download/15.1.0/ripgrep-15.1.0-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl.tar.gz)`

Solution for me was to Restart Extension Host (Ctrl+Shift+P -> Restart Extension Host) - seems to work fine now.

VS Code 1.124.0, Kilo Code 7.3.46, Debian 13

Thanks!  I gave that a try and it did not work.  I think what may be happening is Kilo code checks to see if ripgrep is available.  That fails to find it even though ripgrep is normally available in a standard VSCode install (and I've even downloaded a new copy of ripgrep and put it in the path elsewhere).   Since it fails that initial check, Kilo Code immediately attempts to pull the latest ripgrep from the BruntSushi repo.  It fails that (isolated system) so then it marks that ripgrep is unavailable.  

Sometimes rule files are respected and it's able to find the tool per rule/agent file instructions.  Other times rule files are ignored and it forgets that ripgrep does, in fact, exist.

title: VS-Code + Kilo Code offline use fails to properly use ripgrep (search)

body:
### Description

## Environment

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Kilo Plugin | `@kilocode/plugin` 7.3.45 |
| OS | Windows 10 Pro 10.0.19045 (Build 19045) |
| Chrome | 142.0.7444.176 (system install, `C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe`) |
| Shell | PowerShell 5.1 |
| Proxy | clash-verge @ 127.0.0.1:7890 |

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Kill any existing Chrome processes
2. Invoke `kilo-playwright_browser_navigate({ url: "about:blank" })` (or any URL)

## Actual Behavior

```
Error: async initializeServer: Target page, context or browser has been closed
```

**Browser log sequence:**
```
<launching> chrome.exe ... --remote-debugging-pipe about:blank
<launched> pid=4836
<gracefully close start>     鈫?MCP initiates shutdown
<kill>
<will force kill>
taskkill stderr: Process "4836" not found
<process did exit: exitCode=0, signal=null>
```

## Root Cause Analysis

Chrome 142 has changed its process model on Windows. When started in headed mode, the **launcher process exits immediately (exitCode=0) after spawning child processes** that handle the actual browser work (rendering, GPU, network, etc.). This is consistent across all headed launch modes (with or without `--remote-debugging-pipe`).

The Playwright integration monitors the PID of the process it launched. When that PID exits, it interprets this as a browser crash and initiates a graceful close, which kills all remaining child processes via `taskkill`.

**Verified: 16 Chrome child processes were alive and functioning when the parent exited.**

## Evidence

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| `--headless=new` mode | Success (main process stays alive) |
| `chrome.exe --remote-debugging-pipe about:blank` (manual launch) | 16 child processes running after parent exit |
| `chrome.exe about:blank` (headed, no pipe) | Main exits, child processes stay alive |
| Kilo `kilo-playwright_browser_navigate("about:blank")` | Parent exits 鈫?MCP kills all children 鈫?"browser has been closed" error |

Manual verification: after launching chrome with `Start-Process`, the parent process `HasExited = true` with `ExitCode = 0`, but 16 Chrome child processes remain visible via `Get-Process chrome`. The browser is fully functional 鈥?it just no longer has a parent process that the MCP server can track.

## Suggested Fixes (detailed)

### Fix 1: Track the child process tree instead of the launched PID (Recommended)

When Chrome is launched, instead of watching only the PID returned by `child_process.spawn()`, use OS-level process tree monitoring:

- On Windows: use `wmic process where (ParentProcessId=<pid>) get ProcessId` or PowerShell `Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter "ParentProcessId=<pid>"` to enumerate child PIDs
- Select the child process with the longest lifetime or the process holding the CDP pipe connection as the "actual browser process" to monitor
- Only consider the browser dead when ALL child processes have exited (or when the CDP pipe closes without reconnection)
- This approach is more robust than tracking the parent PID and handles Chrome's new process architecture across versions

### Fix 2: Add a grace period after parent process exit before initiating shutdown

When the launched PID exits with code 0, don't immediately trigger shutdown:

- Add a configurable cooldown period (e.g., 3-5 seconds) before declaring the browser dead
- During this period, continue attempting to establish/re-establish the CDP connection via the remote debugging pipe
- If CDP connects successfully within the grace period, the parent death was normal Chrome behavior 鈥?proceed normally
- If CDP fails to connect within the grace period, then initiate shutdown as a genuine failure

### Fix 3: Fallback to headless mode for Chrome 142+ on Windows

As a compatibility safety net:

- Detect Chrome version at launch time (parse `chrome.exe --version` output)
- For Chrome >= 142 on Windows, if headed mode with `--remote-debugging-pipe` is attempted, automatically add `--headless=new` flag
- Or provide a user-facing configuration option in `kilo.json` to force headless mode:
  ```json
  "playwright": {
    "headless": true,
    "channel": "chrome"
  }
  ```
- `--headless=new` mode was verified to keep the main process alive and works correctly with the current MCP integration

---

**Impact:** This makes Playwright browser tools completely unusable on Windows machines with up-to-date Chrome. The workaround options (headless fallback, process tree tracking) should be straightforward to implement.


### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

_No response_
title: Playwright browser fails on Windows with Chrome 142 — launcher process exits immediately, mistaken as crash

body:
## Description

The cloud app's Session Actions dialog tells users they can resume a cloud session locally with:

```sh
kilo --session ses_... --cloud-fork
```

Running that command can fail immediately with:

```text
Error: Session not found: ses_...
```

The CLI validates the `--session` value against local session storage before importing the cloud session. Since the cloud ID is not local yet, validation fails before `--cloud-fork` has a chance to import it.

## Plugins

None reported.

## Kilo version

Current CLI main branch behavior.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open a cloud-hosted session in the Kilo cloud app.
2. Open Session Actions.
3. Copy the CLI fork command, for example `kilo --session ses_1393f0111fffeUA4LeemDIe3QH --cloud-fork`.
4. Run the command locally in the target repository.

## Expected behavior

The CLI imports the cloud session, creates a local fork, and opens/resumes that imported local session.

## Actual behavior

The CLI reports `Session not found` for the cloud session ID before import.

## Screenshot and/or share link

Reported from `https://app.kilo.ai/cloud/chat?sessionId=ses_1393f0111fffeUA4LeemDIe3QH`.

## Operating System

Linux.

## Terminal

Shell command reproduced from terminal.

Signed-off-by: _gpt-5.5 on behalf of matt_
title: CLI cloud fork rejects valid cloud sessions as missing

Cohere's compatibility layer explicitly rejects 'medium' and 'low' reasoning tiers, strictly requiring "high". 2. In "Generate Commit Message": The action fails silently without surfacing the error to the UI. comments.body:
Hi! Not a maintainer, but I went looking and figured I'd share what's findable so you don't keep hunting:
Neither kilocode-landing nor community-contributions-dashboard exists as a public repo in the Kilo-Org org. The current public repos are kilocode, kilocode-legacy, kilo-design, kilo-marketplace, docs, and a handful of smaller ones — none under those names.
A couple of things that might explain what you saw:

The apps/web/src/app/admin/community-prs/page.tsx path is from the older monorepo layout. The current kilocode repo was restructured (it's now packages/, sdks/vscode/, etc. — no apps/ directory), so that web app and its admin page now live in kilocode-legacy rather than kilocode. The deployed community-contributions-dashboard.vercel.app doesn't seem to have been broken out into its own standalone public repo.
The kilocode-landing folder in the VS Code sidebar of the hero screenshot is just the local workspace folder name from whoever recorded it — it doesn't necessarily correspond to a published repo.

body:
**Note:** This issue was filed by KiloClaw on my behalf.

**Describe the bug**
The `kiloclaw-morning-briefing` plugin fails to deliver the briefing, outputting: `delivery: telegram skipped (missing_target)`, even when `channels.telegram.defaultTo` and `channels.telegram.accounts.default.defaultTo` are correctly configured in `openclaw.json`.

**Root Cause Analysis**
The plugin resolves delivery routes by reading `api.config.channels`. However, the OpenClaw config API redacts sensitive fields (e.g., `botToken` becomes `"__OPENCLAW_REDACTED__"`). The plugin's `resolveDeliveryRoute` function apparently fails to correctly extract the `defaultTo` target from this redacted config object, falling back to `missing_target`. 

Manual message sending via the `message` tool works perfectly with the same target, proving the config is valid and the bot is functional. The issue is isolated to how the morning briefing plugin parses the runtime config.

**To Reproduce**
1. Configure Telegram with `defaultTo` and `accounts.default.defaultTo` in `openclaw.json`.
2. Run `/briefing run`.
3. Observe the output: `delivery: telegram skipped (missing_target)`.

**Expected behavior**
The plugin should either:
1. Successfully parse the unredacted `defaultTo` field from the config.
2. Fall back to a documented plugin-specific config path (e.g., `plugins.entries.kiloclaw-morning-briefing.config.defaultTo`) if the channel config is partially redacted.

**Environment**
- KiloClaw (hosted OpenClaw)
- Plugin: `@kiloclaw/kiloclaw-morning-briefing`

body:
## Summary

The Kilo system prompt contains a rule that reads in relevant part:

> "NEVER end your result with a question or request to engage in further conversation. Formulate the end of your result in a way that is final and does not require further input from the user."

While the intent — preventing conversational bloat (e.g., "would you like me to continue?") — is reasonable, the rule as written is **over-broad** and creates perverse incentives:

1. **Promotes confident hallucinations**: An agent that encounters ambiguity (two possible files to edit, unclear intent, missing context) is forbidden from asking for clarification and instead invents an assumption and commits to it.
2. **Suppresses legitimate clarification loops**: Queries like "Which of these three files should I modify?" or "I see two interpretations — which one?" are task-blocking ambiguity resolution requests, not social turn-passing. The rule lumps them together.
3. **Removes confidence signaling**: The agent cannot say "I'm unsure — please confirm" or end with a lightweight check. Instead it must package uncertainty as false certainty.

## Suggested Resolution

Replace the blanket prohibition with a narrower rule that distinguishes between:

**Forbidden:** Purely social turn-passing ("Do you want me to continue?", "Would you like me to explain more?") — i.e., questions that engage in further conversation without advancing the task.

**Permitted:** Task-blocking ambiguity resolution ("Which file should I edit?", "I see two approaches — which do you prefer?") — i.e., questions necessary to proceed with certainty.

A possible replacement formulation:

> "Do not end your response with purely social follow-up questions ('Do you want me to continue?', 'Would you like to know more?'). However, if the task genuinely requires clarification to proceed correctly, you MAY ask a concise, single-sentence question. Prefer to state your best assumption first and only ask when the ambiguity would likely produce the wrong result."

## Impact

This change would reduce hallucination risk, improve output correctness on ambiguous tasks, and better align agent behavior with user intent — all while preserving the anti-drift benefit of the original rule.

comments.body: @OzMan21 This is what we have the question tool for at the moment, did you disable that one?
I am not aware of it. I'll look for it.

title: System prompt rule 'NEVER end your result with a question' suppresses legitimate clarification loops, promotes confident hallucinations

body:
### Description

**Labels:** `bug`, `android`

**Plugin type:** Kilo Android App + Kilo Code VSCodium extension (shared session)

**App version:** v1.0.0 (67)

## Description

When a Kilo Code session running on a remote machine (VSCodium + Kilo Code extension) is paused waiting for user input — e.g. a clarification question in Plan mode — the Kilo Android app shows no new messages and gives no indication that the session requires attention. The session appears idle rather than blocked.

## Expected behavior

The pending question should appear in the Android app's session view, allowing the user to answer it from mobile just as they would from the desktop client.

## Observed behavior

The Android app shows no new messages. The session appears idle. The user has no way to know input is required unless they check the desktop client directly.

## Impact

Beyond the UX inconvenience, this bug actively leads users into a second bug: if the user eventually does interact with the session from the Android app (e.g. by sending a message), the session resumes but the Android client submits a malformed model ID (`kilo/MiniMax-M3` instead of `minimax/minimax-m3`), causing an `UnknownError`. The two bugs compound: the display failure pushes the user toward the only interaction path that triggers the model ID failure.

See related issue https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/11114.

## Notes

- The session in question was using **MiniMax Token Plan (minimax.io) / MiniMax-M3** via the Kilo Gateway in Plan mode.
- The VSCodium desktop client showed the question correctly; only the Android app failed to surface it.
- It is not yet clear whether this affects all question types or only Plan-mode clarification prompts.


### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

7.3.41

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start a Plan-mode session in Kilo Code (VSCodium extension on a remote VPS).
2. The session reaches a point where it asks the user a question (clarification/confirm prompt).
3. Open the Kilo Android app connected to the same account/session.
4. Observe: no new message is shown; the session appears to have no pending activity.

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Android 15

### Terminal

_No response_
title: Bug: Android app does not show pending session questions, making blocked sessions appear idle

body: ### Description ### Ollama embedding spam / excessive `/v1/embeddings` requests causing log flooding **Summary** **Environment** * OS: Arch Linux * Ollama version: 0.30.7 * KiloCode version: 7.3.42 * Backend: Ollama running via systemd (`ollama serve` on localhost:11434) --- **Problem** When KiloCode is running, it repeatedly calls: ``` POST /v1/embeddings 127.0.0.1 200 ``` at a very high frequency (multiple times per second). This leads to: * constant embeddings generation * excessive CPU/GPU usage * extremely noisy Ollama logs in `journalctl` * llama.cpp internal slot debug logs flooding the system journal: ``` slot update_slots slot release srv update_slots ``` --- **Observed behavior** * Continuous embedding requests even when no visible user action is happening * Ollama logs become unusable due to verbosity * Systemd journal fills rapidly * Connections show persistent local client: ``` ESTAB ... users:(("kilo",pid=...,fd=...)) ``` --- **Expected behavior** * Embeddings should be batched or cached * Requests should only be sent when needed (e.g., on file change or explicit indexing) * No continuous polling of `/v1/embeddings` --- **Steps to reproduce** 1. Start Ollama server locally 2. Run KiloCode 3. Observe network connections or `journalctl -fu ollama` 4. See continuous `/v1/embeddings` requests without user interaction --- **Impact** * High CPU/GPU usage * Disk/log spam * Reduced usability of local LLM setup * Potential unnecessary model load and cost (if remote backend) --- **Possible hint** This looks like a runaway indexing / embedding refresh loop or missing caching layer in the embedding pipeline. --- This doesn't resolve the issue ``` /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service.d/override.conf: [Service] Environment=OLLAMA_LOG_LEVEL=warn ```
title: Ollama embedding spam / excessive /v1/embeddings requests causing log flooding

body: ### Description ## Problem I am currently developing a Visual Studio Code extension that adds functionality to the "Kilo Code" extension. Its name is [AIFlowBridge](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=LaurentOngaro.aiflowbridge). **When using the new Minimax "M3" model, The "Reasoning Effort" dropdown in the chat input is hidden, even though M3 supports an optional reasoning mode. The same dropdown works correctly for DeepSeek V4 Pro / Flash (which also supports reasoning).** This is caused by a hardcoded list in `packages/opencode/src/provider/transform.ts` (function `variants()`) that explicitly hides the dropdown for any model whose `id` matches `minimax` (case-insensitive). ## Root cause In `variants()`, this block returns `{}` (no variants → no dropdown) for any model whose lowercase id contains `minimax`: ```ts if ( id.includes("deepseek-chat") || id.includes("deepseek-reasoner") || id.includes("deepseek-r1") || id.includes("deepseek-v3") || id.includes("minimax") || // ← blocks MiniMax M3 too id.includes("k2p") || id.includes("qwen") || id.includes("big-pickle") ) return {}; ``` **MiniMax M3** supports an optional reasoning mode via the `reasoning_split: true/false` body parameter. - DeepSeek V4 Pro / Flash → `id = "deepseek-v4-pro"` → no pattern matches → dropdown shown ✅ - MiniMax M3 → `id = "minimax-m3"` (lowercase of `MiniMax-M3`) → matches `id.includes("minimax")` → dropdown hidden ❌ ## Suggested fix Narrow the block to only the MiniMax generations that actually don't support reasoning, e.g.: ```ts if ( id.includes("deepseek-chat") || id.includes("deepseek-reasoner") || id.includes("deepseek-r1") || id.includes("deepseek-v3") || id === "minimax-m2" || id === "minimax-m2.1" || id === "minimax-m2.1-highspeed" || id === "minimax-m2.5" || id === "minimax-m2.5-highspeed" || id === "minimax-m2.7" || id === "minimax-m2.7-highspeed" || // Note: M3 is NOT in the list - it supports reasoning via reasoning_split id.includes("k2p") || id.includes("qwen") || id.includes("big-pickle") ) return {}; ``` Or, more cleanly, return explicit variants for `MiniMax-M3`: ```ts if (id === "minimax-m3") { return { none: { reasoning: { enabled: false } }, high: { reasoning: { enabled: true } }, }; } ``` This works because downstream gateways (e.g. [AIFlowBridge](https://github.com/LaurentOngaro/AIFlowBridge)) already translate `reasoning: { enabled: ... }` into MiniMax's native `reasoning_split` field. Translating at the gateway level means the Kilo Code client can send a standard OpenAI-compatible `reasoning` block and the upstream provider-specific adaptation happens transparently. ## Environment - Kilo Code: 7.3.41 - AIFlowBridge: 1.5.2 (or any version exposing MiniMax M3 as a reasoning-capable model) - MiniMax M3: supports `reasoning_split: true/false` per upstream API documentation ### Plugins None ### Kilo version 7.3.41 ### Steps to reproduce 1. Install the AIFlowBridge VS Code extension (or any OpenAI-compatible gateway that exposes MiniMax M3) 2. Configure the gateway's `baseUrl` as a custom OpenAI provider in Kilo Code 3. Select `MiniMax-M3` from the model picker 4. Observe: no "Reasoning Effort" dropdown in the chat input (it IS present for DeepSeek V4 Pro / Flash) 5. Manually send `reasoning: { enabled: true }` in a request body - it works correctly end-to-end because the gateway translates it to `reasoning_split: true` ### Screenshot and/or share link Once the following steps are done: - AIFlowBridge extension is installed - AIFlowBridge provider is added in Kilo code settings. - AIFlowBridge available models are added in Kilo code settings. **Model picker with Deepseek V4 Model selected - DROPDOWN for reasoning** **Model picker with Minimax M3 Model selected - NO DROPDOWN for reasoning** ### Operating System Windows 11 ### Terminal _No response_
title: `variants()` are hardcoded and hides the reasoning dropdown, breaking MiniMax M3 (which supports reasoning)

body:
### Description

**Labels:** `bug`, `kilocode-api-provider`, `android`

**Plugin type:** Kilo Android App + Kilo Code VSCodium extension (shared session)

**App version:** v1.0.0 (67)

## Description

When a Kilo Code session running on a VPS (VSCodium + Kilo Code extension) is paused waiting for user input, the Android app fails to surface the pending question. Answering from the Android app causes the session to resume but immediately fail with a model ID error.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Start a Plan-mode session in Kilo Code (VSCodium extension on a remote VPS) with the model set to **MiniMax Token Plan (minimax.io) / MiniMax-M3** via the Kilo Gateway.
2. The session reaches a point requiring user input (question/clarification prompt).
3. Open the Kilo Android app — it shows no new messages; the session appears idle.
4. Answer the pending question from the Android app.
5. The session attempts to continue and errors immediately.

## Observed error

```json
{
  "name": "UnknownError",
  "data": {
    "message": "Model not found: kilo/MiniMax-M3. Did you mean: minimax/minimax-m3?"
  }
}
```

## Expected behavior

The session resumes using the correct model ID (`minimax/minimax-m3` or the appropriate Gateway-routed equivalent) without error.

## Root cause hypothesis

The Android client constructs the model ID as `kilo/MiniMax-M3` — using Kilo's internal display name with `kilo/` as a provider prefix — rather than resolving to the upstream model string `minimax/minimax-m3`. The desktop VSCodium extension holds the correct model handle in memory; the Android app apparently reconstructs or re-serializes the model ID differently when submitting. The Gateway correctly rejects the malformed ID and suggests the right one in the error response.

## Secondary bug

The Android app does not show the pending question to the user (step 3). This forces the user to answer from mobile, which then triggers the model ID bug. Both issues compound each other.

## Related issues

- #7419, #7894 — same `kilo/` prefix pattern: `Model not found: kilo/kilo-auto/free`
- #6686 — `kilo/auto` and `kilo/auto-free` missing from Gateway models endpoint

### Plugins

n/a

### Kilo version

7.3.41

### Steps to reproduce

1. created a Kilo shared session on vps using VSCodium with Kilo Code extension.
2. did some work in the session on the vps
3. opened Android app and entered the session
4. typed a message, nothing happened
5. typed another message, nothing happened
6. looked at the vps console session and noticed the test messages queued, followed by the model error

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Ubuntu 24.04

### Terminal

_No response_

body:
### Description

When selecting Claude 4.7 or 4.8 models, every request fails with a 400 ValidationException. Claude 4.6 works without any issue.

Error message:
ValidationException: "thinking.type.enabled" is not supported for this model.
Use "thinking.type.adaptive" and "output_config.effort" to control thinking behavior.

Root cause:
Kilo sends thinking: { type: "enabled", budget_tokens: ... } for all models. This format is only valid for Claude 3.7 Sonnet. Claude 4.7+ on AWS Bedrock requires a different schema:

{
  "thinking": { "type": "adaptive" },
  "output_config": { "effort": "medium" }
}

Expected behavior:
Kilo should detect the model version and send the correct thinking parameter format accordingly.

Fix suggestion:
When the target model is Claude 4.7+, replace thinking.type: "enabled" with thinking.type: "adaptive" and add output_config.effort ("low" / "medium" / "high").

Environment:
