body:
### Description

The `copyToClipboard` message type only exists for the Agent Manager. The sidebar chat has **no clipboard bridge** to the extension host. The `kilo-ui` components use `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` directly.

Here's the root cause:

**The bug is in `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx` lines 1340-1345 (assistant text part copy) and lines 799-804 (user message copy).**

The `handleCopy` functions use `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` **without a try/catch**. In VS Code webviews, `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` can fail because:

1. The webview iframe origin is `https://{{uuid}}.vscode-webview.net/` — while technically a secure context, the Clipboard API requires the document to be focused AND the write to be triggered by a user gesture in the same tick (not after an `await`).

2. More critically: the `handleCopy` is an `async` function that `await`s `navigator.clipboard.writeText(content)`. If this promise rejects, the error is silently swallowed (unhandled rejection), and `setCopied(true)` never executes — so the "Copied" tooltip never appears.

The fix should match the pattern used in `MessageBubble.tsx` (KiloClaw) which wraps the clipboard call in try/catch, or better yet, use a fallback clipboard mechanism (like `document.execCommand('copy')` with a temporary textarea) when `navigator.clipboard` fails. Alternatively, the sidebar chat should implement the same `postMessage` → extension host → `vscode.env.clipboard.writeText()` bridge that the Agent Manager already uses.

**Summary of the problem:**

| Location | Issue |
|---|---|
| `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx:799-804` | User message `handleCopy` — no try/catch on `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` |
| `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx:1340-1345` | Assistant text part `handleCopy` — no try/catch on `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` |
| `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx:2080-2085` | `BashCopyButton` handler — same pattern |
| `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/shell-rolling-results.tsx:137-144` | Shell expanded copy — same pattern |

All these will silently fail without showing the "Copied" tooltip because `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` rejects in VS Code webview contexts and the promise rejection prevents `setCopied(true)` from executing.

### Plugins

VSCode

### Kilo version

7.3.42

### Steps to reproduce

When model provides final response, sometimes it happens that copy button does not copy content to clipboard. In such case there is no `Copied response` tooltip upon click.

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

macOS 26.5.1

### Terminal

_No response_

body:
## Problem / User Pain

When a user creates a new worktree in the Agent Manager, two friction points make the experience feel slow and disruptive:

### 1. Setup script blocks starting a Kilo session

After creating a worktree, the setup script runs synchronously — the user cannot start a session or begin prompting until it finishes. Setup scripts commonly install dependencies, configure secrets, or run other preparatory steps that can take 30–60+ seconds. During that window the user is stuck waiting with nothing they can do in the new worktree, even if the first tasks they want to run don't depend on setup completing.

**Impact:** Users feel the workflow is slow. The delay is especially noticeable when switching between multiple worktrees or when iterating quickly on agent tasks.

### 2. Setup script runs in the main VS Code terminal

The setup script currently executes in the existing (main) VS Code terminal, pushing out or interrupting whatever the user had open there. Users with a carefully arranged terminal layout have to recover their workspace state after every new worktree creation.

**Impact:** The setup interrupts the user's existing workflow and forces them to reorganise their terminal layout manually every time.

---

## Potential Directions

These are options to evaluate and discuss — not prescriptive solutions. The right trade-offs need input from the team:

- **Non-blocking / async setup**: Run the setup script in the background so the user can open a session immediately. The session could show a status indicator or a non-blocking warning (e.g. "Setup still in progress — some tools may not be ready yet") until the script completes.
- **Worktree-scoped terminal tab**: Open the setup script in a dedicated terminal tab named after the worktree (e.g. `agent-manager: <branch> setup`) rather than in the shared main terminal, keeping the user's existing layout intact.
- **Setup progress indicator in the Agent Manager UI**: Show a progress/spinner state on the worktree card while setup is running, so the user has visibility without needing to watch a terminal.
- **Allow session start before setup completes (with a warning)**: Let the user start prompting immediately, but surface a clear indicator that setup is still running. The agent could delay tool calls that need a fully initialised environment, or the user could choose to proceed anyway.
- **Combination approach**: Run setup in a scoped terminal tab (for visibility) while keeping the session UI unblocked, with a status badge on the worktree card.

---

## Notes

- The right solution likely involves balancing visibility (users want to see setup output) with non-blocking UX.
- Any approach should avoid silently swallowing setup errors — failures must still be surfaced clearly.
title: Agent Manager worktree setup blocks session start and disrupts terminal layout

body:
### Description

### Problem
Auto-compaction does not work reliably for local Ollama models with 32K context window. After several automatic compactions, the session crashes with:
Compaction exhausted: context still exceeds model limits after 3 attempts
{
"name": "ContextOverflowError",
"data": {
"message": "Compaction exhausted: context still exceeds model limits after 3 attempts"
}
}


Manual compaction (`/compact`) works fine.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Configure Kilo Code to use a local Ollama model with 32K context (e.g., `qwen3-coder-fixed:latest`).
2. Set up `limit.input: 28000`, `limit.output: 8192`, and compaction settings (`reserved: 4000`, `preserve_recent_tokens: 4000`).
3. Start a new session and work on a project (e.g., a Tetris game with ~700 lines of code).
4. Let the conversation grow near the 32K limit. Auto-compaction triggers automatically.
5. After 2-3 successful compactions, the error appears and the session stops.

### Expected behavior
Auto-compaction should work continuously without crashing, allowing very long sessions (1M+ tokens) as claimed in Kilo Code documentation.

### Actual behavior
Auto-compaction works 1-3 times, then fails with the above error.

### Root cause analysis (my findings)
- Kilo Code's built-in system prompt consumes ~15K tokens.
- Each compaction adds a summary message that accumulates over time.
- Even with `limit.input: 28000`, the combined size of system prompt + compaction prompt + accumulated summaries + conversation eventually exceeds the model's context window.
- Using a 64K model for compaction helps but doesn't fully solve the problem (summaries still accumulate).

### Environment
- OS: Windows 11
- VS Code: version 1.99.0 (or your version)
- Kilo Code extension: version 0.19.x (or your version)
- Ollama: 0.6.2 (or your version)
- Model: `qwen3-coder-fixed:latest` (32K context) / `qwen3-coder:30b-64k` (64K context for compaction)

### Configuration
```json
{
  "provider": {
    "ollama-local": {
      "options": { "baseURL": "http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1/" },
      "models": {
        "qwen3-coder-fixed:latest": {
          "limit": { "context": 32768, "input": 28000, "output": 8192 }
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "compaction": {
    "auto": true,
    "threshold_percent": 90,
    "prune": true,
    "tail_turns": 2,
    "preserve_recent_tokens": 4000,
    "reserved": 4000
  }
}


Related GitHub issues
#9774

#9494

#9887 (partial fix)

Additional context
I have attached a detailed investigation report. The issue is reproducible on Windows 11 with multiple local Ollama models.

### Plugins

none

### Kilo version

kilocode.kilo-code@7.3.41

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

_No response_
title: Auto-compaction fails for local Ollama models (32K context) with "Compaction exhausted" error

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

## Problem

The "Enhance Prompt" feature (the ✨ button in the VS Code extension) is not available in the CLI TUI, even though the entire backend for it already lives in the CLI codebase:

- The enhancement logic is implemented in `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/enhance-prompt.ts` (`enhancePrompt(text)`, with the rewrite system instruction and small-model selection).
- It is exposed as an HTTP endpoint `POST /enhance-prompt` (`packages/opencode/src/kilocode/server/httpapi/groups/enhance-prompt.ts` + `handlers/enhance-prompt.ts`) and consumed by the JS SDK and the VS Code extension.
- However, there is no reference to it anywhere in the TUI (`packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/`): no keybind action in `config/keybind.ts`, no slash command, no UI affordance.

So terminal users currently have no way to use prompt enhancement, except by manually curling the endpoint of a running `kilo serve` instance.

## Proposed solution

Add a `prompt_enhance` action to the TUI that:

1. Takes the current content of the prompt input box,
2. Calls the existing `enhancePrompt()` / `POST /enhance-prompt`,
3. Replaces the input box content in place with the enhanced text (same UX as the ✨ button in the extension: review, edit, then submit),
4. Supports undo to restore the original draft (the extension already does this with Ctrl+Z / ⌘Z in `PromptInput.tsx`).

### Suggested integration

- Register a new keybind in `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/config/keybind.ts`, e.g.:
```ts
  prompt_enhance: keybind("<leader>e", "Enhance the current prompt"),
```
  (Note: `ctrl+p` is already bound to `command_list` and dialog navigation, but since keybinds are user-configurable, anyone could remap it to `ctrl+p` if they prefer.)
- Optionally also expose it as a `/enhance` slash command for discoverability.
- Show a spinner/placeholder in the input while the enhancement request is in flight, and surface errors non-destructively (keep the original draft on failure).

## Why

- Feature parity between the extension and the CLI, which are now built on the same core.
- Very low implementation cost: the model call, endpoint, and even the undo UX pattern already exist; only the TUI wiring is missing.
- Docs currently list "Enhance Prompt" under productivity features without mentioning it is IDE-only, which is confusing for CLI users.

## Alternatives considered

- Custom markdown command (`.kilo/command/enhance.md`): works, but the result comes back as an assistant reply in the session instead of rewriting the input box, so the UX is significantly worse and it pollutes the conversation.
- Curling `POST /enhance-prompt` on `kilo serve`: works, but requires leaving the TUI.
title: [FEATURE]:add prompt enhancer into kilocode CLI

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Finish the cold-snapshot performance work by avoiding unnecessary content and filter checks for unchanged tracked paths while preserving byte-for-byte equivalence with the existing filesystem-built snapshot.

On the Kilocode repository, the original cold snapshot took about 7.25 seconds. Agent Manager index seeding reduces this to about 1.86 seconds. The seed and policy reconciliation take about 0.58 seconds, while the existing `diff-files` and `add()` reconciliation still take roughly 1.28 seconds. A warm snapshot is about 0.14 seconds.

The remaining work should safely reuse Git index stat information or an equivalent validated cache so unchanged files do not run through clean/smudge and line-ending checks on every new seeded snapshot. A direct binary index copy is not generally safe because of split-index references, sparse-index entries, fsmonitor and untracked-cache extensions, source-local filter configuration, and differing snapshot Git configuration.

Required design work:

- Determine when source and snapshot clean behavior is equivalent for `core.autocrlf`, `core.eol`, `.gitattributes`, `working-tree-encoding`, and custom filter drivers.
- Reconcile only paths whose worktree stat changed or whose effective conversion configuration differs.
- Handle split indexes through Git plumbing without retaining `sharedindex.*` lifecycle dependencies.
- Strip or safely refresh fsmonitor and untracked-cache index extensions.
- Preserve symlink, executable, gitlink, staged, deleted, ignored, and oversized-file behavior.
- Keep conservative fallback to the current seeded reconciliation when equivalence cannot be established.
- Avoid an O(repository bytes) validation pass, since hashing every file would erase the optimization.

Acceptance requires final tree equality against the original cold path across line-ending and filter configurations, plus first-turn edit/reset/unreset checks. Benchmark a large repository with LFS attributes and target cold initialization close to warm snapshot latency without weakening fallback correctness.
title: Reduce seeded snapshot reconciliation to warm-snapshot latency

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Cold snapshot initialization should reuse the checkout's local Git index for regular CLI and VS Code sessions, not only newly created Agent Manager worktrees.

The first implementation deliberately gates seeding on Agent Manager's `snapshotInitialization: \"wait\"` policy. It imports the source index tree into a new snapshot repository, reconciles the actual filesystem through the existing snapshot `add()` path, and falls back to the original empty-index scan when it cannot prove eligibility. This avoids changing normal CLI and sidebar behavior before their broader directory and repository states are covered.

General rollout must preserve the exact tree produced by current cold initialization and keep all reset and unreset behavior unchanged. It must:

- Select seeding independently from the Agent Manager slow-snapshot UI policy.
- Apply only when the per-directory snapshot repository is new.
- Reconcile staged, unstaged, deleted, untracked, ignored, oversized, symlink, executable, submodule, line-ending, encoding, and filter-driven working-tree state.
- Support a client directory below the Git worktree root without including files outside the current snapshot scope.
- Define and test sparse-checkout semantics. The initial implementation intentionally falls back for sparse checkouts because the full Git index and materialized filesystem contain different path sets.
- Initialize snapshot repositories with the source object format or fall back for SHA-256 repositories.
- Retain the existing full scan for unborn Git repositories, unmerged indexes, unsupported layouts, and any failed validation.
- Keep non-Git directories and `snapshot: false` behavior unchanged.
- Never fetch or require a remote.

Acceptance requires seeded and existing cold initialization to produce equivalent trees across clean and dirty repositories, followed by identical immediate-edit, reset, and unreset results. Validate new CLI processes, VS Code sidebar sessions, editor tabs, moved checkouts, and repeated sessions in an already-warm checkout.
title: Seed cold snapshots for regular CLI and extension sessions

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

## Problem

Session and turn diffs are stored under a session, but their contents are currently derived from whole-workspace snapshot intervals. A snapshot identifies the state of a working directory at a point in time. It does not identify which session or tool produced each change between two snapshots.

This is normally invisible when one session writes to a directory at a time. It becomes incorrect when multiple sessions or subagents overlap in the same directory.

For example:

1. Session A and session B both capture the same clean directory state.
2. Session A edits `a.ts`.
3. Session B edits `b.ts`.
4. Session A captures its end state after both writes have completed.
5. Session A's diff can contain both `a.ts` and `b.ts`, even though B produced `b.ts`.

The same contamination can appear in the per-turn change summary and in the patch records used for rewind. Resetting A can therefore revert B's work. If both sessions edit the same file, an older session can also overwrite a newer edit because restore does not currently verify that the file still matches the state produced by the operation being undone.

A separate shadow Git index or repository per session does not solve this while all sessions still share one live working tree. Each index would continue to observe the same bytes on disk.

## User impact

- The files shown in a session's change summary can include changes produced by another session.
- Rewinding one session can remove or overwrite changes from another session.
- A user edit made after an agent edit can be overwritten during rewind.
- Child-agent changes are visible in the same directory without durable ownership semantics.
- The behavior depends on timing, so it can appear correct in ordinary runs and fail only under overlap.

Agent Manager worktrees remain correctly isolated because each session uses a different working directory. This issue concerns sessions and subagents that intentionally share one directory.

## Requested behavior

Track Kilo-mediated workspace mutations as session-owned operations and use those records for both displayed changes and rewind.

Each operation should identify at least:

- workspace and monotonic workspace operation order
- session, message, tool-call, and parent task/subagent relationship
- pre-operation and post-operation snapshot IDs
- changed paths and their pre/post identities
- whether capture excluded ignored, oversized, external, or otherwise unsupported paths
- whether the tool may have external side effects that workspace rewind cannot undo

A rewind should select operations belonging to the chosen session range and compensate them in reverse order. Before restoring a path, it must verify that the current path still matches the operation's recorded post-state. If it does not match, rewind should report a conflict and leave the path unchanged by default.

## Minimum-conflict implementation direction

Keep the existing Git snapshot storage and minimize changes to shared OpenCode files:

1. Put the coordinator, operation model, attribution logic, and guarded restore implementation in Kilo-owned modules under `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/`.
2. Add only narrow marked hooks around built-in and MCP tool execution so Kilo can run mutating tools through the coordinator.
3. Classify tools conservatively:
   - read-only tools remain parallel
   - known mutating tools participate in workspace mutation tracking
   - bash, unknown MCP tools, and plugins are treated as opaque mutators initially
4. For the first implementation, serialize mutating operations per physical directory. Hold the lease from pre-capture through tool completion, post-capture, and durable operation recording.
5. Do not hold a workspace lease around an entire `task` invocation. Child tools should acquire the same directory coordinator individually and record their child-session and parent-call identity.
6. Store operation references in existing tool metadata if that avoids a message schema or SDK change. Add protocol fields only if the metadata route cannot provide durable recovery.
7. Derive per-turn and per-session change summaries from owned operation records instead of unfiltered whole-directory intervals.
8. Keep the existing worktree flow as the stronger isolation mode for sessions intentionally developing alternate versions.

This can start with exclusive mutator serialization. Declared disjoint path transactions and reverse three-way merges can be added later without changing the ownership model.

## Rewind semantics

For each affected path, let `P` be the recorded pre-state, `Q` the recorded post-state, and `C` the current state:

- If `C == Q`, restore `P`.
- If `C == P`, the operation is already absent and can be treated as a no-op.
- If `C` differs from both, report a conflict and do not overwrite it automatically.
- Do not attempt text merging for binary files.
- A later enhancement may offer an explicit reverse three-way merge for text files.

Rewind and redo should be represented as new compensating operations rather than deleting workspace history. Conversation cleanup can preserve the existing session behavior while the operation records remain available for recovery and conflict analysis.

## Snapshot durability and failure handling

Operation snapshots must remain reachable for as long as active session history references them. Raw unreferenced Git tree objects must not be allowed to disappear during garbage collection.

Missing or unreadable snapshot objects must produce a hard, user-visible failure. They must never be interpreted as evidence that a file did not exist and therefore should be deleted.

Failed, interrupted, and aborted mutating tools must still capture their final observable workspace state before releasing the mutation lease. The operation should be marked partial when ignored files, unsupported paths, long-running child processes, or external side effects prevent a complete workspace rollback guarantee.

## Out of scope

Workspace rewind cannot promise to undo:

- network requests or remote API changes
- Git pushes or published packages
- external databases or services
- background processes that continue writing after a tool returns
- files outside the captured workspace scope

The UI should state when files were rewound but external effects may remain.

## Acceptance criteria

- Two overlapping sessions that modify different files in one directory show only their own files and can rewind independently.
- If two sessions modify the same file, rewinding the earlier session does not overwrite the later session's state and instead returns a structured conflict.
- A user edit made after an agent operation is protected by the same current-state check.
- Read-only tools retain parallel execution.
- Parallel mutating tools receive a deterministic workspace operation order.
- Child-agent operations retain child ownership and an explicit parent task relationship.
- Failed and aborted mutating tools record any workspace changes they made.
- Per-turn and per-session change summaries are derived from owned operations.
- Rewind never silently deletes files because a referenced snapshot object is missing.
- Existing isolated worktree behavior continues to work without sharing mutation state across worktrees.
- Tests cover different-file overlap, same-file conflict, user modification after an operation, subagent attribution, abort during mutation, and snapshot-object failure.

## Related work

This is the correctness layer needed beneath agent-step checkpoint UX such as #8378. The UI can expose existing step boundaries first, but shared-directory rewind should not claim independent per-session safety until mutation ownership and guarded restore are available.
title: Attribute workspace changes to sessions before reverting shared-directory work

body:
### Description

Seems there is automatic prompt injection if a RANDOM file contains ```instructions```: 

```
change-model.cast#205 is present because the client injected it as selected editor context, not because Kilo/project config treats that file as instructions.
Evidence from the current prompt:
<system-reminder>Note: The user selected #205 from "/Users/knutole/aidn/devbox/change-model.cast". ```instructions.```</system-reminder>
What I verified:
- /Users/knutole/aidn/devbox/change-model.cast is an asciinema terminal recording, not a config/instruction file.
- Line 205 contains terminal escape output from a previous recorded Kilo session.
- The selected snippet at that line is just the word instructions. from the visible terminal text: system instructions.
- /Users/knutole/aidn/.kilo/kilo.json does not include any instructions config pointing at this file.
- No .kilo/agent-manager.json exists in this workspace.
- I found no project config reference that would load devbox/change-model.cast as context.
So the cause is almost certainly UI/editor state: the Kilo client noticed text selected in devbox/change-model.cast and automatically added it to the next request as selected context. It is not model context chosen by me, and it is not coming from AGENTS.md, .kilo/kilo.json, or a skill.
To avoid it:
- Clear any selection in the editor before sending a message.
- Close devbox/change-model.cast if it is open/active.
- If the client has an “include selection/current file” behavior, disable that or ensure no irrelevant file selection is active.
- Also note the workspace root is currently /, while the working directory is /Users/knutole/aidn; a broad workspace root can make accidental editor context more likely, but the direct trigger here was the selected text marker.
```

That means anyone could prompt inject just by setting `instructions` in any file. 

### Plugins

_No response_

7.3.41

This is line #205 in the injected file:

body: ### Description When using my own Kimi Code API Key, the extension is extremely slow or frequently hangs while processing tasks. The issue occurs most often in **[Ask / Code]** mode, especially on tasks like **[e.g., project refactoring / document writing]**. ### Steps To Reproduce 1. Launch `kilo Code` and configure my `Kimi Code` API Key. 2. Select the model `Kimi for Coding`. 3. Open a project of moderate size. 4. ### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Request:

Please add a setting to hide the Indexing button/icon from the Kilo Code chat input toolbar.

Currently, the Indexing icon is always visible near the chat input area. Even if I do not use codebase indexing, the icon still takes space in the toolbar and cannot be hidden from the UI.

Suggested setting:

{
  "kilo-code.new.indexing.showToolbarButton": false
}
Expected behavior:

When this setting is true or unset, the Indexing button behaves as it does today.
When this setting is false, the Indexing button is hidden from the chat input toolbar.
This should only affect the visibility of the toolbar button, not necessarily disable the indexing feature itself.
Reason:

Some users prefer a cleaner chat input UI and do not want unused feature buttons to appear permanently. This would make the toolbar more customizable and reduce visual clutter.
title: [FEATURE]:Add an option to hide the Indexing button in the chat input toolbar

    at processTicksAndRejections (native:7:39); provider.list: SyntaxError: JSON Parse error: Unexpected identifier "undefined"
    at <parse> (:0)
    at parse (unknown)
    at <anonymous> (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-qq83vywn.js:611:69998)
    at ~effect/Utils/internal (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:8:1446)
    at <anonymous> (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:25:5042)
    at ~effect/Effect/evaluate (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:25:5550)
    at runLoop (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:25:2045)
    at evaluate (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:25:1435)
    at <anonymous> (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-10vrn70r.js:25:5589)
    at <anonymous> (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-n6v53qp5.js:4:5234)
    at <anonymous> (node:fs:225:13)
    at processTicksAndRejections (native:7:39)
    at NC (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-c6bw8p5c.js:459:1149)
    at <anonymous> (B:/~BUN/root/chunk-c6bw8p5c.js:459:9059)
    at processTicksAndRejections (native:7:39)

"enabled_providers": [
    "deepinfra"
  ],

The above line causes the issue.

A bit of tracing on this one, in case it helps narrow the fix.

The signature `JSON Parse error: Unexpected identifier "undefined"` is a parser hitting a bare `undefined` token, i.e. something serialized a JS `undefined` into a string and that string later went through a JSON/Schema decode. It is not on-disk corruption: `~/.config/kilo/kilo.json` is still valid JSON. Deleting it "fixes" the error only because it stops a particular provider from being selected, which is why @roychung96 removing `provider.amazon-bedrock.name` and @marcfarrow's `enabled_providers: ["deepinfra"]` both clear it. Both repros share a shape: a provider that is *referenced* in config but whose resolved catalog/model data comes back `undefined`.

Walking the stack trace (`Provider.list` -> `~effect/Utils/internal` -> `parse`) against the source:

- `ProviderHttpApi.list` builds the response in `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/httpapi/handlers/provider.ts:52` (`provider.list()`) and only maps results through `Provider.toPublicInfo` afterward at line 72.
- `toPublicInfo` (`packages/opencode/src/provider/provider.ts:983-991`) is a `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(provider, replacer))` round-trip, but its replacer already returns `undefined` for `value === undefined`, so `stringify` omits those keys. That round-trip cannot emit a bare `undefined`, so it is not the culprit.

That leaves the throw inside `provider.list()` itself, before `toPublicInfo` runs — the Effect Schema decode of the models catalog reached via `ModelsDev.Service` (`provider.ts:1203`, `mapValues(modelsDev, fromModelsDevProvider)` at 1212). The bare `undefined` is being injected into that catalog string for the affected provider, then the `~effect/Utils/internal` decode chokes on it.

Concrete next step to pin it: wrap the decode that fails in `provider.list()` and log the raw input string plus the provider id before it is parsed (or have an affected user run with that instrumentation). That will show exactly which provider's catalog entry carries the `undefined` and where it was written — which distinguishes "fetched from models.dev malformed" from "stale local model cache written during a failed fetch" (the latter would also explain why it persists across version rollbacks).

Happy to open a PR with a guard once a maintainer confirms which of those two it is, since the right fix differs (validate-on-write vs. tolerate-on-read).

title: JSON Parse error loading ~/.config/kilo/kilo.json on both VS Code Extension and CLI

body:
## Description

Global install of `@kilocode/cli` fails on linux-x64 because the platform-specific binary packages are not published for recent versions.

```
npm install -g @kilocode/cli@7.3.39
```

**Error:**
```
Failed to setup kilo binary: Could not find any binary package. Tried: "@kilocode/cli-linux-x64", "@kilocode/cli-linux-x64-baseline", "@kilocode/cli-linux-x64-musl", "@kilocode/cli-linux-x64-baseline-musl"
```

## Root Cause

The main package `@kilocode/cli` is at **7.3.40**, but the linux-x64 binary package is stuck at **7.3.18**:

| Package | Latest | RC |
|---|---|---|
| `@kilocode/cli` | 7.3.40 | — |
| `@kilocode/cli-linux-x64` | **7.3.16** | **7.3.18** |

The `postinstall.mjs` script tries to download the matching platform binary, but none exist for versions > 7.3.18. This affects all 4 linux-x64 variants (`-baseline`, `-musl`, `-baseline-musl`).

## Environment

- OS: Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2 (x86_64)
- Node: v25.2.1
- npm: v11.9.0

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
npm install -g @kilocode/cli@7.3.39
# or any version > 7.3.18
```

## Expected Behavior

Platform binary packages are published in sync with the main package, or the main package's `postinstall` gracefully handles missing binaries (e.g. falls back to building from source).

## Current Workaround

Pin to the last working version:
```bash
npm install -g @kilocode/cli@7.3.18
```
title: CLI npm install fails on linux-x64: platform binary packages not published beyond 7.3.18

body:
Hi Kilo team,

I would like to suggest adding a short Starveri API setup example to the OpenAI Compatible provider docs, or otherwise documenting it as a custom OpenAI-compatible gateway users can configure manually.

Starveri API is an independent OpenAI-compatible GPT/Codex API gateway with public docs, model metadata, prepaid credits, and a browser demo. It is not affiliated with OpenAI.

Public source links:
- Docs: https://api.starveri.net/docs
- Buyer / credits page: https://api.starveri.net/credits
- Public model metadata: https://api.starveri.net/models
- OpenAPI: https://api.starveri.net/openapi.json
- LLM discovery: https://api.starveri.net/llms.txt

Suggested Kilo configuration shape:

```text
API Provider: OpenAI Compatible
Base URL: https://api.starveri.net/v1
API Key: a Starveri API key from the credits dashboard
Model ID: one of the public model IDs from https://api.starveri.net/models
```

I am opening this as a docs/config request first because I do not want to assume the project wants Starveri as a built-in provider. If this is in scope, I can prepare a small docs PR using only the public URLs above.
title: Docs request: Starveri API via OpenAI Compatible provider

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Could kilocode be updated to integrate with the VSCode vscode.lm.tools API so that it could use MCP servers and tools from other extensions?  I currently utilize this functionality with github copilot (which I'm looking to move to kilo from) to interact with oracle databases through the oracle sql developer vscode extension.  It seems like there could be many other extensions this functionality would allow integration with as well.


explanation from kilocode chat to the difference between current kilo and copilot implementation:

Copilot uses VS Code's vscode.lm.tools API — a built-in mechanism where any extension can register tools, and the chat provider (Copilot) discovers them automatically. The Oracle extension registers its tools via vscode.lm.registerTool(), and Copilot picks them up.

Kilo uses its own MCP infrastructure — it connects to standalone MCP servers via stdio/HTTP, configured in kilo.json. It doesn't currently bridge to the vscode.lm.tools API that other extensions register against.

Both are VS Code extensions, but they use different tool discovery paths:

Oracle Extension ──registerTool()──> vscode.lm.tools <──discovers── Copilot Chat
Oracle Extension ──registerTool()──> vscode.lm.tools <──(no bridge)── Kilo
                                                                       │
                                                              kilo.json MCP config
This is a feature gap in Kilo, not a technical limitation. Kilo could be enhanced to discover and invoke tools registered by other extensions via the vscode.lm API. But it doesn't currently do that.
title: [FEATURE]: integration with vscode.lm.tools API

body:
### Description


Description

When using KiloCode for autonomous or long-running workflows, the agent frequently terminates its execution loop even when explicitly instructed to continue working until a goal is achieved.

Additionally, there appears to be no mechanism to intercept or react to agent completion/termination events, making it difficult to build resilient workflows that can automatically resume or take follow-up actions.

Problem
The agent stops its execution loop despite instructions such as:
"Continue until the task is complete."
"Do not stop unless blocked."
"Keep iterating forever."
Adding persistence instructions to the system prompt does not reliably prevent termination.
The agent may declare a task complete when additional work remains.
There is no way to configure custom continuation behavior when the agent stops.
There are no exposed lifecycle hooks or callbacks for:
Agent completion
Agent termination
Agent idle state
Maximum iteration reached
Context exhaustion
Error recovery
Expected Behavior

The platform should support one or more of the following:

Persistent Agent Mode
A configuration option that allows the agent to continue indefinitely.
User-configurable stopping conditions.
Ability to disable automatic "task complete" termination.
Lifecycle Hooks

Expose events such as:

onAgentStart
onAgentStep
onAgentComplete
onAgentTerminate
onAgentError
onMaxIterationsReached
onContextLimitReached

This would allow extensions, scripts, or integrations to react to agent state changes.

Continuation Policies

Allow users to define behaviors when the agent attempts to stop:

Continue automatically.
Request confirmation before stopping.
Run a custom command.
Invoke a webhook.
Trigger another workflow.
Spawn a new iteration.
Actual Behavior
The agent eventually exits its execution loop.
System prompt instructions requesting continuous execution are ignored or overridden by internal stopping logic.
Users cannot intercept termination events.
Users cannot automatically restart or continue the workflow when the agent stops.
Impact

This limitation makes KiloCode difficult to use for:

Autonomous coding agents
Long-running refactoring tasks
Large repository analysis
Background maintenance workflows
Self-healing automation pipelines
Multi-hour or multi-day agent processes

Users must manually monitor the agent and repeatedly restart execution, defeating much of the value of autonomous workflows.

Suggested Solution
Add a configurable "persistent agent" mode.
Expose agent lifecycle events/hooks.
Allow custom continuation policies.
Surface the exact reason an agent stopped.
Provide extension APIs or webhooks for completion and termination events.
Allow users to override default stopping behavior when appropriate.
Additional Context

In practice, adding instructions to the system prompt to continue indefinitely is not sufficient. The agent still terminates due to internal completion heuristics or loop limits, and there is currently no way for users to detect, intercept, or automatically recover from these termination events. This significantly limits the usefulness of KiloCode for fully autonomous workflows.

body:
### Description


Description

When using the VS Code extension for long-running workflows, the conversation context continuously grows with every step. Even with a very large context window (e.g. 256k tokens), the accumulated conversation eventually becomes so large that performance degrades dramatically.

Problem
Each interaction appends more content to the existing context.
Context size grows throughout the session without effective pruning.
As the context becomes larger, response generation becomes progressively slower.
Tasks that initially complete in seconds can take hours near the end of a long session.
Large context windows only delay the problem rather than solving it.
Expected Behavior

The extension should provide mechanisms to manage context growth, such as:

Automatic context pruning of older, less relevant messages.
Intelligent summarization of previous conversation history.
Ability to manually delete portions of earlier conversation context.
Ability to remove or replace the initial prompt/context after it is no longer needed.
Visibility into current context usage and what is being sent to the model.
Configurable retention policies (e.g. keep last N messages, summarize older content).
Actual Behavior
Context appears to accumulate indefinitely during a session.
No obvious way to selectively remove earlier context.
No option to replace old context with a summary.
Performance degrades substantially as conversation length increases.
Impact

For extended coding sessions:

Response latency increases dramatically over time.
Agent effectiveness decreases due to excessive context size.
Long-running tasks become impractical.
Users are forced to start new chats and manually reconstruct state.
Suggested Solutions
Manual Context Management
Delete selected messages from conversation history.
Remove the initial prompt/system context when no longer needed.
Clear portions of context without starting a new session.
Automatic Summarization
Periodically summarize older sections of the conversation.
Replace detailed history with compact summaries while preserving key decisions and state.
Context Pruning
Drop older messages that are unlikely to affect future responses.
Prioritize recent messages and explicitly pinned information.
Context Inspector
Show estimated token usage.
Display which messages are currently included in the prompt.
Allow users to pin/unpin context.
Additional Context

This issue is particularly noticeable in the VS Code extension during long, iterative coding workflows where the agent repeatedly appends analysis, code changes, and tool outputs. Even with large context windows, performance eventually degrades significantly because the total conversation history continues to expand without sufficient pruning or summarization.

### Plugins

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Add **OrcaRouter** (https://www.orcarouter.ai) as a selectable provider.

OrcaRouter is an OpenAI-compatible meta-router: one API key routes across 80+ upstream
models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, DeepSeek, xAI, Qwen, etc.) and it exposes an adaptive
`orcarouter/auto` router that picks an upstream per request. It's the same category of
provider as the ones Kilo already supports (OpenRouter, Vercel AI Gateway, ZenMux, NVIDIA).

**Why it's low-effort to add:** OrcaRouter already ships in the models.dev catalog that Kilo
consumes (provider id `orcarouter`, `api: https://api.orcarouter.ai/v1`, env `ORCAROUTER_API_KEY`,
`npm: @ai-sdk/openai-compatible`). So no model catalog needs to be embedded in the repo — the
only client-side wiring required is registering a provider loader that injects the Kilo
attribution headers, exactly like the existing OpenRouter/ZenMux/NVIDIA loaders.

**Proposed behavior:**
- Provider activates when the user sets `ORCAROUTER_API_KEY` (or via auth).
- Requests carry the standard Kilo attribution headers (`HTTP-Referer` / `X-Title`), with
  user-configured headers taking precedence.

Disclosure: I'm an engineer on the OrcaRouter team. I have a PR ready for this.

### Have you searched for existing issues to avoid duplicates?

- [x] Yes, I have searched the existing issues and this is not a duplicate.
title: [FEATURE]: Add OrcaRouter as a provider

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I searched existing issues for direct overlaps around `indexing symbol search`, `indexing hybrid retrieval`, `indexing worktree`, and `indexing cache cleanup`.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

This is a proposal only — not a request to start a code PR yet.

Now that #6966 has landed codebase indexing for the CLI/new extension, it would be valuable to extend indexing from "semantic snippet search" into a fuller retrieval stack for agents:

1. indexing-backed symbol engine and tools
2. structure tools for file/repo outlines
3. hybrid semantic + lexical retrieval
4. worktree-aware cache reuse
5. safe cache status / cleanup operations

Prior art: [`Sharper-Flow/lgrep`](https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep) implements these pieces as a standalone MCP code-intelligence server for OpenCode.

Both KiloCode and lgrep are MIT-licensed according to GitHub repo metadata, so Kilo maintainers could copy/adapt implementation ideas or code with normal MIT attribution/license handling if desired.

### Why this matters

Current Kilo indexing is strong for natural-language semantic discovery, but agents still need additional retrieval primitives:

- exact symbol lookup when the agent already knows the name
- file/repo structural navigation before reading whole files
- hybrid ranking for queries that mix concepts with exact identifiers
- search in worktree/agent-manager workflows without duplicating embeddings
- cache observability and cleanup for long-running local installs

Kilo already has LSP `documentSymbol` / `workspaceSymbol`, but those are language-server dependent and live-query based. The proposed symbol/structure layer would be indexing-backed, provider-independent, and available as part of the codebase index/tool surface.

Related existing issues this could help or complement:

- #6391 — long-running code indexing
- #6453 — codebase indexing not being used/fed to model
- #8311 — uncontrolled parallel embedding requests / retry storms
- #10271 — `semantic_search` barely used while indexing enabled
- #10841 — indexing status indicator improvements
- #10963 — indexing cost visibility

### Proposed capabilities

#### 1. Indexing-backed symbol engine

During indexing, persist a lightweight symbol index derived from existing Tree-sitter parsing/captures:

- symbol id
- name
- kind (`function`, `class`, `method`, `interface`, etc.)
- file path
- line range
- parent/container when available
- optional docstring/comment/preview

Suggested tools:

- `code_symbol_search`
  - search by symbol name
  - optional `kind`
  - optional `path`
- `code_symbol_get`
  - retrieve exact source range for one symbol id
- `code_symbols_in_file`
  - return one file's outline

Value:

- no embedding call required
- precise when names are known
- avoids reading full files
- complements LSP instead of replacing it
- lets agent flow be: `semantic_search` → `code_symbol_search` / `code_symbol_get`

Relevant lgrep prior art:

- Symbol tools list: https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep#symbol-tools
- Parser/symbol code:
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/tree/main/src/lgrep/parser
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/index_folder.py
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/search_symbols.py
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/get_symbol.py

#### 2. Structure tools

Add low-token structure retrieval tools backed by the index:

- `code_file_tree`
- `code_file_outline`
- `code_repo_outline`

Value:

- agents can inspect architecture before reading source
- reduces blind `Glob` / `Grep` / full-file reads
- works even when semantic provider is unavailable if the symbol index exists

Relevant lgrep prior art:

- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/get_file_tree.py
- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/get_file_outline.py
- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/get_repo_outline.py

#### 3. Hybrid retrieval for `semantic_search`

Improve `semantic_search` ranking by combining vector similarity with lexical/full-text signal:

- keep vector similarity as the semantic layer
- add indexed text/keyword scoring over chunks
- combine rankings with RRF or similar deterministic reranking
- return retrieval metadata such as `retrievalMode: "hybrid" | "vector"` and maybe per-result `matchType`

Suggested rollout:

- implement first for LanceDB, where local vector + FTS style indexing can live together
- leave Qdrant behavior unchanged initially unless/until backend-specific hybrid design is added
- if hybrid unavailable for a backend, fall back to vector search with explicit metadata

Value:

- exact identifiers and domain terms do not lose to merely similar snippets
- better for queries like `Session.resume state machine` or `auth middleware JwtVerifier`
- avoids forcing agents to choose between semantic search and grep

Relevant lgrep prior art:

- Hybrid search uses vector + full-text search + RRF reranking:
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/storage/_chunk_store.py

#### 4. Worktree-aware cache reuse

Kilo currently disables indexing for `.kilo/worktrees` / `.kilocode/worktrees` sessions. Instead, allow worktree sessions to reuse the canonical repo index safely.

Proposal:

- derive cache identity from the git common dir / canonical repo identity
- track worktree alias paths in cache metadata
- avoid embedding duplicate chunks for every worktree path
- do not delete a shared canonical cache just because one worktree disappears
- optionally keep indexing writes disabled inside worktrees but allow read-only search against the canonical cache

Value:

- agent-manager/worktree workflows keep indexed search where agents need it most
- avoids duplicate vector stores and provider cost
- improves multi-agent workflows

Relevant lgrep prior art:

- Worktree dedup docs: https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep#git-worktree-workflow
- Cache key / indexing code:
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/indexing.py
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/server/lifecycle.py
  - https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/invalidate_worktree.py

#### 5. Cache status and cleanup operations

Expose safe cache operations in CLI/API/UI:

Status fields:

- cache path
- backend (`qdrant`, `lancedb`)
- indexed files/chunks/symbols
- last indexed timestamp
- disk size where available
- active indexing/search jobs
- stale worktree aliases / orphan caches

Operations:

- rebuild current workspace index
- clear current workspace index
- prune orphan caches
- invalidate one worktree alias
- dry-run cleanup

Safety requirements:

- deletion confined to Kilo indexing storage
- dry-run available before destructive cleanup
- no deletion of active/shared canonical cache because one alias disappeared
- status path should be read-only and cheap

Relevant lgrep prior art:

- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/prune_orphans.py
- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/tools/invalidate_worktree.py
- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/server/tools_diagnostics.py
- https://github.com/Sharper-Flow/lgrep/blob/main/src/lgrep/server/tools_maintenance.py

### Suggested implementation phases

#### Phase 1 — symbol + structure layer

- extend existing Tree-sitter indexing path to emit symbol records
- store records in a lightweight local store keyed by workspace/cache identity
- add `code_symbol_search`, `code_symbol_get`, `code_file_outline`, `code_repo_outline`, `code_file_tree`
- no embedding provider dependency for these tools

#### Phase 2 — hybrid retrieval

- add lexical/full-text index for chunks where backend supports it
- implement LanceDB hybrid retrieval first
- add explicit fallback metadata for unsupported backends
- evaluate relevance against existing semantic-only behavior

#### Phase 3 — worktree/cache operations

- add canonical git repo cache identity
- track alias paths for worktrees
- expose cache status and dry-run cleanup
- add guarded prune/invalidate operations

### Acceptance criteria

- Existing `semantic_search` behavior remains compatible for current users.
- Symbol search can find a known function/class/method by name without any embedding provider call.
- Symbol get returns only the requested symbol's source range, not the whole file.
- File outline returns symbols and line ranges for one file.
- Repo outline returns a bounded structural summary across indexed files.
- Hybrid retrieval improves exact-identifier + concept queries without regressing pure semantic queries.
- Worktree sessions can reuse canonical repo cache or explicitly report why reuse is unavailable.
- Opening two worktrees of the same repo does not duplicate embeddings/cache data.
- Cache cleanup has a dry-run mode and cannot delete outside Kilo indexing storage.

### Non-goals

- Replacing LSP language intelligence.
- Requiring embeddings for symbol/structure tools.
- Requiring Qdrant hybrid retrieval in the first iteration.
- Importing lgrep as a runtime dependency.
- Blocking current indexing bugfixes behind this proposal.

### Extra context

The proposed direction is not "make Kilo use lgrep". Kilo already has the better product integration surface: config UI, status UI, provider choices, vector-store choices, and agent/tool registry integration.

The ask is to copy/adapt the highest-value retrieval primitives from lgrep into Kilo's native indexing architecture.
title: [FEATURE]: Extend codebase indexing with symbols, structure tools, hybrid retrieval, and cache ops

body:
﻿## Summary

On Windows, when Kilo is launched from a `subst` drive, the "read before
overwrite" guard fires incorrectly with:

> You must read file <path> before overwriting it. Use the Read tool first

even though the file *was* read in the same session. The Read and the
Edit/Write target the same file via two different path spellings, so the
guard''s lookup misses.

## Environment

- Platform: Windows (win32)
- A `subst` drive maps a virtual root to a real path, e.g.:
  ```
  subst S: F:\Projects
  # S:\  =>  F:\Projects
  ```

## Reproduction

1. `subst S: F:\Projects` so `S:\MyProject` == `F:\Projects\MyProject` (same files).
2. Launch Kilo from `S:\MyProject`.
3. Kilo reports the working directory as the **resolved** target
   `F:\Projects\MyProject` (the subst is collapsed to its real path).
4. The model, following project rules/paths, reads a file as
   `S:\MyProject\...\Foo.cs` and then edits it as `F:\Projects\MyProject\...\Foo.cs`
   (or vice versa).
5. The Edit/Write fails: "You must read file ... before overwriting it."

## Root cause

The guard that tracks which files have been read keys its set/map by the
**raw path string** passed to the tool. Because the working directory is
canonicalized (subst `S:` -> real `F:\Projects`) but the model-supplied
Read/Edit paths are not consistently canonicalized, the two calls produce
different keys for the same underlying file, so the "was this read?" lookup
misses.

`path.resolve()` / `path.normalize()` do **not** fix this — they only clean
the string and leave `S:` as `S:`. Following the subst/symlink to the real
target requires `fs.realpath`.

## Proposed fix

Normalize every path to its canonical real filesystem path with
`fs.realpathSync.native()` (follows subst drives, symlinks, junctions),
using the **same** helper in both the Read tracker and the Edit/Write guard,
before recording or checking a file.

```ts
import { realpathSync } from "node:fs";
import path from "node:path";

export function canonicalKey(p: string): string {
  const abs = path.resolve(p);
  let real: string;
  try {
    real = realpathSync.native(abs); // follows subst (S:->F:), symlinks, junctions
  } catch {
    real = abs;                      // file may not exist yet (new file via Write)
  }
  return process.platform === "win32"
    ? real.toLowerCase().replace(/\\/g, "/") // normalize case + separators
    : real;
}
```

- Read tool: `readFiles.add(canonicalKey(filePath))`
- Edit/Write guard: `if (!readFiles.has(canonicalKey(filePath))) throw ...`

Both sides then collapse `S:\MyProject\Foo.cs` and `F:\Projects\MyProject\Foo.cs` to an
identical key and the guard stops false-firing.

## Notes

The same canonicalization should be applied anywhere paths are compared for
identity (not just the read/edit guard), to avoid similar mismatches across
subst drives and symlinked roots on Windows.
title: Read-before-edit guard false-positives on Windows subst drives (S: -> F:)

body:
### Description

## Environment
- OS: Windows 11
- Kilo default shell: PowerShell 7 (C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7\pwsh.exe)
- Git for Windows 2.54.0

## Bug Description
Kilo spawns pwsh.exe via Node.js child_process.spawn() WITHOUT windowsHide: true.
On Windows, when a GUI parent process (VS Code extension host) spawns a console-mode child, Windows creates a new conhost.exe console window (black box popup) for every command.

## Root Cause
Process chain:
kilo (VS Code extension, GUI, no console) └─ spawn("pwsh.exe", args) ← missing windowsHide:true └─ Windows creates conhost.exe → visible black box popup


Every pwsh.exe spawn creates a matching conhost.exe window. This applies to ALL local MCP server spawns too (npx, uvx, etc.).

## To Reproduce
1. Use Kilo on Windows
2. Execute any bash command (git status, npm, dotnet, openspec, etc.)
3. Observe black console window popup each time

## Frequency
Every bash tool call = 1 popup. Dozens per minute during normal use.

## Expected Fix
Add `{ windowsHide: true }` to child_process.spawn() for all child processes on Windows.

## Evidence
Process monitor log from a 5-minute session:
[15:16:53] pwsh 父进程=kilo(18288) [15:16:52] conhost 父进程=pwsh(9448) ← black box

[15:16:59] pwsh 父进程=kilo(18288) [15:16:58] conhost 父进程=pwsh(27524) ← black box

[15:17:01] pwsh 父进程=kilo(18288) [15:17:01] conhost 父进程=pwsh(3748) ← black box


## Impact
Severe UX degradation on Windows. Makes Kilo nearly unusable for Windows users.

### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

_No response_
title: Windows: spawn pwsh.exe without windowsHide:true causes conhost.exe popup on every bash command

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

The real-time elapsed timer disappears immediately when the agent finishes a task. It would be useful to show the final duration as a static label so users can see how long the task took without having to manually track it.

Additionally, show the time taken for each individual tool call (e.g., read, edit, grep, bash) within a task. The CLI already displays per-tool-call timing — the extension should implement the same.

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I have used local codebase indexing (implemented by @shssoichiro ) with LanceDB in my projects at my work. While I believe that it saves plenty of time and costs by avoiding complete traversal of the codebase using merely grep/glob searches, I think it would be good to know how much the codebase indexing has in fact costed to begin with.

I am referring specifically more to the usage of embedding models. For instance, I am using my company's OpenAI embedding models when indexing my codebases and it does follow with a certain cost, especially when you are indexing larger codebases. That's why it would be beneficial for me if I know how many tokens that have been feeded to the embedding models, and thus costs. This way, I can more easily measure and see if local codebase indexing is worth having enabled for a project or not.

Whether this is doable or not is perhaps not up to me, but I sense it should be possible if we can provide similar information for usage of LLMs in the sessions.
title: [FEATURE]: Include information about the cost of indexing a codebase

body:
## Overview

Windows CI tests (`unit (windows)` job in `test.yml`) have been consistently flaky over the past 2 weeks — failing on first run, then passing on re-run. All failures are **timeout-related**, suggesting the Windows runner is slower than hardcoded test timeouts allow.

## Flaky Test Names

### `test/session/prompt.test.ts` (packages/opencode)
Timing out at the 3000ms test timeout:
- `assertNotBusy throws BusyError when loop running`
- `cancel with queued callers resolves all cleanly`
- `records aborted errors when prompt is cancelled mid-stream`

### `kilocode/daemon.test.ts` (packages/opencode)
Timing out / failing on Windows:
- `daemon manager > reuses one daemon across caller directories`
- `daemon manager > starts, reuses, authenticates, and stops a daemon`
- `daemon manager > daemon client honors the escape hatch`
- `daemon manager > daemon client returns authenticated attach settings`

### `cli/tui/plugin-loader.test.ts` (packages/opencode)
Whole test file timing out after 300s on Windows.

### `node_modules/shiki/dist/langs/ts.mjs`
Being picked up as a test file and timing out after 300s (bun test discovery issue on Windows).

## Affected PRs

| PR | Title |
|---|---|
| [#10925](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10925) | Suggest skill slash commands in CLI |
| [#10917](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10917) | feat: add JetBrains release skill |
| [#10889](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10889) | release(jetbrains): v7.0.1-rc.5 |
| [#10887](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10887) | docs(kilo-docs): clarify contributor architecture docs |
| [#10827](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10827) | feat(kilo-vscode): support promoted model deep links |
| [#9976](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/9976) | fix(jetbrains): add editor-backed code blocks and optimize session UI |
| [#10611](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10611) | Session export capture |

## Pattern

- Only `unit (windows)` fails; `unit (linux)` and `jetbrains` pass on the first attempt.
- Every failure is a timeout — no assertion errors or logic failures.
- Tests pass on re-run, confirming flakiness rather than a broken test.

## Suggested Fixes

1. Increase per-test timeouts (or use a `SLOW_TIMEOUT` multiplier) for Windows in `session/prompt.test.ts` and `daemon.test.ts`.
2. Investigate why `cli/tui/plugin-loader.test.ts` takes >300s on Windows and optimize or increase the file timeout.
3. Exclude `node_modules` from bun test discovery on Windows (or add a `.testignore` / glob exclude).

---

*Methodology: identified runs with `run_attempt > 1` on `test.yml`, confirmed multiple `unit-windows-N` artifacts (indicating Windows specifically was re-run), and extracted failing test names from job logs for each flaky run.*
title: Flaky Windows Tests - Last 2 Weeks

## Description

The `codebase_search` agent tool fails with a JavaScript runtime error.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run the `codebase_search` tool with any query (e.g., `user authentication`)
2. Observe the error: `undefined is not a constructor (evaluating 'new I(H,L,A)')`

## Expected behavior
The tool should search the codebase and return relevant results.

## Actual behavior
The tool throws an internal JavaScript error before returning any results.

## Environment
- Kilo CLI version: 7.3.21
- Node.js: v22.22.2
- OS: Linux (ZorinOS/Ubuntu 24.04, x86_64, kernel 6.17.0-35-generic)
- ripgrep binary: 15.0.0 (from @vscode/ripgrep@1.18.0 + @vscode/ripgrep-linux-x64@1.18.0)
- Project type: Next.js 16

## Notes
- The dependency `@vscode/ripgrep` and platform binary `@vscode/ripgrep-linux-x64` are correctly installed and the `rg` binary works when called directly.
- The `semantic_search` agent tool works fine.
- Error is minified: `new I(H,L,A)` — suggests a class/service in the tool's implementation is undefined.
- Related issue: https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/912 (closed stale, not planned)

## Additional context
- Kilo IDX indexing: Completed
- Qdrant configured as vector store
- Embedding model configured
title: codebase_search agent tool fails: undefined is not a constructor (evaluating 'new I(H,L,A)')

body:
## Problem

JetBrains-specific changesets are incorrectly appearing in regular releases (extension/CLI). These changesets should only be included in JetBrains-specific releases, but they are surfacing in the standard extension and CLI release changelogs/artifacts.

## Expected Behavior

JetBrains changesets should be scoped exclusively to JetBrains releases and not bleed into regular extension or CLI releases.

## Actual Behavior

JetBrains changesets are included/visible in regular extension and CLI releases.

## Impact

- Confuses users of the standard extension and CLI who see irrelevant JetBrains-specific changes
- May indicate a release pipeline/changeset scoping issue

## Next Steps

- Investigate how changesets are being filtered (or not filtered) per release target
- Ensure JetBrains changesets are properly scoped and excluded from non-JetBrains release outputs
title: JetBrains changesets appearing on regular extension/CLI releases

body:
## Bug: Auto-scroll to bottom broken when "Auto-Collapse Reasoning" is enabled

### Description
On VS Code extension **v7.3.28**, when the option **Settings > Display > Auto-Collapse Reasoning** is enabled, the conversation panel no longer automatically scrolls to the bottom as new messages arrive. Instead, the scroll position stays frozen, requiring constant clicking of the **green down arrow** button to see what the AI model is currently outputting.

This behavior severely degrades the user experience because:
- Every other major VS Code AI extension (OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot, etc.) handles auto-scrolling correctly under equivalent settings.
- The down arrow button is small, easily missed, and requires manual intervention after every few tokens of output.
- It breaks the natural "set and observe" flow of watching the model reason through a response.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Install or update to Kilo Code VS Code extension v7.3.28.
2. Navigate to **Settings > Display** and enable **Auto-Collapse Reasoning**.
3. Start a new conversation and send a prompt.
4. Observe that the viewport stays locked in place instead of following the model's output.
5. Notice the green down arrow button (sticky-scroll indicator) appears, requiring a click each time to catch up with new output.

### Expected behavior
When **Auto-Collapse Reasoning** is enabled, the conversation should continue to auto-scroll to the bottom so the user can see live output without manual intervention. The down arrow indicator should only appear when the user has intentionally scrolled upward — not as the default scroll state.

### Plugins
_No response_

### Kilo version
v7.3.28

### Screenshot and/or share link
_No response_

### Operating System
- **OS:** CachyOS Linux (Arch-based rolling release)
- **Kernel:** 7.0.10-2-cachyos x86_64
- **Desktop:** KDE Plasma 6.6.5 (Wayland)
- **Display Server:** Wayland

### Terminal
- **VS Code version:** 1.122.1 (x64)
- **Terminal:** VS Code integrated terminal (fish shell — `/bin/fish`)

---

## Feature Proposal: "Jump to original prompt" button (inspired by Discord's Reply mechanic)

### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

When a conversation turn produces a very long response, it becomes difficult for the user to quickly refer back to the **original prompt** they sent for that turn. Manually scrolling up through hundreds of lines of reasoning and code output is painful.

**Proposed solution:** Add a button that, when clicked, instantly scrolls the conversation back to the original user message (prompt) that started the current turn. This is inspired by Discord's "Reply" feature: on Discord, when someone replies to a message, a quoted snippet appears above the text input — clicking that quoted snippet scrolls you directly to the original message, no matter how crowded or long the channel has become.

The same scrolling mechanic applied to Kilo Code would look like this:
- During or after a conversation turn, a small UI element (e.g., an upward arrow with a label like "Jump to prompt" or a clickable prompt preview) appears near the bottom of the assistant's response.
- Clicking it smoothly scrolls the view back to the corresponding user message that initiated that turn.
- This eliminates the need to manually scroll up through potentially hundreds of lines of model output just to verify what was originally asked.

**Why this matters:**
- Users often need to check what exactly they asked to verify the model stayed on track.
- Long reasoning chains from deep-thinking models can push the original prompt far off-screen.
- No manual scrolling needed — one click and you're back at your prompt.
- This would bring Kilo Code's UX in line with modern chat applications that already solve this navigation problem.

I believe both the auto-scroll fix and this navigation enhancement would significantly improve the daily experience of Kilo Code users.
title: Auto-scroll to bottom broken when Auto-Collapse Reasoning is enabled + Proposal: 'Jump to original prompt' button (inspired by Discord's Reply mechanic)

body:
## Bug Description

When using a custom OpenAI-compatible provider with `modalities.input` including `"image"` and `attachment: true`, Kilo still blocks image input with the error:

> ERROR: Cannot read 'image.png' (this model does not support image input). Inform the user.

The API endpoint itself fully supports image input (verified via direct API call).

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure a custom OpenAI-compatible provider in `kilo.jsonc` with image modalities
2. Restart Kilo
3. Try to send/paste an image in chat
4. Error appears: "this model does not support image input"

## Expected Behavior

Kilo should recognize the `modalities.input` configuration and allow image input.

## Actual Behavior

Kilo ignores the custom provider's modalities configuration and rejects image input.

## Config

```json
{
  "provider": {
    "xiaomi-mimo": {
      "name": "xiaomi-mimo",
      "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
      "api": "openai",
      "options": {
        "baseURL": "https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1"
      },
      "models": {
        "mimo-v2.5": {
          "name": "mimo-v2.5",
          "reasoning": true,
          "attachment": true,
          "modalities": {
            "input": ["text", "image"],
            "output": ["text"]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "model": "xiaomi-mimo/mimo-v2.5"
}
```

## Environment

- Kilo CLI version: latest
- OS: Windows 11
- Node.js: v24.16.0

## Additional Context

Direct API test confirms the model accepts image input:

```
POST https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1/chat/completions
Body: {"model":"mimo-v2.5","messages":[{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"text","text":"describe this"},{"type":"image_url","image_url":{"url":"data:image/png;base64,..."}}]}]}
Result: Success - model correctly describes the image
```

It appears Kilo's image support check for custom providers does not read the `modalities` field from the model config, instead falling back to a default text-only assumption.
title: Custom provider model ignores modalities config - image input blocked

### Description

Multiple independent community reports indicate that code indexing setup in the CLI can fail before indexing becomes usable. The indexing entry point is visible in the CLI, but attempting to configure or start indexing results in an error. One report confirmed that selecting multiple embedding models did not resolve the failure.

This was reported after the recent indexing startup, worker-isolation, embedding-model constraint, and stale-config fixes landed. The available reports do not include the rendered error text, provider, or selected model, so the underlying failure path still needs to be identified.

Expected behavior: `/indexing` should either complete setup successfully for supported configurations or present an actionable error that identifies the failing provider, model, or dependency.

Potentially related existing issues cover narrower failure modes, including authentication failures, OpenRouter provider failures, and Debian Tree-sitter initialization. There is not enough information to classify these reports as one of those cases yet.

### Plugins

None known.

### Kilo version

Reported against a recent CLI build on 2026-06-02. Exact version not yet captured.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the Kilo CLI.
2. Run `/indexing`.
3. Configure an embedding model and attempt to initialize code indexing.
4. Observe that setup fails before indexing becomes usable.
5. Try another embedding model and observe that setup can continue to fail.

### Screenshot and/or share link

Not available. Follow-up diagnostics needed: exact CLI error output, CLI version, OS, redacted `indexing` config, embedding provider, and selected embedding model.

Not yet confirmed.

Not yet confirmed.

body:
## Description

The post-merge review for OpenCode v1.14.42 in PR #10811 found three `kilocode_change` comments that no longer protect a real Kilo divergence. The upstream merge branding transforms now reproduce the branded text and URL automatically, so these comments add merge noise and cause the reset-candidate checker to classify two files as `markers-only`.

This is cleanup only. Keep the Kilo-branded values unchanged and remove only the redundant comments.

## Stale comments

- `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/run/permission.shared.ts`: two inline `// kilocode_change` comments on the `Kilo is restarted` strings.
- `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/component/error-component.tsx`: one inline `// kilocode_change` comment on the `https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/new?template=bug-report.yml` URL.

## Why they are stale

- `script/upstream/transforms/transform-take-theirs.ts` applies the `OpenCode` to `Kilo` branding replacement.
- The same transform pipeline rewrites `github.com/anomalyco/opencode` to `github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode`.
- `bun run script/upstream/find-reset-candidates.ts --dry-run` currently reports exactly these two files in the `markers-only` bucket.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Remove the three redundant marker comments without changing the branded values.
- [ ] Run `bun run script/upstream/find-reset-candidates.ts --dry-run` and confirm the two files no longer appear under `markers-only`.
- [ ] Run `bun run script/check-opencode-annotations.ts`.
- [ ] Run `bun run script/check-forbidden-strings.ts`.
- [ ] Keep the patch limited to marker cleanup unless validation reveals a directly related issue.
title: Remove stale kilocode_change markers covered by upstream branding transforms

body:
### Description

With Kilo installed but disabled, GitHub Copilot Chat intermittently auto-skips clarifying questions instead of waiting for user input. In my repro, a fresh post-restart chat replaced the expected question flow with: "The user is not available to respond and will review your work later. Work autonomously and make good decisions."

I then uninstalled Kilo, restarted VS Code, and repeated the same test in a new chat. The problem disappeared immediately: Copilot showed normal interactive askQuestions prompts and captured my answers correctly across multiple follow-up questions.

This looks like an extension interaction bug rather than a pure Kilo UI issue, because the failing mechanism appears in Copilot/VS Code logs. But on my machine the installed-vs-uninstalled state of Kilo was the only variable that changed, and it cleanly flipped the behavior.

### Plugins

GitHub Copilot Chat; LM Studio Copilot Provider

### Kilo version

7.3.21

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install Kilo in VS Code
2. Disable Kilo, but leave it installed
3. Restart VS Code
4. Open a fresh GitHub Copilot Chat agent session
5. Send: Before doing anything else, ask me one clarifying question and wait for my answer.
6. Observe that the clarifying question may be auto-skipped and replaced with: "The user is not available to respond and will review your work later. Work autonomously and make good decisions."
7. Uninstall Kilo completely
8. Restart VS Code
9. Open a new fresh chat session
10. Repeat the exact same prompt
11. Observe that the clarifying-question UI works normally and captures real user input

### Screenshot and/or share link

Failing run:
- C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260602T215823
- Session: f00e58b0-95ba-403e-a8cd-1fdb79fa3c01
- renderer.log: [AskQuestionsTool] Autopilot mode: auto-responding to questions
- GitHub Copilot Chat.log: [ToolCallingLoop] Autopilot: model produced a text-only response, treating as done

Successful run after uninstall:
- C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260602T220156
- Session: e0f2e2a7-b166-490f-8923-9de167eba4c2
- chatSessions artifact shows repeated normal vscode_askQuestions interactions with real user answers recorded

### Operating System

Windows

### Terminal

VS Code integrated terminal (pwsh / PowerShell)

### Additional diagnostics:

1. **VS Code version:** `1.122.1`
2. **GitHub Copilot Chat version:** `0.50.1`
3. **LM Studio Copilot Provider version:** `0.1.27`
4. **Reproduces without LM Studio provider:** **not tested**. Both the failing and successful runs had LM Studio provider active, so LM Studio alone does not explain the issue.
5. **Kilo globally disabled vs workspace-disabled:** **not verified**. I only confirmed **installed-but-disabled** reproduces and **uninstalled** stops reproducing.
6. **Extension host logs before/after uninstall:**
   - **Before uninstall / failing run:** `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260602T215823`
     - `renderer.log`: `[AskQuestionsTool] Autopilot mode: auto-responding to questions`
     - `GitHub Copilot Chat.log`: `[ToolCallingLoop] Autopilot: model produced a text-only response, treating as done`
     - `exthost.log` shows `GitHub.copilot-chat` and `DanLambiase.lmstudio-copilot-provider` activating
   - **After uninstall / successful run:** `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260602T220156`
     - successful fresh session: `e0f2e2a7-b166-490f-8923-9de167eba4c2`
     - corresponding session artifact shows repeated normal `vscode_askQuestions` interactions with real user answers recorded
7. <details><summary> `code --list-extensions --show-versions`:</summary>
   devlon.gitl@1.0.20
   foxundermoon.shell-format@7.2.8
   imperium-dev.copilot-chat-to-markdown@0.0.9
   local.telemetry-sink@1.0.0
   mechatroner.rainbow-csv@3.24.1
   ms-python.debugpy@2026.7.11491011
   ms-python.python@2026.5.2026052901
   ms-python.vscode-pylance@2026.2.103
   ms-python.vscode-python-envs@1.33.2026052901
   ms-vscode.powershell@2026.1.1
   timheuer.awesome-copilot@0.1.42
   upstash.context7-mcp@1.0.1
   usernamehw.errorlens@3.28.0vsls-contrib.gitdoc@0.2.3
</details>


## Additional verification:

I performed another controlled repro with Kilo reinstalled. After restarting VS Code, I disabled Kilo **for this workspace only**, restarted the extension host, and repeated the clarifying-question test in a fresh chat. The same auto-response behavior reproduced.

### This appears to strengthen the original report in two ways:

1. The issue still reproduces when Kilo is disabled only at the workspace level.
2. In that same repro window, the extension host log shows `kilocode.kilo-code` activating.

Current evidence therefore supports the following pattern:

- **installed + disabled** → reproduces
- **workspace-disabled** → reproduces
- **uninstalled** → does not reproduce

<details>
<summary>Logs and session correlation</summary>

- **Extension Host log**  
  `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260603T053047\window1\exthost\exthost.log`  
  `2026-06-03 05:30:50.430 [info] ExtensionService#_doActivateExtension kilocode.kilo-code, startup: false, activationEvent: 'api', root cause: Joouis.agent-maestro`

- **Window log**  
  `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260603T053047\window1\renderer.log`  
  `2026-06-03 05:32:14.758 [info] [AskQuestionsTool] Autopilot mode: auto-responding to questions`

- **GitHub Copilot Chat log**  
  `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260603T053047\window1\exthost\GitHub.copilot-chat\GitHub Copilot Chat.log`  
  `2026-06-03 05:32:19.666 [info] [ToolCallingLoop] Autopilot: model produced a text-only response, treating as done`

- **Session correlation**
