- **Kilo output logs**  
  Present under:  
  `C:\Users\Gustavs\AppData\Roaming\Code\logs\20260603T053047\window1\exthost\output_logging_20260603T053048\`  
  Relevant Kilo log files were present but empty during this repro.

body:
## Bug Report

### Description
When a user removes a skill from the Settings page, the skill is successfully removed in the backend, but the UI does not refresh immediately. The skill entry remains visible in the list until the user manually refreshes the page.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the Settings page
2. Navigate to the Skills section
3. Remove a skill
4. Observe the UI — the removed skill entry is still displayed

### Expected Behavior
After removing a skill, the entry should disappear from the list immediately, reflecting the updated state.

### Actual Behavior
The skill is removed (backend/state is updated), but the UI still shows the entry until a manual page refresh.
title: Removing a skill on Settings page does not refresh the UI immediately

body:
### Description

### Problem
The built-in `kilo-config` skill only documents the scalar form for bash:
`"bash": "allow"` (or "deny"/"ask").

The JSON schema at https://app.kilo.ai/config.json accepts the object form:
```json
"bash": {
  "*": "ask",
  "git *": "deny",
  "git push*": "deny",
  "git reset --hard*": "deny"
}
```

### Kilo version

7.3.16
title: [Docs] kilo-config skill and JSON schema misleading/outdated on bash permission object + glob patterns

comments.body: Same here, but on Windows 11 25H2. Icon disappeared a week or two ago at max I think.

It's a VSCode remote issue. It affects multiple extensions, not just Kilo. The issue and a workaround are described at https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-remote-release/issues/11686

To save a click, the workaround is to add the setting below to your settings file and reconnect:
```json
"remote.SSH.useExecServer": false
```

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

### Problem
Anthropic’s new billing policy (effective 2026-06-15, announced 2026-05-13 via @ClaudeDevs) splits agentic traffic from subscription pool usage:

- **Agent SDK**, `claude -p` (headless), and third‑party apps built on the Agent SDK → only a small monthly credit (e.g. $20-200/mo for Pro/Max plans), then either API‑rate metered billing or suspension.
- **Interactive Claude Code** → remains in the existing subscription pool with no extra per‑token charges.

Currently, Kilocode forwards requests to Anthropic in the original `claude -p` / Agent SDK wire shape. After June 15, that will exhaust the small credit within hours and then incur metered costs or stop working.

### Solution
Add a `--stealth` flag (or environment variable) that reconstructs each outbound request so it is **indistinguishable from an interactive Claude Code session**:

- Rebuild HTTP wire shape: header order, TLS stack, body key order, session‑id lifecycle.
- Match temporal axis: response‑correlated think time and session‑start latency.

### Expected behavior after June 15, 2026
| Tool → Proxy | Before `--stealth` | After `--stealth` (this PR) |
|--------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Kilocode → Anthropic API directly | metered API pricing | metered API pricing (unchanged) |
| Kilocode → proxy that forwards as‑is | small credit → metered | (N/A) |
| Kilocode → proxy with `--stealth` | (N/A) | **subscription pool - unchanged** |

### Verification

After implementing `--stealth` mode, verify the fix works correctly:

1. **Run a KiloCode session** with `--stealth` enabled against a Claude subscription account
2. **Check rate-limit headers** - KiloCode should receive subscription bucket headers (`five_hour` or `seven_day`), not credit-bucket headers
3. **Monitor credit consumption** - The separate agentic credit (e.g., $20-200/mo for Pro/Max plans) should remain untouched after extended KiloCode usage
4. **Test without `--stealth`** - Same KiloCode session should consume the agentic credit, confirming the classifier sees the difference

### Implementation Notes

- Intercept all requests that KiloCode sends when the "Claude Code" provider is selected
- Rewrite them to match Claude Code's **interactive** wire shape (not headless `claude -p`)
- Key differences to implement:
  - Header order and TLS stack fingerprint
  - Session-id lifecycle management
  - Response-correlated timing patterns
  - Interactive session latency characteristics
- Add configuration flag: `--stealth` or `KILO_STEALTH=true`
- Update documentation: "Without `--stealth`, KiloCode will be billed under Anthropic's agentic credit rules after 2026-06-15"

### Why This Matters for KiloCode Users

KiloCode's Claude Code provider currently invokes `claude` CLI in headless mode. After June 15, Anthropic will classify this traffic as "agentic" and move it to a small monthly credit (e.g., $20 for Pro users) before switching to metered API pricing.

With `--stealth` mode, KiloCode users keep their existing subscription benefits without surprise charges or service interruptions.
title: [FEATURE]: Add --stealth mode to reshape agentic traffic as interactive Claude Code (bypass June 15 billing split)

title: Bug Report: Cache hit statistics are inflating rapidly and displaying unrealistic values

body:
### Description

Bug Report: Agent execution panel fails to auto-scroll and resets scroll position
Environment: Visual Studio Code (VS Code)
Extension: Kiocode
Version: 7.3.16
Component: Agent Execution Panel (Extension UI/Webview)
Severity: Medium (Impairs usability and monitoring)

Summary
Within the Kiocode VS Code extension, the Agent execution panel has lost its ability to automatically track and scroll to the latest execution traces. Additionally, manual attempts to scroll to the bottom using the UI's down arrow are overridden, as the view unexpectedly forces the scroll position back to the middle of the panel after a brief delay.

### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

Open the Kiocode Agent execution panel in VS Code while an agent is actively executing tasks.

Observe the panel's scrolling behavior as new log traces are generated (Notice that the view does not automatically scroll down to follow the latest output).

Manually click the "down arrow" button on the panel/scrollbar to force the view to the latest execution position at the very bottom.

Wait for a few seconds without interacting with the mouse, and observe the scroll position.

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

body: ### Question ### Question I recently built the JetBrains plugin from source using the `v7.0.1-rc.4` branch on Linux. I managed to compile it and get it running in PhpStorm, but I am having trouble accessing the plugin settings. When I open the Kilo tool window and try to access the settings ,there are only two options : restart kilo, reinstall kilo **My question is:** Is this a known UI issue/missing feature with the current `v7.0.1-rc.4` frontend ? **Environment:** * **OS:** Fedora 43 * **IDE:** PhpStorm 2026.1.2 * **Kilo version:** v7.0.1-rc.4 (Built from source) * **Java:** Openjdk version "21.0.11" Any guidance on whether this is a local setup issue or a work-in-progress on the RC would be greatly appreciated!

body:
﻿## Description

When deleting a custom agent in the VS Code extension settings, the UI briefly confirms removal but the agent immediately reappears in the list. The agent persists across extension restarts.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open Kilo Code extension settings - Agent Behaviour tab
2. Create a custom agent (e.g. via `Add Agent'' or by editing an existing one)
3. Click the remove/delete button on the custom agent
4. Confirm the deletion in the dialog
5. Observe: agent disappears for a fraction of a second, then reappears

## Expected Behavior

The custom agent should be permanently removed from both the agent list and the underlying configuration (`kilo.json''). It should not reappear after deletion or after extension restart.

## Actual Behavior

The agent reappears immediately after deletion and survives extension restarts. The only way to remove it is to manually edit `kilo.json'' and delete the corresponding `agent.<name>` entry.

## Root Cause Analysis

There are **four coordinated defects** in the deletion pipeline. The two critical ones combine to cause the persistent agent:

### Bug 1 (Critical): Server-side `remove()` does not clean `kilo.json` entries

**File:** `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/agent/index.ts:454-516`

The server-side `KiloAgent.remove()` function:
1. Deletes `.md` files from `{agent,agents}/**/<name>.md` and `{mode,modes}/<name>.md`
2. Removes entries from legacy `.kilocodemodes` YAML files
3. Calls `InstanceRuntime.disposeInstance()` to clear in-memory cache
4. **Does NOT remove the `config.agent[name]` entry from `kilo.json`**

When the CLI instance is rebuilt after deletion, it reads `kilo.json` and **recreates the agent** from the surviving config entry:

```typescript
// packages/opencode/src/agent/agent.ts:257-272
const agentConfigs = KiloAgent.preprocessConfig(cfg.agent ?? {})
for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(agentConfigs)) {
  if (value.disable) { delete agents[key]; continue }
  let item = agents[key]
  if (!item)
    item = agents[key] = {
      name: key,
      mode: "all",
      permission: Permission.merge(defaults, user),
      options: {},
      native: false,  // agent recreated from kilo.json config
    }
  // ... fields populated from value
}
```

### Bug 2 (Critical): `handleRemoveAgent` success path does not re-fetch config

**File:** `packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts:1959-1981`

When the server-side removal succeeds, the extension:
- Clears `cachedAgentsMessage` and re-fetches agents
- Does NOT clear `cachedConfigMessage`
- Does NOT call `fetchAndSendConfig()`
- Early returns, skipping the marketplace fallback

```typescript
private async handleRemoveAgent(name: string): Promise<void> {
  if (!this.client) return
  try {
    const result = await this.client.kilocode.removeAgent({ name, directory: ... })
    if (!result.error) {
      this.cachedAgentsMessage = null        // agents cache cleared
      await this.fetchAndSendAgents()         // agents re-fetched
      return                                  // config NOT refreshed
    }
  } catch { /* fall through */ }
  // Fallback: marketplace removal (only reached if server failed)
}
```

The marketplace fallback path (triggered only on server errors) works correctly via `invalidateAfterMarketplaceChange()`, which refreshes both agents AND config:

```typescript
// KiloProvider.ts:2127-2129 (fallback path - CORRECT)
this.cachedAgentsMessage = null
this.cachedConfigMessage = null
await Promise.all([this.fetchAndSendAgents(), this.fetchAndSendConfig()])
```

### Bug 3 (Visual): Optimistic removal does not update `allAgents` signal

**File:** `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/context/session.tsx:352-369`

`removeAgent()` optimistically filters `agents` but not `allAgents`:

```typescript
const removeAgent = (name: string) => {
  setAgents((prev) => prev.filter((a) => a.name !== name))  // agents updated
  // setAllAgents NOT called
  vscode.postMessage({ type: "removeAgent", name })
}
```

The `agentNames` memo in `AgentBehaviourTab.tsx:85-100` reads from `allAgents()`, so the agent immediately reappears in the UI list until the server response arrives (which, due to Bugs 1-2, still contains the agent).

### Bug 4 (Race condition): `saving()` guard can block config refresh after removal

**File:** `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/context/config.tsx:84-87`

```typescript
if (message.type === "configLoaded") {
  if (saving()) return  // skips config update if save is in-flight
}
```

If the user edited the agent before deleting it, the in-flight save can cause the subsequent `configLoaded` message (triggered by deletion) to be silently dropped.

## Impact Table

| Scenario | .md deleted? | kilo.json cleaned? | Config refreshed? | Agent persists? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Agent created via UI, deleted (server path) | yes | no | no | **Yes** |
| Marketplace agent, deleted (server path) | yes | N/A (no entry) | no | May work if no kilo.json edits |
| Agent created via UI, deleted (fallback path) | yes | yes | yes | No |

## Suggested Fix

1. **`packages/opencode/src/kilocode/agent/index.ts` (`remove`):** After deleting `.md` and `.kilocodemodes` entries, also remove the `config.agent[name]` entry from `kilo.json` (project and global scopes), similar to what `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/marketplace/installer.ts:130-162` does in the fallback path.

2. **`packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts` (`handleRemoveAgent`):** On the success path, replace the single `fetchAndSendAgents()` call with `invalidateAfterMarketplaceChange()` or equivalent logic that refreshes both agents and config.

3. **`packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/context/session.tsx` (`removeAgent`):** Add `setAllAgents((prev) => prev.filter((a) => a.name !== name))` alongside the existing `setAgents` call for immediate visual consistency.

## Environment

- Kilo Code extension (latest)
- VS Code
- Windows
title: Custom agent remains listed after deletion — kilo.json config entry not cleaned up

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Current behaviour:
[Gemini:] 429 Too Many Requests - try again in 30 sec
[KiloCode:] OK, let's retry in 5 sec
[Gemini:] 429 Too Many Requests - try again in 25 sec
[KiloCode:] OK, now let's try 10 sec
[Gemini:] 429 You're a spammer. Go cool off for a minute.
[KiloCode:] Let's try half a minute!
[Gemini:] 429 No, not yet. Still half a minute to go.
[KiloCode:] Well, damn, no luck... I guess I'll wait for like 5 minutes and then try again.

Better behaviour: add a configuration option to set those values - either to one value (30s) or a list of values ("10,30,60")

Ideal behaviour: in case of a 429, parse the reply looking for `'[Tt]ry again in (\d+) s'`, and if found, retry exactly in that time.

I know that there is an option to "rate-limit" by setting a "minimum time between API requests", but this can only set "wait for one minute between any calls"; it's too aggressive when all you want is real "rate limiting" (e.g. "no more than 5 requests per minute, but 5 quick requests even in 10 seconds is fine").

body:
## Description

Autocomplete begins locally processing editor content as soon as the VS Code extension activates, even when autocomplete is disabled, snoozed, or not the selected mode.

This behavior already exists on `main`: `AutocompleteServiceManager` eagerly constructs `AutocompleteInlineCompletionProvider`, whose constructor immediately initializes recent-edit, recent-visit, and context tracking services. Those services subscribe to editor/document activity and retain context after user interactions before autocomplete is actually active.

While reviewing Mercury Next Edit in #10621, the same lifecycle concern surfaced for its edit-history tracker. NES should follow the eventual shared lifecycle pattern rather than introducing another opt-in-specific eager tracker.

This is local processing rather than unintended transmission: provider request paths still guard what is sent upstream. However, it increases memory/runtime overhead and performs privacy-sensitive content retention outside the opt-in boundary.

## Steps to reproduce

1. On current `main`, activate the VS Code extension with autocomplete disabled or snoozed.
2. Observe that `AutocompleteServiceManager` constructs `AutocompleteInlineCompletionProvider` during activation in `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/autocomplete/AutocompleteServiceManager.ts`.
3. Observe that the provider constructor immediately creates tracking/context services in `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/autocomplete/classic-auto-complete/AutocompleteInlineCompletionProvider.ts` before inline completion registration is enabled.
4. Edit or navigate through files while autocomplete remains disabled; the eagerly initialized trackers receive document/editor events and retain recent context.

## Expected behavior

Document-content trackers used for autocomplete should only be active while the relevant autocomplete mode is enabled and registered, and should release retained context when autocomplete is disabled, snoozed, or switched away.

## Suggested direction

- Lifecycle-gate classic autocomplete tracking behind actual enabled/registered state.
- Provide the same activation/deactivation boundary for Next Edit edit history and pending-state resources.
- Clear retained snapshots/snippets/diffs when tracking is deactivated.
- Add tests covering disabled, snoozed, and provider-switch transitions.

## References

- Follow-up discovered during review of #10621; this is pre-existing on `main` and should not by itself block that PR.
- Related external implementation: #10536.

## Kilo version

Current `main` and the Mercury Next Edit draft branch.

## Operating System

All platforms; lifecycle behavior is extension-side.
title: Defer autocomplete document tracking until the feature is active

### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

## Summary

Kilo Gateway currently exposes an embedding model catalog endpoint:

```bash
GET /api/gateway/embedding-models
```

which returns a valid list of embedding models such as:

* `openai/text-embedding-3-small`
* `mistralai/mistral-embed-2312`
* `qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b`

However, the actual OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint appears to reject requests:

```bash
POST /api/gateway/v1/embeddings
```

Response:

```json
{
  "error":"Invalid path",
  "error_type":"invalid_path",
  "message":"This endpoint only accepts the path `/chat/completions`."
}
```

## Why this matters

Kilo Gateway is documented and advertised as OpenAI-compatible.

Many external tools and OSS projects (Open Notebook, LangChain, LlamaIndex, etc.) expect OpenAI-compatible embedding support through:

```txt
POST /v1/embeddings
```

Currently, Kilo Gateway exposes embedding model metadata but not the inference endpoint itself, which creates confusion for integrators.

## Existing related issue

Related to:

* #9478

That issue mentioned that Kilo Gateway did not yet have embedding models.

Now, embedding models are already exposed through `/embedding-models`, so this seems like the next logical step.

## Request

I would like clarification on the intended direction:

* Is `/v1/embeddings` support planned for Kilo Gateway?
* Is the current restriction intentional?
* Should external tools use another endpoint or architecture for embeddings?

I am not currently able to contribute an implementation PR myself, so this issue is mainly intended to start discussion and clarify roadmap / intended behavior for OpenAI-compatible integrations.
title: [FEATURE]: Expose Kilo Gateway  embedding model

body:
## Problem
In the provider settings UI, there is an 'Enable' button that is confusing to users. The button actually adds a provider to the disabled list (i.e., disabling it), but the label 'Enable' implies the opposite action. Users are unsure what will happen when they click it.

## Suggested Fix
Replace the 'Enable' button with an X (remove/close) icon to make it visually clear that clicking it will disable/remove the provider from the active list.


---
Built for [Marius Wichtner](https://kilo-code.slack.com/archives/C0AFBRXUH2N/p1779806763627349?thread_ts=1779805447.785029&cid=C0AFBRXUH2N) by [Kilo for Slack](https://kilo.ai/slack)

comments.body:
## Design Feedback

The current `Enable` button label is misleading: it implies a provider can be enabled from that control, but the action actually removes the provider from the active list. This creates confusion about the expected outcome.

**Refined requirement:** Replace the `Enable` button with an ❌ (remove/close) icon. Using a recognisable dismiss icon makes the destructive intent immediately clear — users will understand they are removing the provider from the active list, not enabling something new.

title: Replace 'Enable' button with an X icon in the provider settings UI

body:
## Problem
The 'Show more providers' button in the provider settings UI is not obviously actionable. Users don't immediately understand that clicking it allows them to add more providers. The button is easy to overlook and its purpose is unclear at a glance.

## Suggested Fix
Make the 'Show more providers' button more visually prominent (e.g., a clearly styled button or call-to-action) and consider updating the label to something more descriptive like "Add a provider" or "Add more providers".

comments.body:
## Design Feedback

The existing provider list/dropdown draws the eye naturally, which means the mechanism to add new providers gets visually overshadowed. Users spend their attention on the already-configured providers rather than discovering how to add more.

**Refined requirement:** The "Show more providers" option must be treated as a primary action — it is the main entry point for adding providers, and it should look like one. The current presentation makes it feel secondary or optional. Consider a clearly styled call-to-action (e.g. a prominent button or a visually distinct link) so users can discover it without having to search.

title: Make 'Show more providers' button more prominent and descriptive

### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I'd like to add **OrcaRouter** as a built-in AI provider in Kilo Code, alongside the existing AI gateways (OpenRouter, Glama, Requesty, etc.).

  **What is OrcaRouter?**

  [OrcaRouter](https://www.orcarouter.ai) is an OpenAI-compatible API gateway that routes requests to OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, DeepSeek, xAI Grok, Alibaba Qwen, Moonshot Kimi, MiniMax, and Z.ai. Models are reached at the upstream provider's published per-token price; the
   gateway's revenue comes from optional paid plans, not from inflating token costs.

  **Why add it to Kilo Code?**

  - Single API key gives access to ~80 chat models from 9 vendors via Kilo Code's existing provider abstraction.
  - Speaks the OpenAI wire shape end-to-end, so it slots in via `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` with no new SDK dependency.
  - Has a built-in `auto` router (cheapest / balanced / quality / adaptive strategies, configurable from the OrcaRouter dashboard) that Kilo Code users can address as `orcarouter/auto`.
  - Reasoning effort, streaming, tool calling, and multimodal inputs all work out of the box because OrcaRouter forwards in OpenAI shape and translates per-vendor where needed.

  **Proposed implementation**

  - New provider module `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/provider/orcarouter.ts` that fetches the OrcaRouter `/api/pricing` catalog and transforms it to the Kilo `ModelsDev.Model` shape. Filters non-chat models (image / video / embedding / TTS / STT / rerank). Adds the
  `orcarouter/auto` router as a synthetic catalog entry.
  - Custom loader entry in `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/provider/provider.ts` that gates `autoload` on whether a key is configured.
  - Minimal `// kilocode_change` hooks in the shared `packages/opencode/src/provider/{model-cache,models,transform}.ts` files to wire the loader into the upstream flow — mirroring the pattern other Kilo-side providers (apertis) already use.
  - New docs page `packages/kilo-docs/pages/ai-providers/orcarouter.md` and supporting nav / lychee / index edits.
  - Unit tests covering catalog parsing, chat-only filtering, temperature blacklist for known-reject reasoning models, and reasoning-by-family-pattern detection.
  - A corresponding gateway-side PR in `Kilo-Org/cloud` will register OrcaRouter as a known upstream so Kilo Gateway users can also reach it.

  **Maintenance**

  I'm an engineer on the OrcaRouter team and will own ongoing maintenance for this provider integration.

  I've verified there's no existing issue or PR proposing this provider in `Kilo-Org/kilocode`.
title: [FEATURE]: Add OrcaRouter as a built-in AI provider

body:
Kilo Code currently supports `AGENTS.md` at the project level and `~/.config/kilo/AGENTS.md` as a global instruction source. 

To improve cross-agent interoperability, we should support checking the unified `~/.agents/AGENTS.md` file as a global fallback. This ensures that developers don't have to duplicate their global safety rules, post-quantum cryptography standards, and reasoning preferences between Kilo Code, Claude Code, and other CLI tools.

comments.body:
To clarify the motivation for standardizing on `~/.agents/AGENTS.md` (the global `.agents` home directory file):

While several CLI tools have their own tool-specific global folders (e.g., `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, `~/.config/kilo/AGENTS.md`, `~/.deepagents/AGENTS.md`), standardizing on a single, vendor-neutral `~/.agents/AGENTS.md` file is crucial for modern multi-agent workflows. 

When developers write custom skills, orchestrators, or MCP tools that trigger multiple CLI agents with different provider/model configurations, it is highly complex to keep multiple rules files synchronized. Using a unified `~/.agents/` directory in the home folder ensures that all agents and sub-agents can fetch their core guidelines (such as security standards, reasoning structures, and data handling rules) from a single source of truth, while still respecting project-level `AGENTS.md` specifications for workspace-specific context.
Can you reference the standard that supports this feature request?

title: Feat: Support global ~/.agents/AGENTS.md fallback path

body:
## Description

After #10358, auto-retry on network errors works with a 10-second countdown. However, for transient provider-side connection resets, the "Network disconnected" / "Connection reset by server" banner still appears in the TUI, breaking the user's flow — even though the retry always succeeds within seconds.

## Context

When using Zhipu GLM-5.1 via OpenAI-compatible API (`zai-coding-plan` provider), extended thinking phases produce 60-120 seconds of silence on the SSE stream. The provider's infrastructure (likely a load balancer idle timeout) resets the connection at the boundary when the model finishes thinking and starts emitting tokens. This happens **3-5 times per 2-hour coding session**.

The log shows `isRetryable: true` on every one of these resets, and manual retries (or the 10s auto-retry) always succeed immediately:

```
17:22:12  INFO  service=llm providerID=zai-coding-plan modelID=glm-5.1 stream
17:22:13  WARN  service=session.network message=Connection reset by server waiting for network
17:22:13  WARN  service=session.processor.kilo message=Connection reset by server session offline
17:22:19  INFO  service=llm providerID=zai-coding-plan modelID=glm-5.1 stream   ← retry succeeds
```

## Current behavior

1. Connection drops → "Network disconnected" banner appears
2. TUI shows "Network reconnected" with 10s countdown
3. Auto-retries after countdown (or user presses Enter)
4. Retry succeeds → turn resumes

**Problem:** Steps 1-3 are visual noise. The user sees the error banner, loses their train of thought, and has to wait — even though the situation is fully recoverable.

## Proposed behavior

For `isRetryable: true` stream errors:

1. Connection drops → **retry silently in the background** (no TUI banner)
2. If retry succeeds → turn continues as if nothing happened
3. If retry fails → **then** show the network error banner with countdown
4. After N consecutive silent failures (e.g., 3) → show the banner regardless

This matches the behavior of other streaming clients (Cursor, Continue, OpenAI SDK) which handle transient resets transparently.

## Environment

- Kilo CLI v7.3.1 (Arch Linux / CachyOS)
- Provider: `zai-coding-plan/glm-5.1`
- Related: #9792, #10358
title: Feature: Silent auto-retry for transient stream errors (suppress visual interruption)

body:
### Description

Currently there are three locations where model variants are stored:
- VSCode state storage under the `variantSelections` key
- `~/.kilo/kilo.json` configuration file under the `agent.[agent-name].variant` keys
- `~/.local/state/kilo/model.json` under the `variant` key

This leads to bugs where the selected variant does not match what the user expects, mismatch between selected variants in the VSCode extension and the CLI, and general maintenance burden.

`~/.kilo/kilo.json` seems to be the most reasonable location to use as the source of truth, as it is already shared between the VSCode extension and CLI. Update any sites in both the VSCode extension and CLI code which write to or read from one of the other sources to instead use `~/.kilo/kilo.json`.

Together with #10505, this may also resolve #8394 and #8753, as the settings UI stores the variant per-agent in `~/.kilo/kilo.json` in #10505, but these variants are not used as expected because they are read from VSCode state storage. While updating, ensure the specified variant for each agent in `~/.kilo/kilo.json` is actually used, unless overridden by more specific program behavior.

body:
### Description

### Describe the bug
When using MiMo reasoning models (e.g., MiMo-V2.5-Pro) in Agent mode with tool calls, the API throws a `400 Bad Request` error on the second turn of the conversation. 

Based on the official API documentation from the model provider, this happens because the `reasoning_content` generated in the first turn is not preserved and passed back in the subsequent request.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Configure a MiMo reasoning model via the custom OpenAI/Anthropic API provider.
2. Enable Agent mode / tool calls (e.g., asking it to search files or run a terminal command).
3. Send a prompt that triggers a tool call. The first turn works fine.
4. Send a follow-up prompt in the same conversation.
5. The API immediately returns a 400 error.

### Expected Behavior
The multi-turn conversation should continue smoothly with tool calling, and the model should receive the full context of its previous thoughts.

### Root Cause Analysis
According to the provider's API guidelines:
> If the model generates a `reasoning_content` field along with `tool_calls` in a turn, the client **MUST** include this exact `reasoning_content` field in the `assistant` message history for all subsequent API requests. If this field is missing when `tool_calls` exist in the history, the API will reject the request with a 400 error to prevent context fragmentation and hallucination.

It appears that Kilo Code currently strips out or drops the `reasoning_content` (or thinking blocks) from the `assistant` message history when building the `messages` array for the next round of requests.

### Suggested Fix
When appending the assistant's previous response to the message history, please ensure that the `reasoning_content` field (if present) is mapped and fully preserved in the payload sent back to the API. 

For example:
```typescript
const assistantMsg: any = {
    role: "assistant",
    content: message.text,
    tool_calls: message.toolCalls
};
// Must preserve reasoning_content for API validation
if (message.reasoning_content) {
    assistantMsg.reasoning_content = message.reasoning_content;
}
messages.push(assistantMsg);

### Plugins

comments.body:
Looks like there is an upstream issue there 
https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/19130

Same issue here on **Windows 11 Insider Preview 25H2 (build 26200, x64)** but with a different underlying error.

### Symptom
Running `kilo` (v7.3.50) in PowerShell hangs indefinitely (no output, no crash).

### Investigation findings

1. **kilo.exe is actually Bun v1.3.14** like opencode:
   ```
   OriginalFilename : bun.exe
   FileDescription  : Bun
   ProductName      : Bun
   CompanyName      : Oven
   FileVersion      : 1.3.14
   ```

2. **Opencode (v1.17.x) has the same TUI crash** on the same machine — and its detailed error (visible in v1.16.2) is:
   ```
   Failed to initialize OpenTUI render library: 
   Failed to open library "B:/~BUN/root/opentui-jdk99d7k.dll": error code 126
   ```
   Error 126 = `ERROR_MOD_NOT_FOUND` — Windows `LoadLibrary` cannot resolve the Bun virtual filesystem path.

3. **The pattern is consistent**: any Bun-packaged CLI tool that uses OpenTUI fails on this Windows build.

4. **Working modes** (no TUI involvement):
   - ✅ `kilo run`
   - ✅ `kilo web`
   - ✅ `kilo --help`

5. **The `B:/~BUN/root/` path is a Bun internal virtual path**, not a real filesystem path. When Bun passes this path to Windows `LoadLibrary`, it cannot resolve it.

### My setup
- Windows 11 Insider Preview 25H2 (build 26200)
- PowerShell 7.6.3
- Windows Terminal
- Kilo v7.3.50 + opencode v1.17.9 (both affected, both Bun v1.3.14)

### Related
- opencode issue #32706 (same OpenTUI / Effect.tryPromise crash)
- Bun issue oven-sh/bun#28055 (Windows ARM64 FFI, same `B:/~BUN/root/` pattern)

If a RAM disk is installed and the system and user TMP and TEMP directories are placed on the virtual disk, this issue will occur. If you need to use a virtual disk, the mode should be set to SCSI hard disk, not Direct-IO hard disk.

title: Failed to initialize OpenTUI render library

body:
## Summary

The JetBrains plugin should support **plan mode** — where the agent creates a structured plan before executing — and allow the user to switch from plan mode to implementation mode within the plugin UI.

## Context

Plan mode is implemented in the CLI as a built-in agent named `"plan"`. It is distinct from the `"code"` (implementation) agent. The plan agent has a restrictive permission guard: it may only write to `.kilo/plans/*.md`, and all other file writes are denied. It uses read-only tools to analyse the codebase and produce a plan document.

When the plan agent finishes, the CLI surfaces a `question.asked` SSE event asking the user: **"Ready to implement?"** with two options:
- **Start new session** — creates a new session, carries over the plan and todo list, and starts the `code` agent
- **Continue here** — injects a synthetic user message and switches to the `code` agent in the current session

## VS Code implementation reference

### Mode selection

| File | Key role |
|---|---|
| `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/shared/ModeSwitcher.tsx` | Dropdown listing all available agents; `ModeSwitcherBase` (line 41), `ModeSwitcher` (line 177) |
| `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/chat/PromptInput.tsx:1086` | Renders `<ModeSwitcher sessionID={sid} />` |
| `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/context/session.tsx` | `selectAgent()`, `promptAgent()`, `sendMessage()` with `agent` field |

### Agent list data flow

1. Webview posts `{ type: "requestAgents" }` → extension responds with `agentsLoaded`
2. CLI: `GET /instance/agent/list` returns available agents including `plan`
3. Webview: `store.agentSelections[sessionID]` tracks the selected agent per session

### Plan → implementation switch data flow

1. CLI plan agent completes → calls `PlanFollowup.ask()` (`packages/opencode/src/kilocode/plan-followup.ts:453`)
2. CLI emits `question.asked` SSE event with two choices: "Start new session" / "Continue here"
3. Extension maps `question.asked` → `{ type: "questionRequest", question: {...} }` (via `kilo-provider-utils.ts:537–547`)
4. Webview renders `QuestionDock` (or inline) prompting the user
5. User answer: `postMessage({ type: "questionReply", answer })` → CLI resolves the question and switches agents

## What to build for JetBrains

### 1. Mode switcher UI

- Add a **mode/agent selector** to the JetBrains prompt input area (dropdown, segmented button, or combo box)
- Populate the list by calling `GET /instance/agent/list` — look for agents named `plan`, `code`, `ask`
- Display human-readable names (use `displayName` from `AgentInfo` if present, else the `name` field)
- Pass the selected `agent` name when calling `POST /session/{id}/chat`
- Keyboard shortcut parity: `Ctrl+.` / `Cmd+.` to cycle modes (tracked separately in #9878)

### 2. Question rendering for plan → implementation

- Handle `question.asked` SSE events in the JetBrains session event loop
- Render the question as an inline UI element in the conversation panel (buttons for each option)
- On user selection, call `POST /session/{id}/question/reply` with the chosen option
- This powers the plan → implementation switch and any other blocking questions from the CLI

### AgentInfo type (from the CLI)

```ts
interface AgentInfo {
  name: string
  displayName?: string
  description?: string
  deprecated?: boolean
  native?: boolean
}
```

## Plan agent constraints (for reference)

The plan agent:
- Only writes to `.kilo/plans/*.md` (all other writes denied)
- Reads are allowed on all files except `.env`
- Bash is read-only (no side-effecting commands)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can select "plan" mode from the JetBrains prompt area
- [ ] Sending a message with "plan" mode selected calls `chat` with `agent: "plan"`
- [ ] `question.asked` SSE events are surfaced in the JetBrains UI as actionable prompts
- [ ] User can select "Start new session" or "Continue here" to switch to implementation
- [ ] The mode selector reflects the currently active agent during the session

## Related

- Mode shortcuts parity: #9878
- Plan mode research: #8319
- CLI plan-followup: `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/plan-followup.ts`
- CLI agent list: `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/agent/index.ts`
title: Plan mode support and plan-to-implementation switch in the JetBrains plugin

body:
## Summary

On a fresh install of the JetBrains plugin, file-system tools used by the agent (read, edit, write, glob, list, grep) should default to **"ask"** (require user approval before executing). This is a temporary JetBrains-only workaround until proper settings sync between the CLI config and the JetBrains UI is in place.

## Context and motivation

The CLI's permission evaluation (`packages/opencode/src/permission/evaluate.ts`) already defaults to `"ask"` when no matching rule is found:

```ts
return match ?? { action: "ask", permission, pattern: "*" }
```

However, on a fresh JetBrains install the JetBrains plugin may not communicate any permission state to the user, leaving them unaware that the agent is autonomously reading and modifying files. Until a full settings sync flow exists (where the user explicitly configures permissions via a settings UI like the VS Code PermissionEditor), JetBrains should proactively surface the "ask" default to ensure users are informed and in control.

## VS Code comparison

In VS Code, the `PermissionEditor` component (`packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/PermissionEditor.tsx`) shows all tools with their current permission level. The utility `effectiveRuleLevel()` in `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/permission-utils.ts` returns `"ask"` as the fallback when the config is empty. VS Code users see the settings panel and can actively change defaults; JetBrains users currently have no equivalent first-run experience.

### Tools that need "ask" default

| Tool | Pattern |
|---|---|
| `read` | `*` |
| `edit` | `*` |
| `write` | `*` |
| `glob` | `*` |
| `list` | `*` |
| `grep` | `*` |
| `bash` | `*` (already defaults to ask/deny in most configurations) |

## What to build

This is scoped as a **temporary JetBrains-only hack** — not a permanent solution:

1. **On plugin first-run detection** — detect when no permission rules exist in the CLI config for this user (i.e., `permission` section is absent or empty in `~/.kilo/config.json`)
2. **Write "ask" defaults** — write explicit `ask` rules for the file tools listed above into the CLI config, or alternatively instruct the CLI via its config API to treat unset rules as `"ask"` (which it already does by default — so this may just require ensuring JetBrains honors and surfaces the default)
3. **First-run notice** — show the user a one-time notification: "File operations will require your approval. You can change this in Settings → Permissions."
4. **Remove once settings sync exists** — this workaround should be removed when JetBrains gains a full permission settings editor (tracking: #9378) and proper config sync

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] On first install (no prior config), file tools default to "ask" behavior in the JetBrains plugin
- [ ] The user sees a clear first-run notice explaining approval requirements
- [ ] Existing users with configured rules are not affected
- [ ] The workaround is annotated with a `// kilocode_change` marker and a TODO pointing to #9378

## Related

- Permission dock parity: #9383
- Auto-approve settings editor: #9378
- CLI permission evaluation: `packages/opencode/src/permission/evaluate.ts`
title: Default file tool permissions to 'ask' on fresh JetBrains plugin install

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Add Valkey (with ValkeySearch module) as a supported vector store backend for codebase indexing, alongside the existing LanceDB and Qdrant options.

**Why add another vector store?**

The current options each have friction points that Valkey addresses:

- **Qdrant** requires deploying and managing a dedicated vector database that serves no other purpose in most stacks. Many teams won't approve adding a single-purpose service just for code search.
- **LanceDB** is embedded/file-based, which limits it to single-machine use and makes it unsuitable for shared or remote development environments.

Valkey (the community-driven Linux Foundation governed Redis fork) is already deployed in a huge number of organizations for caching, session storage, and pub/sub. Its ValkeySearch module adds vector similarity search capabilities to an existing service. This means teams can enable codebase indexing without provisioning any new infrastructure — they just use what's already running.

**Use case:**

Organizations with strict infrastructure policies or privacy requirements benefit the most. They already have Valkey/Redis running, they control where their data lives, and they don't need to onboard a new service or send embeddings to a third party. Individual developers who run Valkey locally also get a zero-setup path to indexing.

Supporting other vector databases also was raised in the discussion on #6144, where a contributor asked about supporting additional vector databases beyond LanceDB and Qdrant.

**What this would involve:**

- A new `"valkey"` option for the `vectorStoreProvider` configuration field
- Connection configuration (URL + optional password) similar to how Qdrant is configured today
- Full feature parity with the existing vector store interface: initialization, upsert, cosine similarity search with directory filtering, deletion, collection lifecycle, and metadata/indexing state management
- The same graceful behavior users expect (non-existent collections don't throw, indexing state is tracked, embedding profile changes trigger re-indexing)

**Benefits:**

- Reuse existing infrastructure — no new services to deploy, monitor, or pay for
- Familiar operational tooling (valkey-cli, redis-cli, existing monitoring, backups)
- Privacy-friendly — embeddings stay on infrastructure the team already controls
- High performance — Valkey is optimized for low-latency in-memory operations
- Broad ecosystem — works with any Valkey-compatible deployment (AWS ElastiCache, self-hosted, etc.)

The `ValkeyVectorStore` class is functional with full interface parity (init, upsert, search, deletion, lifecycle, metadata). Will open a PR once tests are cleaned up and I do manual testing with the extension.
@rileydes-improving not sure if we want to add this now, we most likely want to stabilize indexing first.
Sounds good @marius-kilocode . I have it mostly completed so I'll put the PR up and update it later once the indexing is stable.
Thanks!

Just a thought while developing valkey vector store, but collection ID or similar could be added configuration parameter.

In production deployments,  with a centralized valkey vector storage, you could do shared indexing between all developers working on a project and save an enormous amount of cost for embedding api calls. It would also ensure reproducibility and allow indexing to become a CI job.

The new VS Code extension, however, doesn't have the `Review` mode at all.

body:
## Problem

Kilo Code's built-in tool descriptions are excessively verbose, burning ~37K chars (~9K tokens) on every request before the first user message even arrives. This is purely Kilo's overhead — MCP tools are separate and not part of this issue.

### Built-in tool sizes (v7.3.1, clean session):

| Tool | Chars | Issue |
|---|---|---|
| `bash` | 10,308 | 9,917 chars of description: git commit/push workflows, PR creation guide, quoting examples, error handling — all inlined in the function definition |
| `todowrite` | 9,220 | 8,845 chars of example narratives explaining when to use a todo list |
| `task` | 4,563 | Long usage notes, subagent type descriptions, agent workflow instructions |
| `suggest` | 2,261 | Full review guidelines, when to suggest / when not to, command selection rules |
| `skill` | 1,725 | Lists all available skills inline despite skills being loaded lazily via tool call |
| `edit` | 1,641 | Verbose instructions about line number prefixes, matching behavior, emoji rules |
| `read` | 1,396 | |
| `kilo_local_recall` | 1,332 | |
| `webfetch` | 983 | |
| `question` | 934 | |
| `grep` | 901 | |
| `write` | 851 | |
| `plan_exit` | 799 | |
| `glob` | 761 | |
| **Total** | **37,675** | |

The `bash` and `todowrite` tools alone account for over half of this.

## Proposed Fix

### Trim built-in tool descriptions (non-breaking)

Move detailed workflow instructions, examples, and step-by-step guides **out of individual function descriptions** and into a shared "tool guide" section in the system prompt (or a lazy-loaded resource). Tool descriptions should be 1-3 sentences describing *what* the tool does:

**Current `bash` description** (9,917 chars): Includes directory verification steps, quoting rules, git commit/Push guides, PR creation instructions, temporary directory notes, OS/shell environment details.

**Proposed `bash` description** (~300 chars):
> Executes a shell command in a persistent session. Use for git, npm, docker, and other terminal operations. Specify `workdir` to run in a different directory. Commands timeout after 2 minutes by default; use the `timeout` parameter to adjust.

**Current `todowrite` description** (8,845 chars): Includes 4 full narrative examples of when to use/not-use the todo list.

**Proposed `todowrite` description** (~200 chars):
> Creates and manages a structured task list for the current session. Use for complex multi-step tasks with 3+ distinct actions. Mark tasks as pending, in_progress, completed, or cancelled.

### Estimated Savings

| Tool | Current | Proposed | Savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| `bash` | 10,308 | ~700 | ~9,600 |
| `todowrite` | 9,220 | ~500 | ~8,700 |
| `task` | 4,563 | ~800 | ~3,700 |
| `suggest` | 2,261 | ~500 | ~1,700 |
| `skill` | 1,725 | ~300 | ~1,400 |
| `edit` | 1,641 | ~500 | ~1,100 |
| Others | ~10,000 | ~5,000 | ~5,000 |
| **Total** | **37,675** | **~8,300** | **~29,000 chars (~7K tokens)** |

This is a **77% reduction in built-in tool overhead** — from ~9K tokens to ~2K tokens — with zero behavioral changes.

## Related: Lazy MCP tool loading (separate feature request)

Users with many MCP servers (Firecrawl, Apify, Tavily, etc.) face additional bloat from third-party tools. A `mcp` meta-tool or tool-gating mechanism that only sends MCP tools on demand would complement this fix, but that is a separate architectural change and should not gate the simpler description-trimming work above.
title: Request: Trim built-in tool descriptions (77% reduction, ~7K tokens saved)

### Question

I was asked by my colleagues about whether there are plans to provide a Kilo Code extension/plugin for Visual Studio in future. I don’t know whether this has been discussed within the team, hence this question. I know that Kilo Code was provided as plugin to Jetbrains products, but maybe the Visual Studio user base is not as big within this community?

Thanks for reply in advance!

comments.body:
It has come up, but we're not seeing a lot of interest, and maintaining an separate codebase is a lot of work.

I'm not saying we won't do it, but it's not on the short term roadmap. Long term it does really fit with our vision that you can use kilo in the editor you're already using. For now i'd use the CLI next to the IDE

Thanks for the quick reply, @markijbema !

That was what I recommended to them as well, as Kilo CLI does offer exactly the same agentic workflows as corresponding extension in VS Code or plugin in Jetbrains products. You can even run Kilo CLI from a terminal that's opened in Visual Studio and place it to the side and there you go. But they weren't really convinced by this, which prompted me to ask you about what the plans are for releasing Kilo Code for Visual Studio.

So I'll forward that information to them. Once again, thanks for the quick reply, @markijbema !

body:
## Problem

When Kilo reviews a PR or performs tasks that require creating a worktree in a temporary/external folder (e.g. `/tmp`), users are presented with permission dialogs that lack granularity.

Previously, users could choose to allow access to everything at once. Now, the dialog only allows access to a specific path (e.g. `app/web/src/*`), meaning the dialog re-appears every time a different folder or file path is accessed — even for the same session.

There is also a potential bug where the dialog prompts again for the **exact same path** that was already approved.

## Expected Behavior

- Permission dialogs should offer an option like "Allow all for this session" or "Allow for entire directory tree" so users are not repeatedly interrupted.
- Once a path is approved, it should not prompt again for the same path within the same session.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Ask Kilo to review/verify a PR.
2. Kilo creates a worktree in a tmp folder outside the workspace.
3. Permission dialogs appear repeatedly for each file/folder accessed.
4. Clicking "Allow" for a path may prompt again for the same path shortly after.

## Additional Context

This behaviour is consistent across both the VS Code extension and the CLI.
title: Add granularity to external directory permissions dialogs

body:
### Description

When modifying settings through the Kilo Code VS Code extension UI (e.g., toggling MCP servers on/off, changing triggers, or other configuration), the changes are **not persisted**. There is no longer a "Save" button, suggesting the intent was to auto-save, but:

1. Changes made in the UI do not take effect
2. After closing and reopening VS Code, all settings revert to their previous state
3. MCP servers that were disabled via the UI still show as active after restart

### Plugins

VS Code Extension (kilocode.kilo-code-7.3.1-win32-x64)

### Kilo version

7.3.1

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open Kilo Code settings in VS Code
2. Disable an MCP server via the UI toggle
3. Close VS Code and reopen
4. Observe the MCP server is still active / settings reverted

### Screenshot and/or share link

N/A

### Operating System

Windows 11

### Terminal

Windows Terminal / VS Code integrated terminal

### Workaround

Manually editing the config files works:
- MCP settings: `%APPDATA%\Code\User\globalStorage\kilocode.kilo-code\settings\mcp_settings.json`
- Global config: `~/.config/kilo/kilo.json` and `~/.config/kilo/kilo.jsonc`

Adding `"disabled": true` directly in these files correctly disables MCP servers.

### Expected Behavior
- Either a "Save" button should be present, OR
- Changes should be auto-saved and persisted immediately
- Settings should survive VS Code restarts

comments.body: update?
@YasinSalehi06 Still the same. No change.

﻿Adding another concrete reproduction that is likely the same underlying bug — but for the **Providers → Disabled providers** field on the **Providers** tab, not MCP.

### Repro (Kilo Code VS Code extension, English UI)

1. Open Kilo Code sidebar → Settings → **Providers** tab
2. Scroll to the **"Disabled providers"** section ("Providers to hide from the list")
3. Pick a provider in the dropdown, click **Add** — the provider appears in the disabled list and the bottom **"Unsaved changes → Save"** bar shows up
4. Click **Save**
5. **Expected:** the change is persisted to the global config and the list survives a VS Code restart
6. **Actual:** nothing is saved; reopening the settings shows the same provider as enabled again; `~/.config/kilo/kilo.json` does not get a new `disabled_providers` entry

The webview optimistically shows the change as applied, so it really looks like it saved — but it didn't.

### Likely root cause (from reading the code)

`packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/Settings.tsx` `handleSave` blocks the save when any tracked session is in the `busy` state:

```ts
const handleSave = () => {
  const busy = busyCount()
  if (busy === 0) {
    saveConfig()
    return
  }
  showToast({
    variant: "error",
    title: language.t("settings.saveBar.warning.one"),  // "One session is running and will be interrupted"
    actions: [
      { label: language.t("settings.saveBar.saveAnyway"), onClick: saveConfig },
      { label: language.t("settings.saveBar.cancel"), onClick: "dismiss" },
    ],
  })
}
```

When `busyCount() > 0` the bottom **Save** button does **not** call `saveConfig()` at all — it just shows an error-styled toast in the **bottom-right corner of the VS Code window** (not in the settings panel). The toast requires clicking **"Save anyway"** inside it. Most users don't notice the toast, and from the settings panel the click on Save looks like a no-op. The "Unsaved changes" bar stays visible, which reinforces the impression that the save was attempted but silently failed.

Two things that make this very easy to miss for the **Disabled providers** case specifically:
- No chat session needs to be open. A session that was started earlier and is still tracked in the status map (e.g. in an **Open in Tab** Kilo panel) is enough to keep `busyCount() > 0`.
- The Add/Enable flow in `ProvidersTab.tsx` only writes to a local draft, so the user **must** use the global Save bar — they can't bypass it like they can for Connect/Disconnect (which call `client.config.update` directly via `provider-actions.ts`).

If the save does get past `busyCount` and `saveConfig` is invoked, the rest of the chain (extension → `PATCH /config/overlay` → `KilocodeConfigOverlay.overlayUpdate` → `Config.updateGlobal` → `~/.config/kilo/kilo.json`) appears to work correctly, so this is almost certainly the same bug class as the MCP / other-config reports on this issue.

### Suggested fix location

`packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/Settings.tsx` — `handleSave`. Two viable options:

1. **Show the warning in the save bar itself** (⚠ icon + tooltip on the Save button) and still save on click. The destructive `disposeAll` is a real concern, but the user should be able to confirm in one click in the same context.
2. **Disable the global save bar** for the few specific fields that don't require `disposeAll` (e.g. `disabled_providers`, `enabled_providers`, `hide_prompt_training_models`) and auto-save them from the Add/Enable button — similar to how Connect/Disconnect already work.

### Related issues (same root cause)

- #6223 — *Settings update is not possible while the agent is running* (closed as stale, never actually fixed)
- #7670 — *Add a "Save" button to all Settings pages* (workaround for the same UX)
- #9554 — *Autocomplete settings don't participate in the save/cancel bar in Models tab* (variant of the same UI problem)

If a maintainer wants a PR for option 1 or 2 I'm happy to put one together.

title: Settings changes in VS Code UI are not saved/persisted (MCP servers, triggers, and other config)

On Windows, two terminal windows briefly flash (open and immediately close) every time VS Code is opened with a workspace where Kilo Code is enabled.

This occurs **even with all MCP servers disabled** - both local and remote. I tested by:

1. Disabling all 5 local MCP servers (`mssql`, `mirth`, `context-mode`, `docker-mcp`, `fallow`) via `"disabled": true` in `mcp_settings.json`
2. Disabling all 3 remote MCP servers (`web-search`, `web-reader`, `zread`) via `"disabled": true` in both `kilo.json` and `kilo.jsonc`
3. The flashing terminals still appear on every workspace open

### Plugins

VS Code Extension (kilocode.kilo-code-7.3.1-win32-x64)

### Kilo version

7.3.1

### Steps to reproduce

1. Have Kilo Code extension installed in VS Code
2. Disable all MCP servers in settings
3. Close and reopen VS Code
4. Observe two terminal windows flash briefly

### Screenshot and/or share link

N/A

### Operating System

Windows 11

### Terminal

Windows Terminal / VS Code integrated terminal

### Related

- #4453 (similar issue, was reportedly fixed in v7.0.42 with `windowsHide` but the problem persists)
- #4475 (fix for spawning many cmd.exe windows)

body:
## Problem

Backend/CLI requests in the JetBrains plugin have **inconsistent error handling** and **no error reporting**:

1. Several request sites silently swallow errors (log a warning, return empty/null) rather than surfacing them to the user. For example, `abort`, `updateConfig`, `post`, `get`, `messages`, `pendingPermissions`, and `pendingQuestions` in `KiloBackendChatManager` swallow HTTP failures with only a `log.warn(...)`.
2. When errors are propagated (e.g. `prompt`, `compact`, `create`, `rename`), they are wrapped in a raw `RuntimeException` with an HTTP status string — no structured extraction of the user-facing message from the CLI error response body.
3. There is **no telemetry or error reporting** anywhere in the JetBrains plugin (`packages/kilo-jetbrains/`). The VS Code extension reports errors to the metrics collection API; the JetBrains plugin has no equivalent.

Relevant JetBrains code:
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendChatManager.kt` — raw HTTP calls for `prompt`, `abort`, `compact`, `messages`, `updateConfig`, `replyPermission`, `replyQuestion`, etc.
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendSessionManager.kt` — raw HTTP calls for `create`, `rename`, `cloudSessions`, `importCloudSession`
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendAppService.kt` — `FetchResult`-based pattern with `httpDetail()` extraction, used for config/profile/notifications but not chat/session mutations

## Action Items

### 1. Consistent error handling for all backend/CLI requests

For every backend/CLI request in the JetBrains plugin:
- Catch HTTP errors and network exceptions
- Extract a user-friendly message from the CLI error response body (the CLI returns structured JSON errors with a `message` field)
- Display the message appropriately in the UI (notification, inline error, etc.)
- Do not silently swallow errors in user-initiated operations

The existing `httpDetail()` helper in `KiloBackendAppService.kt` (lines ~583–597) is a starting point for extracting messages from `ClientException`/`ServerException`, but it doesn't parse the CLI JSON error body. Extend it to extract the `message` field from the response JSON before falling back to the HTTP status string.

### 2. Report errors to the metrics collection API

The VS Code extension reports errors to the same telemetry pipeline used for all other events:

- **API:** `POST {CLI_URL}/telemetry/capture` with Basic Auth (`kilo:{password}`)
- **Implementation:** `TelemetryProxy` singleton in `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/telemetry/telemetry-proxy.ts`
- **Example error event:**
  ```ts
  TelemetryProxy.capture(TelemetryEventName.AGENT_MANAGER_SESSION_ERROR, {
    source: "vscode",
    error: err,
    context: "createSession",
  })
  ```
  See `packages/kilo-vscode/src/agent-manager/fork-session.ts` lines ~49–57 and `AgentManagerProvider.ts` lines ~748, ~795, ~825, ~1118.
- **Event names:** Defined in `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/telemetry/types.ts` lines ~46–75

Implement an equivalent in the JetBrains plugin:
- Create a `KiloTelemetry` (or similar) service that wraps `POST /telemetry/capture` using the existing authenticated HTTP client
- Call it from error catch blocks in `KiloBackendChatManager`, `KiloBackendSessionManager`, and other request sites
- Respect user telemetry consent (check IntelliJ settings equivalent before sending)
- Include at minimum: event name, source (`"jetbrains"`), error message, and context (which operation failed)

### 3. Application-level error reporting

The VS Code extension does **not** use a crash reporter (no Sentry/Bugsnag). Instead it relies on the same `POST /telemetry/capture` pipeline. VS Code itself handles extension host crashes at the platform level (`packages/kilo-vscode/src/extension.ts` lines ~72–104 set up telemetry consent sync but register no unhandled exception handler).

For the JetBrains plugin, consider:
- Registering a global uncaught exception handler (or IntelliJ `IdeEventQueue` listener) that fires a telemetry event for unexpected exceptions in the plugin's coroutine scopes
- Using IntelliJ's `PluginException`-aware error reporting (override `ErrorReportSubmitter`) to forward crash reports to the same `POST /telemetry/capture` endpoint
- At minimum, ensure all coroutine `CoroutineExceptionHandler`s log + report instead of silently dying

## References

**VS Code — error reporting:**
- `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/telemetry/telemetry-proxy.ts` — `TelemetryProxy.capture()` + `POST /telemetry/capture`
- `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/telemetry/types.ts:46` — error-specific `TelemetryEventName` enum values
- `packages/kilo-vscode/src/services/telemetry/errors.ts` — typed error classes with structured property extraction
- `packages/kilo-vscode/src/agent-manager/fork-session.ts:49` — error capture with context

**JetBrains — backend request handling:**
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendChatManager.kt` — mixed error patterns (throw vs. swallow)
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendSessionManager.kt` — raw HTTP for session mutations
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/app/KiloBackendAppService.kt:583` — `httpDetail()` / `FetchResult` pattern (model to follow)
- `packages/kilo-jetbrains/backend/src/main/kotlin/ai/kilocode/backend/cli/KiloBackendHttpClients.kt` — OkHttp client factory with Basic Auth
title: Handle CLI errors in backend requests + add error reporting

body:
## Context

There is a latent behavior mismatch in `GET /config/providers` between the legacy Hono route and the Effect HttpApi route.

This issue is created to track it, and address if needed once the move to Effect is completed

## Current behavior

- The legacy route in `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/config.ts` computes `Provider.defaultModelIDs(providers)` and then overrides `default.kilo` with the Kilo API default from `fetchDefaultModel(token, organizationId)` when that model exists locally.
- The Effect handler in `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/httpapi/handlers/config.ts` only returns `Provider.defaultModelIDs(providers)` and does not preserve the Kilo-specific cloud default override.
- `/config/providers` is routed through Effect HttpApi whenever `KILO_EXPERIMENTAL_HTTPAPI` is active.


## Expected behavior

The Effect HttpApi implementation should preserve the same Kilo cloud default-model semantics as the legacy Hono implementation.


## Relevant references

- `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/config.ts`
- `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/httpapi/handlers/config.ts`
- `packages/opencode/src/server/routes/instance/index.ts`
- `packages/core/src/flag/flag.ts`
- `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/context/sync.tsx`
- `packages/opencode/src/cli/cmd/tui/context/local.tsx`
title: Preserve Kilo cloud defaults when config providers moves to Effect HttpApi

body:
### Description

Error when installing both latest release (v7.3.1) and from main branch, on NixOS. Excerpt from error:

```
       > Running phase: buildPhase
       > 12 |
       > 13 | // relax version requirement
       > 14 | const expectedBunVersionRange = `^${expectedBunVersion}`
       > 15 |
       > 16 | if (!semver.satisfies(process.versions.bun, expectedBunVersionRange)) {
       > 17 |   throw new Error(`This script requires bun@${expectedBunVersionRange}, but you are using bun@${process.versions.bun}`)
       >                  ^
       > error: This script requires bun@^1.3.13, but you are using bun@1.3.11
       >       at /build/source/packages/script/src/index.ts:17:13
       > 
       > Bun v1.3.11 (Linux x64)
```

I see a previous [commit](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/9643/changes) that attempted to resolve the issue, but apparently it didn't work.

I used AI to fix it, the commit can be found [here](https://github.com/eblanshey/kilocode/commit/fddf082d49bef3e9a422b68da87a858a64683b6c). I'm new to Nix so am not confident that it's the best solution, so I won't open a PR unless you want it. Here's the AI explanation of the problem:

<details>
<summary>Click to expand AI slop</summary>
The Nix flake pinned Bun `1.3.13`, but only inside `devShells.default`. The actual package build used `pkgs.callPackage ./nix/kilo.nix` and `./nix/node_modules.nix`, so their `bun` argument still came from pinned `nixpkgs`, where `pkgs.bun` is `1.3.11`.

That made the package build fail because Kilo’s build script checks `package.json` and requires `bun@^1.3.13`.

Fix:
- Hoisted the custom `bun-1.3.13` derivation into shared flake scope.
- Passed that pinned Bun explicitly into both `nix/node_modules.nix` and `nix/kilo.nix`.
- Kept the dev shell using the same pinned Bun.

There was also a second Nix packaging issue after that: the build script patched the generated Linux binary to use `/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2`, which does not exist in the Nix sandbox. The fix adds `KILO_LINUX_INTERPRETER` for Nix package builds so the smoke test uses the Nix store dynamic linker, while normal release builds keep the existing Linux loader paths.
</details>

## Summary
Users need a way to better organize permissions in the Auto-Approve tab. As the list of approved permissions grows, it becomes harder to manage without any organizational tools.

## Proposed Improvements
- **Sorting**: Allow users to sort permissions (e.g., alphabetically, by type, by date added)
- **Filtering**: Allow users to filter the list to find specific permissions quickly
- **Grouping**: Allow users to group permissions by category or type for better organization

## Motivation
When many permissions are auto-approved, the flat unordered list becomes unwieldy. Sorting, filtering, and grouping would make it much easier to review and manage what has been approved.
title: Auto-Approve tab: add sorting, filtering, and grouping for permissions

body:
## Summary
When commands are displayed in the permissions/auto-approve UI, the full command string should always be visible as long as it is not unreasonably long.

## Proposed Behavior
- Always show the full command text as an option in the UI
- Define a reasonable maximum length threshold — commands under that threshold should always display in full without truncation
- Commands exceeding the threshold can still be truncated with an expand option

## Motivation
Truncating short-to-medium length commands makes it harder for users to know exactly what they are approving. Showing the full command builds trust and clarity, especially for security-sensitive operations.
title: Auto-Approve: show full command text for commands up to a reasonable length

body:
## Summary
When Kilo suggests commands, the full command should always be available as an option, as long as it is not unreasonably long.

## Motivation
Currently, command suggestions may truncate or omit the full command, which can be confusing for users who want to see and select the exact command. Users should always have the option to view and choose the complete command string.

## Proposed Behavior
- Always include the full command as a selectable option in command suggestions
- Apply a reasonable length threshold — if the command exceeds this threshold, it may be omitted or truncated
- This ensures users are never left without the ability to run the exact intended command

## Additional Context
Raised by community feedback: users expect to always see the full command as an option, unless it is unreasonably long.
title: Always show full command as an option in command suggestions (up to a reasonable length)

body:
## Summary

Selecting `nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free` (via the `kilo` provider / Kilo Cloud gateway) causes Kilo Code to enter an infinite loop where the model emits junk/malformed XML for every tool call and never produces a valid tool invocation. The task never advances.

This was previously reported in [#7323](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/7323) ("Mimo V2 Pro or Omni has not sent any assistant messages") and auto-closed during the legacy-extension transition with a request to refile. This is the fresh issue.

## Environment

- Kilo Code extension: **v7.3.0** (win32-x64)
- VS Code: 1.105.x
- OS: Windows 11
- Model: `nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free`
- Provider: `kilo` (Kilo Cloud gateway)

## Steps to reproduce

1. Set default model to `nvidia/nemotron-3-nano-omni-30b-a3b-reasoning:free` (provider `kilo`)
2. Open a chat in any workspace
3. Ask for any operation that would require a tool call (e.g. "read file X" or "run a shell command")
4. Observe the assistant looping — emitting incomplete / malformed XML and never invoking a valid tool

## Expected

Either the model produces valid tool calls, or Kilo Code surfaces a clear error and stops after N parse failures instead of looping.

## Actual

Infinite loop of malformed XML output. No tool calls ever succeed. Tokens are burned indefinitely until the user stops the task.

## Related

- Same model behaves identically in another agent framework: [block/goose#6461](https://github.com/block/goose/issues/6461) — they worked around it by marking the model flaky and adding a 55s internal timeout (PRs #6638 and #8837).
- Text-only Nemotrons (`nvidia/nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b:free` etc.) work correctly in Kilo Code — the failure appears specific to the Omni / multimodal variant.

## Suggested fixes

Either of:

1. **Filter the model from the catalog** for the `kilo` provider since it does not produce valid tool calls in Kilo Code's XML format.
2. **Add a parse-failure ceiling** — after N consecutive malformed tool-call responses from any model, abort the task with an error instead of retrying indefinitely (this would also prevent token burn for any future incompatible model).
3. **If native function calling is on the roadmap for this model class**, gate the Omni model behind that path and disallow the XML fallback.

Option 2 is the broadest fix and protects users from this class of bug going forward.
title: Nemotron 3 Nano Omni 30B (free) infinite loop with malformed XML tool calls

body: ### Description **This only happens when approving changes involving many files at once — single-file edits work fine.** When the agent requests permission to edit/write **multiple files** in a single operation (e.g., 10+ files), the permission dock at the bottom of the chat panel shows a long list of file paths and diffs. The scrollable area inside the dock does not extend properly — the action buttons ("Run", "Allow Once", "Deny", and the shield auto-approve toggle) are pushed below the visible area and cannot be reached. Scrolling the permission dock does not bring the buttons into view. With a single file edit, the buttons are always visible and clickable. The problem only appears when the file list is long enough to push the buttons out of the scroll container. This is a recent regression in the last few versions. The buttons are effectively blocked behind the overflowing file list, making it impossible to approve or deny the request. The only workaround is to collapse individual file diffs or use keyboard shortcuts (if available). ### Plugins Kilo Code VS Code Extension ### Kilo version 7.3.0 ### Steps to reproduce 1. Set edit/write tool permissions to "Ask". 2. Ask the agent to perform a task that modifies **many files at once** (e.g., refactor a large component, rename across multiple files, or apply a bulk change). 3. Wait for the permission prompt to appear at the bottom of the chat panel. 4. Observe that the file list takes up most of the permission dock space. 5. Try to scroll down to the "Run" / "Allow Once" / "Deny" buttons — scrolling either does nothing or cannot scroll far enough. 6. The buttons remain hidden below the visible area, making the request stuck. 7. (Optional) Try a single-file edit to confirm the buttons appear correctly in that case. ### Screenshot and/or share link ### Operating System win11 ### Terminal _No response_

### Description

I have two main providers set up, NIM and DeepSeek's official API. I set the model from NIM as the default model (GLM 5.1, NIM provider) for project A in directory A, then secondly open a new VS code window in a separate project directory for project B (directory B). Project B uses the DeepSeek provider while project A uses the NIM provider. Both VS code windows are open simultaneously, each managing their own project, each using kilocode but Project A uses NIM, project B uses DeepSeek.

When I reopen VS code in Project A's directory after a reboot, the default model has now been swapped to the DeepSeek model from Project B instead of the one I have been using in Project A (GLm 5.1 from NIM). Especially If the reboot occurs while both VS Code windows are actively open (power loss, hard shutdown, crashes, etc).


### Plugins

KiloCode

### Kilo version

 7.3.0

### Steps to reproduce

1. Set default model to anything
2. Open VS Code via right click in project 1 directory
3. Use the model set as default in KiloCode's settings from the desired provider set as default in project 1
4. While the project 1 vs code window is open, open a _second_ VS Code Window via right click in project directory 2, leave BOTH open simultaneously.
5. Project 2s vs code window should default to the model set as default in KiloCode's settings when opened.
6. Switch the provider to another saved provider (deepseek in my case) in project 2s vs code window, *while* agents in the project one directory are actively modifying files (Critical, provider change in project 2s vs code window MUST be opened while project 1s vs code window is running and the original default provider is selected in project 1)
7. In project 2s vs code window, switch the active provider to the secondary provider desired (DeepSeek in my case)
8. Task project 2s agent to do a task
9. Close both VS code windows for project 1 AND project 2 simultaneously (Either kill VS Code entirely in task manager or just reboot while they're actively working on tasks).
10. Reopen vs code in project 1's directory
11. Default model should still be set to project 2s provider instead of the model that was selected previously in KiloCode in project 1 before VS Code was closed

Desired behavior:

ALWAYS retain the model/provider settings per project, selecting another provider in a different project should **NEVER** change the provider that was set in a separate project. Swapping providers for a specific project should never affect any other projects active provider. KiloCode should remember the last used model/provider on a per project basis.

Changing providers in another project should never effect what the provider is set to in any other project under any circumstance.

### Operating System

Windows 11 26H2

### Terminal

Powershell
title: Bug: KiloCode does not retain provider/model settings on a per project basis

body:
### Description

Custom modes can't use the question tool unless question is specifically allowed.

This is undocumented, and a very non-obvious bug.

At the very least, the default permissions assigned to custom agents should be documented.

But it would be better to have some sane defaults for custom agents that permit basic tool usage like `question`

This can be easily verified with kilo debug agent `mode`

### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

7.3.0

### Steps to reproduce

1. make a custom mode
2. leave question permission unspecified
3. ask the new mode to ask you whether you like coffee or tea using the `question` tool
4. no such tool exists

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Windows 11 and Gentoo (via WSL 2)

### Terminal

Windows Terminal

comments.body: @kilocode-bot What could be causing this?

Here's what's causing the issue:

## Root Cause

In `packages/opencode/src/agent/agent.ts`, `baseDefaults` explicitly sets `question: "deny"`. Built-in modes like `build` and `plan` override this back to `"allow"`, but **custom user-defined modes don't get this override** — they just inherit the deny default.

So unless a custom mode explicitly sets `permission: { question: "allow" }`, the `question` tool gets filtered out entirely before the LLM sees it — making it appear as if the tool doesn't exist.

## The Fix

When creating a custom agent, merge in `{ question: "allow" }` the same way `build` and `plan` do. Would you like me to open a PR with that fix?

title: question tool not allowed by default for custom modes/agents
