title: [Feature] Add Enable/Disable Toggle and Edit Functionality for Rules and Skills

body:
## Summary

Once JB-04 exposes the cloud session API surface, this issue delivers the actual user-facing flow: browsing cloud sessions, previewing one read-only, and continuing it into a local session on first message.

## What to build

- **Cloud sessions tab** in the History view — paginated list of cloud sessions with title, date, and optional repo/branch filter
- **Read-only preview mode** — selecting a cloud session loads its messages in the Chat view in a read-only state; a banner indicates it is a cloud preview and prompts to continue
- **Continue/import on first prompt** — when the user types a message in the preview, the cloud session is imported to local storage first, then the message is sent on the new local session
- Loading, error, and empty states for all three stages
- Repository URL filter pre-populated from the current project's git remote where available

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Users can see their cloud sessions in the History view
- [ ] Selecting a cloud session shows the transcript in read-only mode with a continue prompt
- [ ] Sending a first message imports the session and continues it as a local session transparently
- [ ] The user is not prompted to "import" manually — the import is automatic on first send
- [ ] Pagination loads more cloud sessions on scroll

## Context

- VS Code cloud list UI: `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/history/CloudSessionList.tsx`
- VS Code import+send handler: `packages/kilo-vscode/src/kilo-provider/handlers/cloud-session.ts`
- Session context — `selectCloudSession`, `selectCloudSession`: `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/context/session.tsx`

## Dependencies

Depends on JB-01 (tool window shell) and JB-04 (cloud session RPC).

comments.body:
Really nice direction overall — the new cloud session workflow already looks very promising.
One related feature that would be very useful here is cloud session cleanup / deletion.
Currently, local sessions can be deleted, but cloud sessions remain permanently visible in the Cloud Sessions UI.
It would be great to support:

- delete cloud sessions
- archive/hide cloud sessions
- optional bulk cleanup for old sessions

Especially useful for temporary/test sessions to reduce dashboard clutter.

title: Cloud session browsing, preview, and continue flow

body: ### Feature hasn't been suggested before. - [ ] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before. ### Describe the enhancement you want to request Hi. In previous versions of the Kilo Code VS Code extension, it was possible to choose a specific underlying provider for a model when using Kilo Gateway. For example, for the same model I could decide whether it should route through providers such as Fireworks or Together. After the recent major update, this option seems to be gone from the new UI. This was a very useful feature, because the same model can differ significantly depending on the provider behind the gateway. For example: different quantization formats, such as FP4 vs FP16 different context window sizes, such as 80k vs 250k potentially different performance, latency, reliability, and cost characteristics Right now, when selecting a model through Kilo Gateway, there does not seem to be a way to explicitly choose the underlying provider for that model. Requested improvement Please bring back the ability to select the underlying provider for a specific model when using Kilo Gateway (similar to how it worked in earlier versions). Example use case For GLM 5 (visit openrouter model page), the default routed provider may expose only 80K context and 131.1K max output, while another available provider may be slightly more expensive but also much faster and support 202.8K context and 202.8K max output. In cases like this, users should be able to explicitly choose the provider instead of relying only on the default gateway routing. I’ve attached a screenshot from **the previous UI for reference**. It shows that Kilo Gateway previously allowed selecting the underlying provider for the chosen model. Thanks!

body:
### Problem

Approval fatigue in agentic coding sessions is real: Anthropic reports that Claude Code users approve 93% of permission prompts ([source](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode)). Today Kilo CLI has two blunt settings: manual prompts on every risky tool call, or `POST /permission/allow-everything` which disables all guardrails. There is no middle ground — nothing looks at a bash command and decides \"this one is safe, don't bother the user.\"

The **legacy kilocode extension had this feature** and called it the **AI Gatekeeper**. On March 25, 2026 Anthropic shipped a conceptually identical (but more sophisticated) feature called **\"auto mode\"** in Claude Code. We should bring the gatekeeper back, and use Claude Code's design as the template since they solved several problems the legacy version left open (prompt injection, false-positive recovery, overeager-action blocking vs. pure blocklists).

### How Claude Code \"auto mode\" works

From [the Anthropic writeup](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode) and the [permissions docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/permissions#configure-the-auto-mode-classifier):

**Two-layer defense:**

1. **Input layer — prompt-injection probe.** Server-side probe scans tool outputs (file reads, web fetches, shell output, MCP results) before they enter the agent's context. When content looks like an injection attempt it prepends a warning telling the agent to treat the content as suspect.
2. **Output layer — transcript classifier.** Runs on Sonnet 4.6. Evaluates each tool call before it executes. **Two stages:** a fast single-token yes/no filter tuned to err on the side of blocking (8.5% FPR, 6.6% FNR on real traffic), followed by a chain-of-thought stage that runs only on flagged actions (0.4% FPR, 17% FNR end-to-end).

**Tiered short-circuiting (most actions never hit the classifier):**

- **Tier 1**: built-in safe-tool allowlist (reads, grep, glob, todo, plan) + user allow rules. On entering auto mode, blanket shell rules and wildcarded script interpreters (`python`, `node`, `ruby`, `npm run *`) are dropped — the whole point of the classifier is to see those.
- **Tier 2**: in-project file edits are allowed without classifier call (reviewable via git).
- **Tier 3**: classifier only sees shell commands, web fetches, external tool integrations, subagent spawns, out-of-project filesystem operations.

**Reasoning-blind classifier.** The classifier only sees user messages and bare tool-call payloads. Assistant prose and tool outputs are stripped so the agent cannot talk the classifier into a bad decision (\"this is safe because the user implicitly approved it earlier\"). Also makes prompt injection structurally harder since the classifier never reads poisoned content.

**Customizable classifier policy — three prose slots:**

- `autoMode.environment` — prose descriptions of trusted infrastructure (GitHub orgs, S3 buckets, internal domains). Anything outside is treated as potential exfiltration.
- `autoMode.allow` — exceptions to block rules (e.g., \"deploying to staging namespace is allowed\").
- `autoMode.soft_deny` — block rules beyond the defaults (20+ ship out of the box, covering destroy/exfiltrate, degrade security posture, cross trust boundaries, bypass review).

Explicit user intent overrides `soft_deny` — if the user's message directly describes the action (\"force-push this branch\"), it's allowed even if a block rule matches.

**Deny-and-continue semantics.** Denial is returned to the agent as a tool result with instruction to \"find a safer path, don't route around.\" **3 consecutive or 20 total denials** in a session escalate to the human (or terminate in headless mode). This makes false positives survivable — without it a 0.4% FPR would be a serious usability problem over long runs.

**CLI surface**: `claude auto-mode defaults` / `config` / `critique`.

### How legacy kilocode \"gatekeeper\" worked

Full source: `kilocode-legacy/src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/gatekeeper.ts` (388 LOC). Ran only when `yoloMode` was enabled and `yoloGatekeeperApiConfigId` pointed to an API profile.

**Flow (`evaluateGatekeeperApproval`, lines 19-140):**

1. Load gatekeeper model profile from user's configured API configs.
2. Build a prompt containing `toolName` + params + workspace dir + git repo path + per-file git tracked/untracked status for paths extracted from bash commands (`extractFilePathsFromCommand`, lines 188-230).
3. Stream response from the chosen model. Parse first word — `yes`/`approve`/`allow` → approved, else denied.
4. Emit `🛡️ Gatekeeper ✅ approved **toolName** ($0.0002)` chat message (lines 123-131).
5. **On any error: default to approve.** (line 138). This is a significant safety gap vs. Claude Code which fails closed.

**Pre-approved tools (bypass the model entirely, lines 285-292):** `read_file`, `list_files`, `search_files`, `codebase_search`, `list_code_definition_names`, `update_todo_list`.

**System prompt (lines 310-381):** ~70 lines of hand-written rules split into 7 \"core principles\":
1. Read operations safe.
2. Task management (todos) safe.
3. Writes within workspace generally safe.
4. Deletion depends on git recoverability.
5. Commands evaluated by intent and scope (`ls`, `cat`, `git status` safe; `sudo` denied; `/etc`, `/usr`, `/bin` denied).
6. MCP tools: read-only safe, writes evaluated.
7. System integrity paramount — no sudo, no system dirs, no public network exposure.

**Wire-up:**

- `kilocode-legacy/src/core/assistant-message/presentAssistantMessage.ts:697-712` — YOLO short-circuit calls `evaluateGatekeeperApproval` before auto-approving.
- Settings: `yoloGatekeeperApiConfigId: z.string().optional()` at `packages/types/src/global-settings.ts:77`.
- UI: `AutoApproveSettings.tsx:464-503` — \"No gatekeeper (approve all)\" select populated from `listApiConfigMeta`.
- Tests: `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/__tests__/gatekeeper.test.ts` (~690 lines).

### Current Kilo CLI state

- `packages/opencode/src/permission/index.ts` — rule-based permissions `{permission, pattern, action}`; no LLM evaluation.
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/routes.ts` — `POST /permission/allow-everything` wildcard approve, no guardrails.
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/config-paths.ts` — forces `ask` on edits to `.kilo/`, `.opencode/`, `AGENTS.md`, etc.
- TUI command `Enable auto-approve mode` at `src/kilocode/cli/cmd/tui/app.tsx:156-176`.
- VS Code settings UI at `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/AutoApproveTab.tsx` — static allow/ask/deny matrix.

No feature evaluates tool calls with a model. Legacy migration (`packages/kilo-vscode/src/legacy-migration/migration-service.ts:589-722`) drops `yoloGatekeeperApiConfigId` silently.

### Requirements

#### 1. Classifier hook in the permission pipeline

Hook into `Permission.ask` (`packages/opencode/src/permission/index.ts:190-239`) so that, when a rule would auto-approve a call and the classifier is enabled, it first consults the classifier. Three possible outcomes:

- Classifier approves → proceed silently as today.
- Classifier denies → return a tool error to the agent with the denial reason (deny-and-continue). Do not prompt the human.
- Classifier errors → **fail closed (fall back to `ask`)**, not open. This diverges from the legacy gatekeeper which approved on error, following Claude Code's model.

Applies to `bash`, `webfetch`, `external_directory`, MCP tools, subagent spawns, edits outside the project. Does not apply to the built-in safe-tool allowlist (`read`, `grep`, `glob`, `list`, `codesearch`, `websearch`, `todoread`) or to in-project file edits (short-circuit before classifier call, matching Claude Code's Tier 1/2).

#### 2. Two-stage classifier

Copy Claude Code's design verbatim:

- **Stage 1**: fast single-token yes/no on a small model (e.g., Haiku / `claude-haiku-4.6` or the gatekeeper model of the user's choice). Prompt ends with \"err on the side of blocking.\" Emits a single token.
- **Stage 2**: runs only on stage-1 blocks. Same prompt template with a different final instruction asking for chain-of-thought reasoning. Most tokens are prompt-cache hits from stage 1.

Single-stage is acceptable as v1 if two-stage adds too much scope; the config shape should be forward-compatible.

#### 3. Reasoning-blind inputs

The classifier sees only:
- User messages from the conversation.
- Bare tool-call payload (name + params).

It does **not** see assistant prose or previous tool results. This is both a prompt-injection defense (tool results are where hostile content enters) and an anti-rationalization defense (agent can't talk the classifier into a bad call). Legacy gatekeeper did not do this and should not be copied blindly.

#### 4. Configurable policy — three prose slots

New config block `permission.classifier` with:

- `model` — string, e.g., `claude-haiku-4.6` or a user-defined provider/model.
- `environment` — string[] — natural-language description of trusted infra. Defaults to \"the current working directory and git remotes.\"
- `allow` — string[] — exceptions to the block rules. Ships with conservative defaults (install packages declared in manifests, push to session working branch, standard credential flows).
- `soft_deny` — string[] — block rules. Port and adapt Claude Code's 20+ default rules covering destroy/exfiltrate, degrade security posture, cross trust boundaries, bypass review.

Replacing `allow` or `soft_deny` replaces the whole list (copy-the-default-then-edit model, not merge). Surface a `kilo classifier defaults` / `config` / `critique` set of commands so users can inspect and validate.

#### 5. Deny-and-continue with escalation backstop

- A classifier denial returns as a tool error the agent can react to, with the reason text. Do **not** halt the session.
- Track `consecutiveClassifierDenials` and `totalClassifierDenials` on the `Session`. At **3 consecutive** or **20 total** denials, halt and escalate to the human (or in headless `kilo run` mode, terminate with non-zero exit and clear error).
- Counters reset on any user turn.

#### 6. Tiered short-circuit

Before calling the classifier:

- **Safe-tool allowlist** (hardcoded): `read`, `grep`, `glob`, `list`, `codesearch`, `websearch`, `todoread`, `todowrite`, `lsp`. These already short-circuit in many cases; formalize the list.
- **In-project edits**: `edit` operations targeting paths inside the workspace root (excluding the existing `config-paths.ts` protected set) skip the classifier. Reviewable via git.
- **On enabling classifier mode**: drop blanket allow rules known to grant arbitrary code execution — `bash: *: allow`, wildcarded interpreter rules (`python *`, `node *`, `bun *`, `npm run *`, etc.), and `task` rules without specifiers. Narrow rules (\"allow running prettier\") carry over. This prevents users from bypassing the classifier via pre-existing broad allow rules.

#### 7. Prompt-injection probe (phase-2)

Server-side probe that scans tool output before it enters the agent's context. When content looks like injection (\"ignore previous instructions,\" curl-pipe-to-bash patterns, etc.) prepend a warning to the tool result. Can be its own small classifier call or a fast heuristic pass first.

Phase-2 because the transcript classifier alone already closes the highest-impact attack paths; the probe is defense in depth.

#### 8. UI surface

- VS Code sidebar: the auto-approve indicator bar (tracked separately in other issues) should surface classifier state — off / enabled / paused — with model name visible on hover.
- Classifier denials and approvals should be marked inline in the conversation similar to legacy's `🛡️ Gatekeeper ✅ approved **toolName** ($0.0002)` message, including model cost per evaluation.
- TUI: `/classifier` command to view status, toggle, and see recent denials.

#### 9. Migration

Update `packages/kilo-vscode/src/legacy-migration/migration-service.ts:589-722` to:

- Map `yoloMode: true` + `yoloGatekeeperApiConfigId: <id>` → `permission.classifier = { model: <resolved model>, environment: [...defaults] }`.
- Map `yoloMode: true` with no gatekeeper → warn the user that their old behavior (approve all on error) is no longer default, and offer to either enable the classifier with a default model or fall back to `allowEverything` global rule.

### Out of scope

- Making the classifier decision authoritative (overriding explicit user `deny` rules). User config always wins. Classifier only gates what would otherwise auto-approve.
- Porting the legacy `yoloMode` toggle itself. This issue is about the classifier, not about bulk auto-approve with no guardrails — that already exists via `allowEverything`.
- Re-implementing Claude Code's server-side prompt-injection probe exactly. We can start with a local heuristic / small model pass.
- Custom model plumbing. Reuse the existing provider/model selection infrastructure used by `agent.model`.
- Loop/cost caps and the quick-toggle indicator bar — separate concerns that benefit from classifier mode but don't depend on it.

### References

**Claude Code auto mode:**

- https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode (Mar 25, 2026)
- https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/permissions#configure-the-auto-mode-classifier

**Legacy kilocode gatekeeper (`/Users/marius/Documents/git/kilocode-legacy/`):**

- `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/gatekeeper.ts:19,72-140` — `evaluateGatekeeperApproval`
- `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/gatekeeper.ts:239-387` — `buildGatekeeperPrompt` + system prompt
- `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/gatekeeper.ts:285-292` — pre-approved read-only tools
- `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/gatekeeper.ts:147-180` — git-tracked detection for bash file paths
- `src/core/assistant-message/presentAssistantMessage.ts:697-712` — wire-up under YOLO short-circuit
- `src/core/assistant-message/kilocode/__tests__/gatekeeper.test.ts` — test coverage patterns
- `packages/types/src/global-settings.ts:77` — setting schema
- `webview-ui/src/components/settings/AutoApproveSettings.tsx:464-503` — gatekeeper model picker UI

**Current Kilo CLI:**

- `packages/opencode/src/permission/index.ts:190-239,353-396` — `Permission.ask`, `allowEverything`
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/routes.ts:14-82` — HTTP route
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/permission/config-paths.ts` — protected paths
- `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/cli/cmd/tui/app.tsx:156-176` — TUI auto-approve command
- `packages/kilo-vscode/webview-ui/src/components/settings/AutoApproveTab.tsx` — settings matrix
- `packages/kilo-vscode/src/legacy-migration/migration-service.ts:589-722` — existing migration

comments.body: Seems to be a duplicate of #7684, although this is a much more extensive plan writeup.
Feel free to assign this issue to me. As discussed in Discord, I'm planning to work on implementing this. Since this is a large feature, I'll try to PR it in small chunks behind a feature flag.

+1 to this. This is exactly the missing middle ground between constant permission prompts and unsafe YOLO/allow-everything behavior.

One additional reference worth considering is Codex’s guardian/auto-review implementation:

- https://github.com/openai/codex/tree/main/codex-rs/core/src/guardian
- https://github.com/openai/codex/blob/main/codex-rs/core/src/guardian/policy.md
- https://github.com/openai/codex/blob/main/codex-rs/core/src/guardian/policy_template.md
- https://github.com/openai/codex/blob/main/codex-rs/core/src/guardian/review.rs
- https://github.com/openai/codex/blob/main/codex-rs/core/src/guardian/review_session.rs

The part I’d especially like to see in Kilo is a harness-side reviewer that considers the current session/chat context, including permissions explicitly granted by the direct user earlier in the session, while still refusing to treat tool output, web content, file content, or prompt-injection text as permission grants.

The current static rule-map approach helps, but it becomes hard to maintain safely once users are hand-curating long allow/deny lists with token-matching edge cases, shell differences, and injection-specific patterns. A reviewer/classifier layer would make routine, clearly authorized work much less click-heavy while keeping hard safety tripwires in place.

Ideally this should apply to the full permission surface, not only bash commands: shell, file edits/writes, webfetch/network access, MCP tools, subagent/task spawns, and external-directory access.

I’d also like the reviewer/classifier model to be configurable independently from the main coding model. For local-first setups, that means I could run the main agent on one model while pointing the approval reviewer at another provider, including a local/OpenAI-compatible endpoint on another machine, e.g. `http://192.168.x.x:port/v1`, or a frontier API model if preferred. The reviewer should not have to be the same model/provider as the working agent.

Useful direction. I would be careful to keep the classifier decision bound to the exact action context, not just the command string.

For example: command, cwd, env boundary, files/globs touched if known, MCP server/tool identity for non-shell tools, and whether the step is read-only or state-changing. Otherwise a learned allow rule can drift from `git status` into a nearby mutating command.

A durable decision object like `{action, context_hash, decision: allow|ask|block, reason, policy_version}` would also make retries and user reports easier without prompting on every safe command.

@marius-kilocode  Opened a draft PR taking a first pass at this: #11619.

Approach (following the auto-mode design discussed here):
- Pluggable `ClassifierProvider`; default uses the user's configured model via the AI SDK (single-pass `<block>yes/no`).
- Hooks `Permission.ask` **only on the would-auto-approve path**: block → deny-and-continue (`ClassifierDeniedError`, surfaces as a tool error, no halt); classifier error/escalation → **fail closed** (human `ask`). Never overrides an explicit user `deny`/`ask`.
- Reasoning-blind transcript (user text + assistant tool calls only), safe-tool allowlist short-circuit, per-session denial counters (3-consecutive / 20-total, reset each user turn), and a `classifier` config block with copy-then-edit `environment`/`allow`/`soft_deny` slots.

Deferred to follow-ups: the local/SaaS HTTP backends, the phase-2 input-layer injection probe, "drop blanket-allow rules on enable", VS Code/TUI surfaces, and the legacy `yoloGatekeeperApiConfigId` migration.

Marked draft — opening for early feedback on the integration approach (notably: gating inside `Permission.ask` vs. at the `session/tools.ts` call site).
@thomaslwang thanks! @shssoichiro had some work going in this direction as well with https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/10267
We would need to find a nice way to integrate it into the auto-approve functionality. We are about to ship sandboxing, so I am not sure yet how we best fit gatekeeper in there, yet.

title: LLM-based bash command auto-approval (inspired by Claude Code "auto mode" / legacy kilo "gatekeeper")

body:
Part of #11538.

## Goal

Extend the centralized agent sandbox to persistent model-originated process surfaces: local stdio MCP servers and `background_process`.

## Current behavior

- Shell and transient formatter processes inherit the call-local profile through the shared process capability.
- `background_process` start/restart is rejected while sandboxing is active because the process outlives the tool-call context.
- Local stdio MCP servers start before individual MCP tool calls and are currently trusted/unconfined.
- Remote MCP is delegated remote authority and cannot be locally OS-confined.

## Required design

Add a persistent sandbox lease that snapshots the normalized profile, prepares the process through the selected backend, and keeps backend resources alive until process termination.

- `background_process` stores the lease/profile with the tracked process and reuses it on restart
- local stdio MCP either receives an instance-level profile or is keyed by the profile/workspace so unrelated sessions do not share authority
- termination closes helper processes, temporary policy files, proxy state, and the complete child tree
- strict mode rejects local MCP configurations that cannot be confined
- remote MCP trust is explicit in configuration and UI

## Acceptance

- Background start/restart works under the active sandbox and cannot write outside its captured scope
- Local stdio MCP child processes receive equivalent filesystem/environment/network policy
- Profiles do not leak between concurrent worktrees or sessions
- Stopping/disconnecting closes the complete sandbox lease
- Remote MCP and trusted plugin limitations are documented and surfaced

## Prior art

- https://github.com/anthropic-experimental/sandbox-runtime

### Question

I’ve recently run into an issue while using KiloCode and would appreciate some help.

I have a workspace in VS Code that contains two folders:

one for a Node.js project,
one for a Python project,

Previously, when writing queries, I was able to reference files from both folders using @.

However, recently when I type @ followed by a few letters, I only get file suggestions from one folder instead of both.

My questions:

Why did I lose access to files in the other folder?,
Is this expected behavior or a bug?,
Is there any way to configure KiloCode so it indexes or references both folders again?,

For context, I also asked KiloCode about this, and it responded:

"The working directory appears to be hard-coded in Kilo's VS Code extension. There's no obvious config option to change it."

Has anyone else faced this issue or found a workaround?

* Clipboard API is functional in JCEF
* But paste/drop events are not delivered to the plugin UI

Kilo Code for INtellij IDEA

5.12.0

2. Copy any text from system (e.g., browser or editor outside IDE)

body: ### Description The **Code Reviewer (Kilo Platform — not the VS Code extension)** agent silently passes with zero analysis on PRs targeting the `master` branch. The agent initializes (system prompt appears in session log) but never executes any tool calls (no file reads, no diff analysis, no `gh` CLI calls) and posts zero comments on the PR. It marks the review as "Completed" with a green checkmark, creating a false positive in CI. PRs targeting feature branches (non-master) continue to work correctly in the same repo, on the same day, with the same BYOK API key. I have also tried reconnecting the GitHub integration multiple times — same result. **API key config:** BYOK (OpenAI). Model on working runs: `gpt-5.3-codex`. #### Timeline | Time (UTC) | Event | |------------|-------| | March 5 – April 14 | Code Reviewer worked correctly on 30+ PRs. Inline comments, review summaries, token counts visible. | | April 15, 04:57 | **Last working review** — PR targeting a feature branch. 31 files, ~2 min session, 288K tokens in, 10+ tool calls, 2 inline comments. | | April 15, 05:21 | **First broken review** — PR targeting master (171 files, +17K lines). 4 retry attempts over 17 min (05:21, 05:26, 05:30, 05:37). Each logs only the system prompt then exits. Zero tool calls, zero comments. Marked "Completed." | | April 15, 10:11 | Second broken review — small PR targeting master (4 files). 8-second session, same failure pattern. | | April 15, 10:23 | Third attempt after removing all custom instructions. Same failure. | If a platform update was deployed between 04:57 and 05:21 UTC on April 15, 2026, it may be the cause. #### Key observations 1. Broken runs show **no Model or Tokens field** in the review details panel — the LLM call never initiated 2. Working run shows Model: gpt-5.3-codex and token counts (288K in / 5K out) 3. Removed custom instructions and re-triggered — same failure (custom instructions are not the cause) 4. .kilo/review-config.json has no branch filters — configured for all pull_request events 5. The agent registers a **passing CI status check despite zero analysis** — undermines CI trust 6. BYOK API key is valid (confirmed by the working run at 04:57 using the same key) ### Plugins N/A — this is the Kilo Platform Code Reviewer, not the VS Code extension. ### Kilo version Platform v2 (shown in review detail badges on the Kilo dashboard) ### Steps to reproduce 1. Have a repository with Code Reviewer enabled and BYOK (OpenAI) configured 2. Create a PR targeting master 3. Observe: session log shows only the system prompt, zero tool calls, zero comments, marked "Completed" 4. Create a PR targeting any non-master branch in the same repo 5. Observe: review works correctly (file reads, comments posted, model/tokens visible) ### Screenshot and/or share link **Broken run (PR targeting master):** Session log shows only system prompt, 8-second session, no model/tokens in review details. **Working run (PR targeting feature branch, same repo, same day):** Session log shows 10+ Tool: read calls, model gpt-5.3-codex, 288K tokens, 2 inline comments posted. ~2 minute session. ### Operating System N/A (Kilo Platform / cloud-hosted) ### Terminal N/A

body:
## Feature Request: Add /resume command for session history

### Problem
Currently, Kilo does not preserve conversation history between sessions. Users cannot see the list of previous sessions when they connect, unlike Claude Code which has `/resume`.

### Proposed Solution

Add a `/sessions` or `/resume` command that:
1. Shows the list of recent sessions with timestamps
2. Allows resuming a previous session
3. Optionally displays conversation content

### Implementation Plan

**Structure:**
```
~/.kilo/
├── sessions.json    # List of sessions
└── config.json      # Existing config
```

**sessions.json format:**
```json
{
  "sessions": [
    {
      "id": "abc123",
      "project": "C:/path/to/project",
      "started": "2024-04-14T10:00:00Z",
      "ended": "2024-04-14T12:30:00Z",
      "title": "Integration devops-tooling"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Benefits
- Track work across multiple sessions
- Resume previous conversations easily
- Better project history visibility

### Questions
1. Display session list only or also message content?
2. Per-project or global sessions?
3. Limit number of sessions to keep (10, 20, 50)?

---
*Submitted by: Raouf KONE*
*Date: 2026-04-14*

comments.body:
Agree.  Since Claude is the standard, why not try for feature parity?

There is a workaround...Login to your account on the web, open the Seesions menu option, and get the resume command for the session you want...run that in your terminal.

title: Feature Request: Add /resume or /sessions command for session history

body:
## Description

Users are reporting that the CLI appears to fail to refresh OAuth tokens, resulting in the following error:

```
{
  "name": "UnknownError",
  "data": {
    "message": "Error: Token refresh failed: 401"
  }
}
```

This is happening for both Claude/Anthropic and Codex providers.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Use Kilo CLI with OAuth authentication (either Claude/Anthropic or Codex provider)
2. Wait for the token to expire
3. The CLI fails to refresh the token and returns the above error

## Expected Behavior

The CLI should successfully refresh OAuth tokens without user intervention.

## Actual Behavior

Token refresh fails with a 401 error, preventing continued use of the CLI until the user re-authenticates. This affects both Claude/Anthropic and Codex providers.

The 'Revert and Checkpoints' function is not working properly. 

- It doesn't revert all the changes. 
- The new files aren't removed 
- it doesn't catch the initial state. 

### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

1. start the task 
2. try to Revert the changes
3. It doesn't revert all the changes.

Windows 11 25H2

comments.body: @marius-kilocode
Hello, how do I trigger this function? I couldn't find how to undo the previous operation in the kilo cli

> Hello, how do I trigger this function? I couldn't find how to undo the previous operation in the kilo cli

this is kilo code extension
@zakimimit yes, this is not supported at the moment, we can only revert changes done by the agent within the current session. We are going to improve this!
@zhaoyizai in the CLI you can use `/undo`

> [@zakimimit](https://github.com/zakimimit) yes, this is not supported at the moment, we can only revert changes done by the agent within the current session. We are going to improve this!
@marius-kilocode 

The probelm is :

- It doesn't revert all the changes.
- The new files aren't removed
- it doesn't catch the initial state.

1. The checkpoint only appears in the first prompt, but when working with a long session, it should appear after any editing.
2. Make it revert only the files that have changed by kilo, not the files that were handwritten by the user.
( kilo should only revert  the files that changed by kilo ) If I change the file AB.txt manually and not using the kilo function, it should not revert the file AB.txt.

It was better in Roo Code where I was able to revert back to **ANY** edit. In Kilo it seems to not work at all. If I revert to any of my messages the files don't change even if I never touched them! I remember this feature working some time before (albeit also not reliably), now it doesn't work completely. I tried turning it off and on, no changes. I suppose it **SHOULD** revert when I click "Revert to here" but as the OP says it doesn't work if I revert to my first message and I keep noticing it doesn't trigger if I revert to any previous message. I now have to manually commit everything I think is important before making changes. I believe the checkpoint revert buttons should be at every edit/write tool invocation and they should prompt whether to revert just the files or the chat too (as it was in Roo).

If a checkpoint can't be reverted because the user changed files there *has* to be an explicit error message or a prompt asking whether I want to overwrite the files from checkpoint, revert the files that only the agent touched, or don't do anything. Currently I'm left in the dark and when I revert the chat I never know if files were reverted or not, and if they weren't I irreversibly lose the part of history that could be used to revert semi-manually by asking the agent to undo the changes. Overall, checkpoint experience is very underwhelming and basically useless as it almost never works and there are no clues as to why.

EDIT: it's actually much, much worse. I just noticed when I started a new task and then reverted to the first message (because the agent's solution was wrong and I wanted to try something else), for whatever reason Kilo reverted A LOT of my changes, like to a few days ago! If I had them uncommitted it'd be a total disaster. I noticed because not only there were a lot of changes reported in the Git view, but also a couple of file references from the main App.tsx disappeared (at least not the files themselves!) that I was working on for a couple of days already. And the task was started just a minute ago. Something's deeply wrong with checkpoint management. Another way to shoot yourself in the foot is to start another task for a small out of context change, and then use revert in the first task. I don't remember the details but the effect was just as dramatic.
Can confirm I experience the same behavior. "Revert to here" not working at all. I guess it might be related with the model choice, but I'm not sure.

My issue might be related to my usual workflow with a multi-root workspace. When I develop a webapp with frontend and backend it's usually split into 3 workspace folders, `ui`, `server`, and `scripts` (for deployment). I can't open just the top folder because I also use Yarn PnP and its VS Code integration requires the JS folder to be the "main" one, otherwise it's impossible to choose a custom Typescript LSP server that works in PnP environment. And without it code navigation is broken, VS Code doesn't see any dependencies because `node_modules` is almost empty. So as a workaround I first open the `ui` folder and then add the remaining folders using `"Add Folder to Workspace..."`. This might confuse Kilo Code as it doesn't understand what the root of the project is, whether it's `ui` or the folder above (that contains all three). It was an issue with Roo Code as well (https://github.com/RooCodeInc/Roo-Code/issues/8041) and it was never fixed. I wish there were an option to explicitly tell the extension what the root is, overriding autodetection.

This thread currently mixes several checkpoint concerns. Step-level restore points are tracked in #8378, preserving user or other-session edits is tracked in #11060, and the specific cross-folder move data-loss case is tracked in #9741.

Let's keep this issue only for reproducible restore correctness failures that are not covered there, such as an existing edit not being restored, a newly created file not being removed, or the initial state not being captured. Please provide one current reproduction per remaining behavior, including the Kilo version, workspace layout, exact file operations, and before/after Git status.

title: The 'Revert and Checkpoints' function is not working properly

However, yesterday, after creating a new section, I attempted to switch to another section (also created under version 7) via "Show History." The moment I entered the "Show History" page, I noticed the list of past sections briefly refreshed and then was completely cleared. I have since conducted a few experiments: I created a new section and then navigated to the "Show History" page. Once again, the moment the page loaded, I could briefly see the newly created section flash before the entire section list in "Show History" was cleared. I tried opening other VS Code projects and found that the sections in those projects had also disappeared (the Kilo Code main page was empty), as shown in the attached screenshot. I uninstalled Kilo Code and reinstalled it, but the issue persists. I used Settings → About → "Migrate from Legacy Version" to force a re-migration. I noticed that Kilo Code did find some previous section lists and successfully migrated 40 sections. However, after the re-migration, the main page of Kilo Code is still empty. I hope this additional information is helpful. Please let me know if there is anything else I can provide to assist with troubleshooting. Thank you for your time and support. comments.body:
I would like to share some additional test results along with a few minor findings:

1. Kilo 7.2.0 connecting to a newly created local directory on Win11: Working normally.
2. Kilo 7.2.0 connecting to an existing Win11 mapped network drive M: (an Ubuntu 16.04 Samba share previously connected by Kilo 5): Working normally.
3. Kilo 7.2.0 connecting to a newly created (never previously connected) Win11 mapped network drive K: (mapped to an Ubuntu 16.04 Samba share): Abnormal / Not working.
4. Kilo 7.2.0 connecting to a newly created (never previously connected) Win11 mapped network drive L: (mapped to an Ubuntu 24.04 Samba share): Abnormal / Not working.

In summary, it appears that Kilo 7.2.0 works properly with local directories and previously established network drive mappings, but seems to fail when connecting to newly created mapped network drives, regardless of the Ubuntu version hosting the Samba share.
Update : The 7.2.5 still has this issue.

I've taken a shot at fixing this issue on version 7.2.0. Please feel free to take a look when you have a chance.

```
diff --git a/packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts b/packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts
index af5b295..263adb6 100644
--- a/packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts
+++ b/packages/kilo-vscode/src/KiloProvider.ts
@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
+import * as fs from "fs"
 import * as path from "path"
 import * as vscode from "vscode"
 import { buildPreviewPath, getPreviewCommand, getPreviewDir, parseImage, trimEntries } from "./image-preview"
@@ -3179,10 +3180,46 @@ export class KiloProvider implements vscode.WebviewViewProvider, TelemetryProper
     })
   }

+  /** Cache for resolved network drive paths to avoid repeated syscalls. */
+  private resolvedDriveCache = new Map<string, string>()
+
+  /**
+   * Resolve a Windows mapped network drive path (e.g., H:\) to its UNC path
+   * (e.g., \\server\share).  The CLI backend stores sessions with the resolved
+   * UNC path, but VS Code reports the drive letter.  Without resolution,
+   * session.list({ roots: true }) fails to match because "H:\" ≠ "\\server\share".
+   *
+   * Uses fs.realpathSync.native() which calls the Windows GetFinalPathNameByHandle API.
+   * Returns the original path on non-Windows or if resolution fails.
+   */
+  private resolveNetworkDrivePath(fsPath: string): string {
+    if (process.platform !== "win32") return fsPath
+
+    const cached = this.resolvedDriveCache.get(fsPath)
+    if (cached !== undefined) return cached
+
+    try {
+      let resolved = fs.realpathSync.native(fsPath)
+      // GetFinalPathNameByHandle returns \\?\UNC\server\share for network paths
+      if (resolved.startsWith("\\\\?\\UNC\\")) {
+        resolved = "\\\\" + resolved.substring(8)
+      }
+      // GetFinalPathNameByHandle returns \\?\C:\... for local paths
+      else if (resolved.startsWith("\\\\?\\")) {
+        resolved = resolved.substring(4)
+      }
+      this.resolvedDriveCache.set(fsPath, resolved)
+      return resolved
+    } catch {
+      this.resolvedDriveCache.set(fsPath, fsPath)
+      return fsPath
+    }
+  }
+
   private getRootDirectory(): string {
     const workspaceFolders = vscode.workspace.workspaceFolders
     if (workspaceFolders && workspaceFolders.length > 0) {
-      return workspaceFolders[0]!.uri.fsPath
+      return this.resolveNetworkDrivePath(workspaceFolders[0]!.uri.fsPath)
     }
     return process.cwd()
   }
```

Hi @lambertjosh @imanolmzd-svg 

For your reference, the changes mentioned above for version 7.2.0 are also applicable to version 7.2.10.

Hi, thanks for tracking this issue!

I've been investigating a related problem on Windows with mapped network drives (e.g. H:\ → \\server\share\path), where the session list always comes back empty.

Root cause: realpathSync.native() resolves H:\ to the correct UNC path, but preserves the trailing backslash from the input (e.g. \\server\share\path\). Sessions stored in the DB don't have the trailing backslash (\\server\share\path), so the SQL equality check fails to match.

There are two possible approaches to fix this:

Approach A — CLI side: In normalizePath(), pass the result of realpathSync.native() through path.resolve() before returning. This strips trailing separators for non-root paths while keeping drive roots like C:\ intact.
1. packages/opencode/src/util/filesystem.ts — return native → return win32.resolve(native)
2. packages/opencode/src/filesystem/index.ts — return native → return pathResolve(native)


Approach B — Extension side: In KiloProvider.ts, resolve mapped drive letters to UNC paths via fs.realpathSync.native() in getRootDirectory() before passing the directory to the CLI. This way the CLI always receives a consistent UNC path.

I've verified both approaches locally on Windows with a mapped network drive. Hope this helps!

Thank you very much.

```
diff --git a/packages/opencode/src/filesystem/index.ts b/packages/opencode/src/filesystem/index.ts
index 01fdcd2e5..1197c6816 100644
--- a/packages/opencode/src/filesystem/index.ts
+++ b/packages/opencode/src/filesystem/index.ts
@@ -190,7 +190,20 @@ export namespace AppFileSystem {
     if (process.platform !== "win32") return p
     const resolved = pathResolve(windowsPath(p))
     try {
-      return realpathSync.native(resolved)
+      let native = realpathSync.native(resolved)
+      // realpathSync.native() returns \\?\UNC\server\share for network paths
+      // and \\?\C:\... for local paths.  Strip the prefix so callers get
+      // standard Windows paths (\\server\share or C:\...).
+      if (native.startsWith("\\\\?\\UNC\\")) {
+        native = "\\\\" + native.substring(8)
+      } else if (native.startsWith("\\\\?\\")) {
+        native = native.substring(4)
+      }
+      // Normalize the result to strip trailing separators that
+      // realpathSync.native() may preserve from the input (e.g. "H:\" →
+      // "\\server\share\" keeps the trailing "\").  pathResolve() strips
+      // trailing "\" for non-root paths while keeping "C:\" intact.
+      return pathResolve(native)
     } catch {
       return resolved
     }
diff --git a/packages/opencode/src/util/filesystem.ts b/packages/opencode/src/util/filesystem.ts
index 5f50231b0..3a65f00eb 100644
--- a/packages/opencode/src/util/filesystem.ts
+++ b/packages/opencode/src/util/filesystem.ts
@@ -115,7 +115,20 @@ export namespace Filesystem {
     if (process.platform !== "win32") return p
     const resolved = win32.normalize(win32.resolve(windowsPath(p)))
     try {
-      return realpathSync.native(resolved)
+      let native = realpathSync.native(resolved)
+      // realpathSync.native() returns \\?\UNC\server\share for network paths
+      // and \\?\C:\... for local paths.  Strip the prefix so callers get
+      // standard Windows paths (\\server\share or C:\...).
+      if (native.startsWith("\\\\?\\UNC\\")) {
+        native = "\\\\" + native.substring(8)
+      } else if (native.startsWith("\\\\?\\")) {
+        native = native.substring(4)
+      }
+      // Normalize the result to strip trailing separators that
+      // realpathSync.native() may preserve from the input (e.g. "H:\" →
+      // "\\server\share\" keeps the trailing "\").  win32.resolve() strips
+      // trailing "\" for non-root paths while keeping "C:\" intact.
+      return win32.resolve(native)
     } catch {
       return resolved
     }
```

body: ## Summary Users report that the ability to click an image icon to browse and select local images from disk is missing in the new version. This feature exists and works in [kilocode-legacy](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode-legacy) and should be brought into the rewrite. It's more than just an image button:

comments.body: Not sure if this is the exact same case. "Attach image" button missing, version 7.2.40.

## Additional evidence — local vision models blocked by missing attach UI (7.3.40)

+1 on this regression. Confirming on **Kilo Code 7.3.40** (VS Code OSS, macOS arm64): the chat toolbar has **no paperclip / attach button**, and there is no obvious file-picker entry point in the input chrome.

### Real-world impact: local multimodal / VLM workflows

I'm running **DiffusionGemma 26B A4B IT (MLX bf16)** locally as an OpenAI-compatible vision+text server (`mlx_vlm.server` on `:8080`). Vision works end-to-end **outside** Kilo:

```bash
./2_run.sh --image ~/Desktop/screenshot.png --prompt "What do you see?"
# → correct description of screenshot contents
```

Kilo is configured correctly for image input — model entry has `attachment: true` and `modalities.input: ["text", "image"]`, default model `diffusiongemma/diffusiongemma-26b-a4b-it-bf16`, `baseURL: http://localhost:8080/v1`. The backend is ready; the **UI is the blocker**.

Without a visible attach affordance, users have to discover undocumented workarounds (Shift+drag into webview, clipboard paste, or `@` → Attach file). That's not discoverable and is easy to mistake for a broken setup — I initially thought `kilo.json` was wrong because the paperclip from older Kilo blog posts (v4.24) simply isn't there in 7.x.

### This looks like a regression, not a net-new feature

- **Kilo 4.24** documented a context-menu paperclip / "Add Image" flow ([blog post](https://blog.kilo.ai/p/kilo-code-4240-image-attachments))
- **Kilo 5.x / legacy** had a full attach toolbar (screenshot in OP)
- **Kilo 7.3.40** extension docs still list a toolbar attach button as **remaining work** (`docs/features/file-attachments.md`, P2) and point at #6078
- **#6078** was closed with "Done" (Apr 2026), but the toolbar file-picker / paperclip button described there is **still absent** in 7.3.40 — so closing that issue may have masked ongoing parity debt

### Why this should be higher priority

1. **Vision/multimodal is a first-class use case** — screenshots, UI debugging, diagram review. Local VLMs (Gemma 4, DiffusionGemma, Qwen-VL, etc.) are increasingly run via `kilo.json` + OpenAI-compatible providers.
2. **Config alone cannot fix discoverability** — `attachment: true` in `kilo.json` enables the capability flag, but users need an obvious attach control in the chat input.
3. **Parity with legacy** — this isn't a nice-to-have; it's restoring functionality users already had.
4. **Support burden** — missing UI causes false "my vision model doesn't work" reports when the server and config are fine.

### Request

Please restore a **visible attach control** in the VS Code chat input (paperclip or equivalent) with a standard file picker, matching legacy/5.x behavior. Minimum: image picker for models with `modalities.input` including `image`; ideally general file attach per #6078.

Happy to retest on any pre-release build. This is a blocker for using Kilo as the front-end for local vision models.

Related: #8277, #8451, #9384, closed #6078 (toolbar item still missing despite "Done")
+1 on 7.3.41, VScodium on Ubuntu 24.04.
Also seeing this on macOS 14.8.5, VSCodium, extenstion version 7.3.46. Note that copy/paste of a screenshot works, but trying to paste a file or drag and drop files doesn't work either.

body:
## Summary

VS Code's built-in quick fix functionality (Ctrl+. / Cmd+.) is degraded by two code action provider issues in the Kilo Code extension, even after the keybinding conflict fix in #8508.

## Root Cause Analysis

### Issue 1: Keybinding conflict (fixed in #8508)

The `cycleAgentMode` command was bound to `Ctrl+.` / `Cmd+.` **without any `when` clause**, overriding VS Code's built-in `editor.action.quickFix` globally. This was introduced in commit `296f5271a` (Mar 20, 2026) and fixed in #8508 (Apr 7, 2026) by adding proper scoping via `when` clauses.

### Issue 2: `AutocompleteCodeActionProvider` always injects into quick fix menu (remaining)

`AutocompleteCodeActionProvider` is registered with document selector `"*"` and **unconditionally** returns a "Kilo Code: Suggested Edits" `QuickFix` action for every `provideCodeActions` call — regardless of:
- Whether autocomplete is enabled in settings
- Whether the file type is relevant
- Whether there's a selection or diagnostics

This means every time a user opens the quick fix menu, they see "Kilo Code: Suggested Edits" as an option, adding noise and clutter.

**File:** `src/services/autocomplete/AutocompleteCodeActionProvider.ts`

### Issue 3: `KiloCodeActionProvider` "Fix with Kilo Code" marked as `isPreferred` (remaining)

The "Fix with Kilo Code" code action sets `isPreferred: true`. In VS Code, preferred quick fixes are auto-applied when users trigger "Auto Fix" (`editor.action.autoFix`). This means Kilo's "send to chat" action can hijack actual language server fixes that have real code edits.

**File:** `src/services/code-actions/code-action-provider.ts:26`

## Expected Behavior

1. The autocomplete code action should only appear when autocomplete is enabled
2. "Fix with Kilo Code" should not be marked as `isPreferred` since it opens a chat rather than directly fixing code — language server quick fixes should take priority

## Timeline

| Date | Commit | What happened |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Mar 12, 2026 | `39325ff52` | `cycleAgentMode` added with sidebar-only `when` clause |
| Mar 20, 2026 | `296f5271a` | `cycleAgentMode` re-added **without** `when` clause (globally active) |
| Apr 7, 2026 | `8b07b5544` | #8508 fixed the keybinding with proper `when` scoping |

## Affected Versions

v7.1.0 through v7.1.22 (keybinding issue). Code action issues remain in v7.1.23+.

comments.body: @markijbema is this the sidebar or agent manager or tab?
don't know, i was intending to try this out on my windows machine

## Analysis

The file path click detection **only works on backtick-wrapped inline code** (markdown code spans). Plain text file paths are never processed.

### How it works

1. The marked renderer's `codespan()` hook (`packages/ui/src/context/marked.tsx:637-651`) calls `parseFilePath()` on the text inside backticks
2. If `parseFilePath()` matches, it emits `<code class="file-link" data-file-path="...">` instead of plain `<code>`
3. The click handler in `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx:1188-1214` listens for clicks on `.file-link[data-file-path]` elements and calls `openFile()`

### Why some paths work and others don't

It's not about the path format — the regex in `packages/ui/src/file-path.ts:6-9` matches both `packages/kilo-vscode/CHANGELOG.md` and `version.ts` (there's even a test confirming `foo.ts` matches). The difference is whether the AI model wraps the path in backticks:

- `` `packages/kilo-vscode/CHANGELOG.md` `` → code span → `parseFilePath` runs → clickable
- `version.ts` (no backticks) → plain text → never enters `codespan` renderer → not clickable

### Repro prompt

To reproduce reliably, send this in a chat session:

> Read `packages/kilo-vscode/CHANGELOG.md` and also read version.ts in the script directory. Tell me what each file does.

The model will likely backtick-wrap the first path (since you used backticks in the prompt) but refer to the second one as plain text. The first will be clickable, the second won't.

Alternatively, compare these two outputs manually:
- `` `version.ts` `` → clickable
- `version.ts` → not clickable

### Possible fixes

1. **Scan plain text too** — In the marked renderer's `text()` hook (or a post-processing step), scan for file-path-like tokens and wrap them in `<code class="file-link">`. Risky: could have false positives on words that look like paths.
2. **Prompt engineering** — Instruct the model to always wrap file paths in backticks. Already partially done via the `file_path:line_number` convention in the system prompt, but models don't always comply.
3. **Post-render regex** — After markdown rendering, scan the DOM for text nodes matching the `parseFilePath` regex and wrap matches in clickable spans. More robust than option 1 but heavier.

### Relevant files

| File | What it does |
|---|---|
| `packages/ui/src/file-path.ts:6-27` | `parseFilePath()` regex + function |
| `packages/ui/src/context/marked.tsx:637-651` | `codespan()` renderer that adds `.file-link` class |
| `packages/kilo-ui/src/components/message-part.tsx:1188-1214` | Click handler for `.file-link` elements |
| `packages/ui/src/components/markdown.css:228-240` | CSS styling for `.file-link` |
| `packages/ui/src/file-path.test.ts` | Tests for path detection |

### Description

## Summary
In Kilo Code 7.x for VS Code, I am unable to add selected editor code to context using any of the available entry points. The command appears in the UI, but it does not perform any action.

## Environment
- VS Code version: 
```
Version: 1.114.0
Commit: e7fb5e96c0730b9deb70b33781f98e2f35975036
Date: 2026-04-01T09:27:11Z (6 days ago)
Electron: 39.8.3
ElectronBuildId: 13658728
Chromium: 142.0.7444.265
Node.js: 22.22.1
V8: 14.2.231.22-electron.0
OS: Darwin arm64 25.4.0
```
- Kilo Code version: 7.1.22
- Operating system: [ macOS ]
- File type tested: [e.g. `.ts`, `.js`, `.py`]


## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open VS Code with Kilo Code 7.1.22 installed also tried to reinstall it.
2. Open any source file.
3. Select a block of code in the editor.
4. Try to add the selected code to context using one of the following methods:
   - Right-click on the selection and choose **Kilo Code** → **Add to Context**
   - Use the keyboard shortcut for **Add to Context** (Cmd+K Cmd+A)
   - Open the Code Actions / lightbulb menu and choose **Add to Context**
5. Observe the result.

## Expected Behavior
The selected code should be added to Kilo Code context and become available in the chat/input context.

## Actual Behavior
Nothing happens. The selected code is not added to context, and there is no visible action or result.

## What I Tested
I verified that:
- The text was actually selected before triggering the command.
- The issue occurs using multiple invocation methods.
- The issue is not limited to the right-click menu.
- The issue also occurs when using the keyboard shortcut.
- The issue also occurs from the Code Actions / lightbulb menu.
- Restarting VS Code did not resolve it.
- The problem persists across multiple attempts.

## Additional Notes
This appears to be a regression in the 7.x release line when use before 5.x this feature works and was very useful. 

## Impact
This makes it difficult to use Kilo Code for code selection workflows, since selected code cannot be sent into context reliably.

## Requested Fix
Please investigate whether the `Add to Context` command is broken in the 7.x extension build, especially for:
- right-click menu integration
- keyboard shortcut handling
- Code Actions / lightbulb menu integration
- context capture from selected editor text

body:
## Summary

This is a tracking issue for "human in the loop" (HITL) improvements in the VS Code extension — features that give users better visibility, review capabilities, and control over agent actions as they happen.

These issues cluster into five areas:

---

## 1. Diff Review / Pre-Approval

The core ask: users need to **see what the agent is changing before approving it**, with accurate, focused diffs.

- [ ] #8264 — **Pre-approve and diff view code changes**: v7 doesn’t show diffs until after approval. Restore the review-then-approve workflow with side-by-side diffs. (More discussion right now than concrete solution)
- [ ] #8324 — **Show only the latest incremental diff in Patch view**: After an agent updates a file, show only what changed in this step, not the cumulative diff from the start.
- [ ] #8290 — **Diff preview shows misleading +N/-M counts**: Line counts are inaccurate because full file contents are stripped from storage; the visual diff falls back to minimal snippets.
- [ ] #9730 — **Edit/diff code blocks take too much vertical space — make them scrollable with a higher threshold**: Long edit blocks push preceding reasoning off-screen. Raise the inline-render threshold and make the block internally scrollable (matching Claude Code and our ask/question tool). Also tracked under the diff-improvements epic #9542.

## 2. Granular Checkpoint / Rollback

- [ ] #8378 — **Expose checkpoint restore by agent step**: Let users see which files changed at each agent step, compare adjacent steps, and restore to a specific step inside a turn — not just at the user-prompt level.

## 3. Permission / Approval Flow (Broken HITL)

The approval mechanism fires incorrectly, creating noise without real oversight value.

- [ ] #8353 — **Commands re-prompted for approval after already being permitted**: Approval state doesn’t synchronize across parallel Agent Manager sessions.
- [ ] #8391 — **Repeated external-directory read approval prompts even when set to allow**: The approval flow doesn’t respect its own `allow` configuration.

## 4. Agent Permission Controls

- [ ] #8398 — **Custom agent UI missing per-agent tool/permission controls**: Users want to constrain what each agent can do (e.g., read-only reviewer, no-bash orchestrator) directly in the UI.

## 5. Context / Visibility

- [x] #8210 — **Graph in taskheader**: Show a context usage visualization in the task header for turn-by-turn visibility into token/context consumption.
- [ ] #8334 — **Show truncated todo message when checking off items**: Reduce clutter in the review surface so users can follow agent actions step-by-step.
- [ ] #8418 — **Setting to expand reasoning blocks by default**: Show more of the agents thought process so users can follow before actions start being taken. (PR #9704)
- [ ] #9706 — **Fold terminal command output to just the top bar**: Apply the same folding UX as reasoning blocks to terminal command entries, with a display setting to control default fold behavior.

## 6. Improved onboarding for HITL users

- [ ] #8419 — **Ask new users preference (HITL or agentic) and align settings**: Ask users how they prefer to work with agents and set up extension accordingly: https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/8419

comments.body:
\+ 1 for Diff Review / Pre-Approval.

My comment at https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/discussions/1563#discussioncomment-16465950

Added #9730 to section 1 (Diff Review / Pre-Approval). It raises the inline-render threshold for edit/diff code blocks and makes them internally scrollable (matching Claude Code and our ask/question tool), so long diffs stop pushing the agent’s preceding reasoning off-screen during review. Also tracked under the diff-improvements epic #9542.

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Summary

Please add a way to restore/revert checkpoints at the agent step level rather than limiting restore to the user-prompt level.

Current behavior

The current snapshot system records state at step boundaries, but the UI only allows revert at the user message level.

Requested behavior

Expose snapshots/checkpoints in the UI by agent step, so a user can:

view the sequence of steps within a single agent turn
see which files changed at each step
restore to a specific step inside a turn
optionally compare adjacent steps before restoring
Why this matters

Long-session usability
In long sessions, it is time-consuming to locate the correct user prompt in a large conversation history when the actual rollback target is a specific agent step within that prompt.

As a supporting usability improvement, it may also help to provide a simple filter so the user can temporarily view only user prompts when navigating long sessions. That is not the main request here, but it would make the overall checkpoint/revert workflow easier to use.

Better recovery from rate limits / interrupted runs
When a model hits a rate limit or a session becomes unusable mid-turn, step-level restore would let the user revert to the exact point before the failure/interruption, instead of losing all work from the entire user prompt.
Parity with the old checkpoint experience
The prior extension exposed checkpointing in a way that was more useful for iterative workflows. The new extension appears to have step-level snapshots internally, but that granularity is not available in the restore UI.
Suggested UX
Add a collapsible Steps section under each agent response
Show each step with:
timestamp/order
tool/model action summary
changed files
“Restore to this step”
“View diff from previous step”
Keep the current message-level revert, but add step-level restore as an advanced option
Notes

The docs already say snapshots are captured at step boundaries, so this seems like a UI/restore-granularity gap rather than a request for a completely new backend concept.

comments.body: The biggest key points I'd like checkpoints added for are Question and Plan Exit responses. These are human inputs, but currently we have no capability to restore to them. However, having a checkpoint at every step would solve this.

## Proposed scope and implementation direction

The current architecture already records snapshot IDs at model-step boundaries and accepts a part-level rewind target. The missing experience is primarily in the VS Code transcript: step boundaries are not rendered, parts after a part-level rewind are not visually truncated, and rewind is only exposed on user messages.

This issue can therefore deliver the legacy-style checkpoint experience with minimal shared OpenCode changes. The separate correctness problem for concurrent sessions sharing one working directory is tracked in #11060 and should not be folded into the first UI implementation.

### Checkpoint semantics

- Render one checkpoint line immediately before each completed model step that contains user-visible or mutating tool activity.
- Treat all tool calls emitted in parallel by one model step as one atomic checkpoint group. There is no stable intermediate workspace state between those calls, so the UI must not imply that users can rewind between individual parallel calls.
- Keep the existing user-message rewind affordance.
- Use the step-start part as the target for later steps within an assistant message.
- Treat the first step boundary as the start of the turn, equivalent to rewinding to the parent user message.
- Include human interaction boundaries such as completed Question and Plan Exit responses so users can return to the state before those interactions.

### Suggested transcript UX

Show a subtle horizontal checkpoint line similar to the previous extension, with actions available on hover or focus:

- **Restore files and conversation** as the primary action
- **View changes from this checkpoint**
- **Rewind conversation only**
- **Restore files only**, if we can preserve clear semantics with the existing revert state

When a step contains parallel tools, the line may say `Checkpoint before 3 parallel tools` or use an equivalent compact label. The individual tool rows should remain visible as they are today.

A checkpoint action selected while the agent is running should behave as **stop and rewind**:

1. cancel the active session and any synchronous child task work
2. wait until tool settlement and final snapshot capture have completed
3. apply the selected rewind
4. report a normal error without changing files if cancellation or snapshot finalization does not complete safely

The implementation must not start file restoration while a tool can still write to the workspace.

### Revert-state behavior

A part-level revert must be represented consistently in the transcript:

- hide or mark all parts from the selected boundary onward
- keep earlier parts in the same assistant response visible
- place the revert/redo banner at the actual inline boundary rather than only after the complete message list
- avoid copying assistant text into the prompt input when the target is an assistant part
- continuing after rewind should remove later parts through the existing cleanup flow and create the next session branch from the selected boundary
- redo should restore the prior visible state while the revert preview is still active

The retained assistant message metadata should remain consistent after partial cleanup. In particular, completion state and finish reason must not refer to a removed step.

### Minimize shared OpenCode changes

Prefer an extension-first implementation:

- group the already-delivered raw message parts by step-start/step-finish boundaries in the webview
- render the checkpoint component in the VS Code extension or Kilo UI package
- apply `revert.partID` when deriving visible assistant parts
- reuse the existing part-aware revert request
- keep new UX, grouping, and state-selection logic in Kilo-owned packages

Only add backend work if needed for an atomic cancel-then-rewind operation or for correcting partial-message cleanup state. Any shared-file change should be a narrow marked delegation into Kilo-owned code.

### Concurrency safety boundary

The current snapshot and patch records describe whole-working-directory intervals. If two sessions overlap in the same directory, one session's checkpoint may include another session's files. Until #11060 is implemented:

- do not claim that workspace rewind is independently safe for overlapping sessions in one directory
- keep conversation-only rewind available
- use an explicit warning or disable file rewind when another active session is known to share the directory
- recommend Agent Manager worktrees for intentionally parallel modifying sessions

Sessions in separate worktrees can use the full checkpoint workflow because their filesystem state is isolated.

### Acceptance criteria

- A checkpoint line appears at every eligible step boundary inside an assistant turn.
- Parallel tool calls from one step produce one checkpoint line, not one line per tool.
- A user can stop a running agent and rewind only after active tools have settled.
- Rewinding to a later step preserves earlier steps in the same turn and hides/removes later parts correctly.
- The first step rewinds cleanly to the start of the user turn.
- Question and Plan Exit interaction boundaries can be selected.
- View changes opens the diff for the selected checkpoint interval.
- Prompt restoration only occurs for user-message boundaries.
- Redo remains available while previewing a rewind.
- Shared-directory concurrency is warned or gated until #11060 provides mutation ownership and conflict-safe restoration.
- The existing message-level revert flow continues to work.

### Test coverage

Add focused coverage for:

- grouping sequential model steps
- collapsing parallel tool calls into one checkpoint group
- first-step and later-step part targets
- part-level visibility and cleanup
- Question and Plan Exit boundaries
- stop, tool settlement, and rewind ordering
- redo after part-level rewind
- shared-directory warning or gating
- isolated worktree behavior

This should be a user-facing patch or minor changeset depending on whether we treat checkpoint-line restore as restored behavior or a new capability.

## Automated reliability requirement

Checkpoint restoration should not rely on a large manual regression checklist. Every supported reset path must have automated behavioral coverage before this is considered complete.

The acceptance suite should use real temporary Git repositories and the actual snapshot/session/revert implementations wherever filesystem behavior is involved. It must verify both exact workspace bytes and exact retained conversation parts for at least:

- the first checkpoint in a turn
- every later checkpoint in a multi-step turn
- all parallel tool calls restored as one atomic step group
- Question rewind, including answering differently after restore
- Plan Exit rewind, including choosing a different direction after restore
- existing-file edits, file creation, deletion, rename, binary content, and multiple files
- active stop-and-rewind at each lifecycle boundary: before write, during write, after write, and before step finalization
- partial tool failure and provider interruption after file changes
- preview, redo, moving between checkpoints, continuing from rewind, and session/webview reload
- prompt text restoration only for user-message boundaries
- abort failure or timeout preventing any restore
- shared-directory warning versus isolated Agent Manager worktrees
- missing or disabled snapshots failing safely without inferred file deletion

Race tests must use deterministic latches/events rather than sleeps. Every scenario should map to a named test in normal package CI. Manual testing should be optional and limited to visual and interaction feel, not restoration correctness.

title: [FEATURE]:Expose checkpoint restore by agent step, not only by user prompt, in the new VS Code extension

FWIW this used to happen on occasion with the old extension as well. Models would just work around the limitations in different ways.  "Well I can't do that but I can do it this way, so I'll just do it this way". Reading files, editing files, etc. We should still try to fix, though.

**Automated codebase check** — @markijbema this is an automated analysis, not a manual review.

This issue appears to be addressed in the current codebase. The edit tool (`packages/opencode/src/tool/edit.ts`) has a sophisticated multi-strategy replacement system with 9 cascading replacers, several of which specifically handle indentation mismatches:

1. **`IndentationFlexibleReplacer`** (lines 437-463) — directly addresses indentation issues by stripping common indentation for comparison while preserving the file's actual indentation in the match.
2. **`LineTrimmedReplacer`** (lines 218-256) — matches lines after `.trim()`, then returns original lines from the file with their indentation intact.
3. **`BlockAnchorReplacer`** (lines 258-391) — uses Levenshtein distance on trimmed lines for approximate matching.
4. **`WhitespaceNormalizedReplacer`** (lines 393-435) — normalizes all whitespace for matching but returns original text.
5. **Post-edit formatting** (line 139) — `Format.file()` runs the project's configured formatter after every edit.

The cascading strategy means even if the AI sends content with incorrect indentation, the tool progressively tries more flexible matching until it finds the correct location while preserving the file's original formatting.

This may be closeable if the reported symptoms no longer reproduce.

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I believe this may be unintentional UX behavior, but it leads to frequent and costly mistakes.

In both the CLI and the VSCode extension, switching agents (e.g., Ask → Code → Orchestrator) can also change the model implicitly. This is easy to miss and has caused issues for me multiple times.

Two common scenarios:

Quality issue
I use a higher-quality model (e.g., Opus) for planning
Then switch to a coding agent expecting the same model
A lower-tier model is used instead, resulting in poor code changes that can break parts of the project

Cost issue
I use a low-cost model (e.g., Kimi) for a simple task
Then switch agents and unknowingly use a high-cost model (e.g., Opus)
This can result in unexpectedly high charges for trivial operations

This happens regularly, even when I try to watch for it. It appears the model change is either not clearly surfaced or only visible after the request is submitted.

Suggested improvements:

Add a “lock model” option (global or per-session)
Prevent automatic model switching when changing agents

Alternatively:
Add a setting: “Keep current model when switching agents”
Improve visibility:
Clearly show the active model before sending a request
Optionally warn when the model changes due to agent switching

This would help prevent unintended costs and inconsistent results, and improve trust when working across agents.

Thanks for considering!

You would specify a specific model in the agent's if you really wanted it to change that drop-down to a known model.  But if it's unspecified it should really leave that drop-down alone.

body:
## Background

In `kilocode-legacy`, users could set a maximum API spending limit (e.g. \$20) for a session. When the accumulated cost of API calls exceeded that threshold, the agent would pause and ask the user: _"You've reached your \$20 limit — do you want to continue?"_ — preventing unexpectedly expensive sessions.

This feature has not been carried over to the current extension and should be restored.

## How it worked in kilocode-legacy

### State / Settings

A `allowedMaxCost` field (type `number | undefined`) was stored in `GlobalState` (see `packages/types/src/global-settings.ts`). When `undefined` or `0`, the limit was treated as unlimited.

### Enforcement — `AutoApprovalHandler`

The core logic lived in `src/core/auto-approval/AutoApprovalHandler.ts`. Before each tool/API call the handler ran `checkAutoApprovalLimits()`, which called `checkCostLimit()`:

1. Accumulated cost was calculated via `getApiMetrics(messagesAfterReset).totalCost` — only counting messages _since the last user-approved reset_.
2. If `currentCost > allowedMaxCost + EPSILON` the handler called `askForApproval("auto_approval_max_req_reached", { count: maxCost.toFixed(2), type: "cost" })`.
3. If the user clicked **Yes / Continue**, `lastResetMessageIndex` was updated to the current message length so the counter restarted from zero.
4. If the user declined (or started a new task), the agent stopped.

The same handler also enforced a **max-requests** limit (`allowedMaxRequests`), using the same approval flow with `type: "requests"`.

### UI — Settings panel

`webview-ui/src/components/settings/MaxCostInput.tsx` rendered a dollar-prefixed numeric field labelled "API Cost Limit" with a placeholder of "Unlimited". It was embedded inside `AutoApproveSettings` / `MaxLimitInputs`. The component used a `unlimitedDecimalFormatter` that treated an empty field as `undefined` (unlimited).

### Approval dialog

The `ClineAsk` type `"auto_approval_max_req_reached"` triggered a chat-level dialog showing the limit that was hit and offering a **Continue** / **Stop** choice.

## Requested behaviour

- Add an **API cost limit** input to the extension settings (under Auto-approve or a new "Limits" section).
- Track accumulated session cost against this limit before each API request.
- When the limit is exceeded, pause and show a confirmation dialog: _"Your session has reached the \$X limit. Continue?"_
- On confirmation, reset the counter so the limit applies incrementally (every \$X from that point).
- On rejection, stop the current task.
- When the field is empty/unset, behaviour is unlimited (no change from current behaviour).

## References

- `src/core/auto-approval/AutoApprovalHandler.ts` (kilocode-legacy)
- `webview-ui/src/components/settings/MaxCostInput.tsx` (kilocode-legacy)
- `webview-ui/src/components/settings/MaxLimitInputs.tsx` (kilocode-legacy)
- `packages/types/src/global-settings.ts` — `allowedMaxCost` field (kilocode-legacy)
title: Restore spending limit (max cost) feature with mid-session approval prompt

body:
## Problem

The settings currently include hardcoded Linux/bash commands in the command allowlist that don't exist on Windows. This happened because we made bash the default shell and allowlisted some commands, but the allowlist is not platform-aware.

For example, Windows users see Linux-specific commands in their settings that are irrelevant to their platform.

## Expected Behavior

The command allowlist should be platform-dependent:
- On Linux/macOS: show Linux/bash commands
- On Windows: show Windows-appropriate commands (e.g., PowerShell/cmd equivalents)

## Affected Areas

- VS Code extension settings
- CLI

## Additional Context

This was introduced when bash was made the default shell with an allowlisted set of commands. The allowlist needs to be conditional based on the user's operating system.
