body:
### Description

When the agent uses the integrated terminal to run `npm` commands in a VSCode WSL environment, the Windows npm (`/mnt/c/nvm4w/nodejs/npm`) is being invoked instead of the Linux npm (`/home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/npm`).

I believe this is similar to what was reported in https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/2495

### Environment
- **Version**: Kilo Code v7.1.17 as VSCode Extension in WSL
- **Platform**: Windows with WSL2 (Debian)
- **Node.js**: Installed via nvm in WSL (`/home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/`)

### Example

> Copied from Kilo Code integrated terminal in VSCode WSL

```
npm run build

> node-project@0.0.0 build
> tsup

'\\wsl.localhost\Debian\home\<user>\<node-project>'
CMD.EXE was started with the above path as the current directory.
UNC paths are not supported.  Defaulting to Windows directory.
npm error code ENOENT
npm error syscall open
npm error path C:\Windows\package.json
npm error errno -4058
npm error enoent Could not read package.json: Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'C:\Windows\package.json'
npm error enoent This is related to npm not being able to find a file.
npm error enoent
npm error A complete log of this run can be found in: C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\npm-cache\_logs\2026-04-02T03_53_02_170Z-debug-0.log
```

#### Expected Behavior
Running `which npm` in the integrated terminal should return `/home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/npm` in WSL.

#### Actual Behavior
`which npm` returns `/mnt/c/nvm4w/nodejs/npm` (Windows npm accessed via `/mnt/c` mount).

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Have Node.js installed in WSL via nvm
2. Have Node.js installed on Windows (e.g., via nvm4w)
3. Open VSCode from Windows and connect to WSL
4. Ask the agent to run `npm` or `which npm`

### Root Cause

The agent spawns **non-interactive, non-login shells** which do not source standard shell initialization files:

1. **Non-interactive shells** do NOT source `.bashrc`
2. **Non-login shells** do NOT source `/etc/profile.d/` or `/etc/profile`
3. **Adding to `/etc/environment`** does not help because the integrated terminal does not inherit from that file in the expected way

This means any PATH modifications made in `.bashrc`, `.zshrc`, or `/etc/profile.d/` are not picked up.

The integrated terminal also appears to inherit a Windows-originated environment when VS Code runs in Windows and connects to WSL, causing `/mnt/c/...` paths to appear in the Linux PATH.

With the legacy extension, this issue could be worked around in settings by switching away from the integrated terminal. The non-integrated terminals would properly source environment files like `.bashrc/.zshrc`, allowing the correct Linux PATH to be set.

### Workaround

**Step 1**: Uninstall Node.js from Windows to eliminate the conflicting path.

**Step 2**: Create symlinks to Linux npm binaries in `/usr/local/bin`:

```bash
sudo ln -s /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/npm /usr/local/bin/npm
sudo ln -s /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/node /usr/local/bin/node
sudo ln -s /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin/npx /usr/local/bin/npx
```

> Since `/usr/local/bin` is in the default PATH for non-interactive shells, this ensures npm is found correctly.

### Plugins

_No response_

### Kilo version

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

_No response_

comments.body:
I have the same issue in IntelliJ kilo plugin `Version: 5.15.0 (6ac2209b)`.

The extension running commands in windows `CMD.exe` instead of WSL shell.
```text
'\\wsl.localhost\Ubuntu\home\...'
CMD.EXE was started with the above path as the current directory.
UNC paths are not supported.  Defaulting to Windows directory.
```
Also, when I disable `Use Inline Terminal` extension tries to use IntelliJ integrated terminal but cannot detect command output.
I have the same problem here with the latest version of PyCharm, KiloCode plugin on Windows11.

Adding another data point from a Windows 11 + WSL2 setup. I observed the same class of Windows/WSL npm shim boundary risk while cleaning up my environment.

My user-side mitigation was:

```ini
# /etc/wsl.conf
[interop]
enabled=true
appendWindowsPath=false
```

Then I restarted WSL and made Linux `node`/`npm`/`npx` available to non-interactive shells through `/usr/local/bin` symlinks pointing to my Linux nvm installation:

```bash
/usr/local/bin/node -> /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node
/usr/local/bin/npm  -> /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/npm
/usr/local/bin/npx  -> /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/npx
```

After that, WSL command resolution became clean:

```text
node=/usr/local/bin/node
npm=/usr/local/bin/npm
npx=/usr/local/bin/npx
cmd.exe=
powershell.exe=
code=
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin:/usr/games:/usr/local/games:/usr/lib/wsl/lib
```

Explicit Windows interop still works when called by full path, for example:

```bash
/mnt/c/Windows/System32/cmd.exe /c ver
```

This is only a user-side workaround, not a Kilo fix. For Kilo, I think the important part is that agent/terminal command execution in a WSL context should avoid inheriting Windows-originated PATH entries, or at least detect and reject `/mnt/c/.../npm`, `/mnt/c/.../node`, and Windows npm shims when the intended execution environment is Linux/WSL.

title: Bug: Integrated Terminal Uses Windows npm Instead of Linux npm in WSL

body:
## Problem

When a user installs the extension fresh or hasn't explicitly configured a small model, the small model selector in the UI appears **empty**. However, the system silently falls back to `kilo-auto/small` for all small model tasks.

This creates confusion because:
- Users can't tell what model is actually being used for small model tasks
- There's no indication that a default/fallback is in effect
- It's unclear whether the feature is working at all

## Context

The small model is used for several background tasks:
- **Session title generation** — Automatically creates titles for chat sessions
- **Commit message generation** — Generates git commit messages for changes made by the agent
- **Conversation summarization/condensing** — Summarizes long conversations to stay within context limits
- **Other lightweight/fast tasks** — Any task where speed matters more than deep reasoning

## Expected Behavior

When no small model is explicitly configured, the UI should clearly indicate which default model will be used (e.g., show `kilo-auto/small` as a placeholder or default value), so users understand what's happening behind the scenes.

## Important Consideration

The UI must still make it easy to distinguish between:
- **Not configured** (using the default fallback) — e.g., shown as a greyed-out placeholder
- **Explicitly configured** — e.g., shown as a normal selected value

This ensures users can tell at a glance whether they've actively chosen a model or are relying on the default.

## Additional Context

Discussion from the team thread: the current behavior is technically correct (it falls back properly), but the UX could be improved to be more transparent about what model is being used when the field is left empty.

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Currently, the AWS Bedrock provider only supports standard foundation model IDs (e.g., `anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0`. There is no way to specify a custom Application Inference Profile (AIP) ARN as the model identifier.

[Application Inference Profiles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-profiles-create.html) are an AWS Bedrock feature for tracking usage and costs when invoking a model. Each AIP has a unique ARN like:

 `arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1:123456789012:application-inference-profile/abc123xyz`

**Requested Change**

Allow users to configure AIPs to be used by specifying the ARN in a config.

Other tools like Claude Code and OpenCode already support configuring AIP ARNs as model identifiers.

comments.body: This is now need more than ever, as Roocode is not available anymore, which supported AIP.

title: [FEATURE]: Support AWS Bedrock Application Inference Profiles (custom model ARNs)

body:
## Problem

When running `kilo acp` (ACP server mode), there is no way to specify which model to use. The server starts with a hardcoded default (`xiaomi/mimo-v2-pro:free`) and ignores any model passed by the ACP client.

This means orchestrators like OpenClaw that want to run Kilo as an ACP harness agent have no way to control model selection. All ACP sessions silently fall back to the default model regardless of what the spawning runtime requests.

In contrast, `kilo run` supports `-m <model>` — that same flag should be available on `kilo acp`.

## Expected behavior

```
kilo acp --model openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
```

The ACP server should use the specified model for all sessions it handles.

## Current behavior

`kilo acp --help` shows no `--model` flag. The server always uses its internal default model.

## Why this matters

ACP orchestrators (e.g. OpenClaw/acpx) define the agent command as a static string like `npx -y @kilocode/cli acp`. Without a `--model` flag, there's no way to configure model selection at the launch site. The acpx runtime passes model info via ACP session metadata (`_meta.claudeCode.options.model`), but since that's a Claude-specific convention, Kilo correctly ignores it.

A `--model` flag on `kilo acp` is the simplest fix that requires no changes on the orchestrator side.

## Workaround

Currently none via ACP. Only `kilo run -m <model>` works, which is one-shot CLI mode and doesn't support persistent ACP sessions.

comments.body:
what a surprise: acp with a hard-coded model...

---

Found a workaround:

```json
                    "command": "npx",
                    "args": [
                        "--yes",
                        "--package",
                        "@kilocode/cli@latest",
                        "kilo",
                        "acp"
                    ],
                    "env": {
                        "KILO_CONFIG_CONTENT": "{\"model\":\"{env:YOUR_MODEL_ID}\"}"
                    },

```

title: kilo acp: add --model flag to allow model selection in ACP server mode

body:
## Problem

When a terminal process runs too long, users currently have no way to stop or unstick it. Previously there was a "Continue running" button that appeared when a terminal ran too long, which was very useful for getting unstuck. That button is no longer present.

Additionally, there is no stop button available per terminal/subtask, so users cannot stop an individual long-running process without affecting the entire session.

## Proposed Solution

1. **Restore the "Continue running" button** — When a terminal process runs for too long, show a button that allows the user to continue (or stop) the running process. This was previously available and is critical for unsticking long-running processes.

2. **Add a Stop button per terminal/subtask** — Each terminal or subtask in the UI should have its own Stop button so users can cancel that specific process without needing to abort the entire task.

## Why This Matters

- Users frequently get stuck on long-running terminal processes with no way to recover other than restarting.
- Granular control over individual subtasks/terminals improves the user experience and reduces frustration.

---

Built for [Mark](https://kilo-code.slack.com/archives/C0A4SA041DE/p1774771065253389) by [Kilo for Slack](https://kilo.ai/features/slack-integration)

## Description

The extension can get stuck in a "Considering next steps..." state indefinitely. When this happens, pressing Escape does not help — the user is unable to cancel or recover without manually intervening (e.g., reloading the window).

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Use the extension normally
2. At some point the extension enters a "Considering next steps..." state
3. The spinner/status remains stuck and does not progress

## Expected Behavior

- The "Considering next steps..." state should either resolve or time out after a reasonable period
- Pressing Escape should cancel the current operation and return the user to an interactive state

## Actual Behavior

- The extension remains stuck on "Considering next steps..."
- Pressing Escape does not cancel or dismiss the state
- The user has no way to recover without reloading

## Additional Context

Reported by a user with a screenshot showing the stuck state.

title: Add context to Settings pages

body:
## Description

When generating a commit message for a repository with many staged files (e.g. 45+ files), the spinner stops but no commit message is set and no error toast appears. The extension host logs show zero `[Kilo New]` entries — the error is being completely swallowed.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a repository with a large number of staged changes (45+ files)
2. Click "Generate commit message" in the Source Control panel
3. Observe: spinner eventually stops, no commit message, no error popup, no logs

## Expected Behavior

Either a commit message is generated, or an error toast explains why it failed (e.g. "Diff too large", timeout message, etc.).

## Root Cause (Suspected)

The `@kilocode/sdk` auto-generated client may not properly support the `signal` (AbortController) option passed via `client.commitMessage.generate()`. If the server-side timeout fires and the HTTP response is a 500/400, the SDK with `throwOnError: true` may not reject the promise correctly for certain error shapes, causing the extension's `catch` block to never execute.

Additionally, the server route (`packages/opencode/src/server/routes/commit-message.ts`) has no try/catch — errors from `generateCommitMessage()` fall through to Hono's default error handler, which returns a generic 500 that may not match the SDK's expected error shape.

## Possible Fixes

1. Add try/catch in the server route returning a proper `{ error: "..." }` 400 response
2. Verify the SDK supports the `signal` option and properly rejects on abort
3. Consider adding a diff size limit in `getGitContext()` that caps the total prompt size, not just per-file (currently 4000 chars per file × many files = massive prompt)

## Related

- #7345 — the original infinite spinner bug (fixed by explicit stream consumption)
- #7434 — PR that fixes the stream consumption issue

comments.body:
## Implementation Notes (Legacy Extension Codebase)

> **Note:** @markijbema : stupid llm won't listen :P this comment describes the situation in kilocode-legacy

> **Note:** The following is based on the current codebase in this repository (the legacy VS Code extension). If the "new repo" referenced in this issue is a separate/rewritten codebase, these details may not apply directly — but they can serve as a reference for how image generation was previously structured.

---

Image generation is already partially implemented in the extension behind an **experimental feature flag** (`IMAGE_GENERATION`). Here is how it works:

### Feature Flag

The tool is gated by the `IMAGE_GENERATION` experiment flag (`src/core/tools/GenerateImageTool.ts:41-53`). When disabled, the tool is excluded from both the tool list and mode filtering (`src/core/prompts/tools/index.ts:160-162`, `src/core/prompts/tools/filter-tools-for-mode.ts:311-313`).

### Tool: `generate_image`

The core tool is implemented in `src/core/tools/GenerateImageTool.ts`. It:
- Accepts a `prompt` (required), `path` (required), and optional `image` (for image editing/inpainting)
- Checks experiment flag and aborts if not enabled
- Selects provider (`openrouter` or `kilocode`) and model via `getImageGenerationProvider()`
- Writes the resulting image to disk and emits it to the UI

### Supported Models (`packages/types/src/image-generation.ts`)

Six models are currently registered for both OpenRouter and Kilo Code providers:
- `google/gemini-2.5-flash-image` — Gemini 2.5 Flash Image
- `google/gemini-3-pro-image-preview` — Gemini 3 Pro Image Preview
- `openai/gpt-5-image` — GPT-5 Image
- `openai/gpt-5-image-mini` — GPT-5 Image Mini
- `black-forest-labs/flux.2-flex` — FLUX.2 Flex
- `black-forest-labs/flux.2-pro` — FLUX.2 Pro

### API Providers (`src/api/providers/utils/image-generation.ts`)

Two generation methods are implemented:
1. **Chat Completions** (`POST /chat/completions` with `modalities: ["image","text"]`) — used by OpenRouter and Kilo Code OpenRouter
2. **Images API** (`POST /images/generations`) — used by the Roo/cloud provider; FLUX models pass input images via `providerOptions.blackForestLabs.inputImage`

The `KilocodeOpenrouterHandler` (`src/api/providers/kilocode-openrouter.ts`) extends `OpenRouterHandler` and inherits `generateImage()` with Kilo-specific headers.

### UI

Generated images are displayed via `ImageViewer` (`webview-ui/src/components/common/ImageViewer.tsx`) which supports zoom, copy path, save, and open in VS Code editor.

### Settings

Four settings keys control image generation (`packages/types/src/global-settings.ts:67-70`):
- `imageGenerationProvider`: `"openrouter"` | `"kilocode"`
- `openRouterImageApiKey`
- `openRouterImageGenerationSelectedModel`
- `kiloCodeImageApiKey`

### Next Steps (for the legacy codebase)

To surface this to users, the main work needed is:
1. Enable the `IMAGE_GENERATION` experiment flag (or graduate it out of experimental)
2. Expose the image generation settings in the UI settings panel
3. Ensure the registered model IDs are current/accurate for each provider
The new VS Code extension is based on Kilo CLI, right? If that is the case, please **enable image generation in the CLI first**. There are many of us interested in using Kilo in the CLI only.
DO IT

However, I am not sure how to get it to work with Kilo Code CLI. It only allows me to select "Ollama Cloud": I can confirm that ollama is installed and works. `curl http://localhost:11434/api/version` gives me `{"version":"0.17.7"}(base)`, and `ollama list` works too: ``` ollama list NAME ID SIZE MODIFIED glm-4.7-flash:latest d1a8a26252f1 19 GB 19 minutes ago lfm2:latest d6c816d74887 14 GB 18 hours ago qwen3-coder:30b 06c1097efce0 18 GB 18 hours ago gemma3:latest a2af6cc3eb7f 3.3 GB 19 hours ago nomic-embed-text:latest 0a109f422b47 274 MB 3 months ago qwen2.5-coder:1.5b-base 02e0f2817a89 986 MB 3 months ago llama3.1:8b 46e0c10c039e 4.9 GB 3 months ago ``` Versions: ``` kilo -v 7.0.41 ollama -v ollama version is 0.17.7 ```

comments.body:
The user is asking how to configure Kilo Code CLI to use a locally-running Ollama instance instead of 'Ollama Cloud'. They have Ollama installed and working locally but cannot find the option in the CLI.

*This response was generated by Kilo Auto-Triage and may not be fully accurate. Please verify the information independently.*

You can check this document: https://opencode.ai/docs/providers/#ollama

Kilo code was built on top OpenCode

@cleong110 you can set it up adding a custom provider pointing to your local Ollama (default http://localhost:11434/v1/), When you will be able to see your local model then you need to set the default model in Models setting. Kilo chat will be showing your model. Previously the configuration was a bit easier for custom local model indeed.

But why need for "custom"? I think ollama is used pretty much, and I thought it would be reasonable to expect it is as simple as to enter ip:port, and later to be able to choose model at any time like on previous version from some combo like box.

So, can local ollama also be included, please?
I would like local ollama added too! But for now, @glaucopater's suggestion works.
I don't see anything relevant here:
You need to add a provider after you setup Ollama locally, then you can set the default model (in this example Gemma4) and then finally you will be able to see the custom model in Kilo chat
I think they drop the ollama intentionally to force you to use cloud service.

+1 would be nice if this was easier and/or better documented. Local LLMs aren't the move for serious coding, but would be nice to be able to have this as an option for airplanes. (I wasn't able to get the CLI working with my local ollama setup).

body:
This is me brainstorming some prompt to easily test functionality in the vscode extension.

Open the kilo-marketplace repo

Before testing, set all permissions to ask, then run these prompt and only use allow once. Use your judgement to see if it works.


(testing the todo tool)
```
please create a todolist of 2 items, create a haiku and create a poem about respectively jan and henk.
then execute steps of the todolist one by one. use the todolist tool
```

(testing questions)
```
Ask me my favourite color (give options), and my favourite animal (give options), then create a short story of a paragraph about this color animal
```

(testing glob, read file)
```
find all markdown files in the repo, then tell me what is the most important one to look at. make sure you read the file before recommending
```

(test subagents which require permissions; this one takes too long, we need a better one)
```
can you please start two subagents. Have one do a glob of all markdown files and the other do a ls of the current dir, then combine the results and tell me what you found
```

---

## Suggest Tool Regressions

### Regression 1: Suggest tool breaks final summary
**Setup**: Set all permissions to ask. Use any repo.
1. Give the agent a small, completable task (e.g., "create a haiku and save it to a file")
2. Wait for the agent to finish the task — it should call the **suggest tool** at the end (e.g., "Would you like me to review?" or similar follow-up suggestion)
3. **Dismiss** the suggestion
4. **Verify**: After dismissing, check that you see a **text summary of what the agent accomplished** before/alongside the suggestion. If the session ends with no visible summary of work done (only the dismissed suggestion), this is a bug.

### Regression 2: Suggest tool keeps session busy indefinitely
**Setup**: Set all permissions to ask. Use any repo.
1. Give the agent a small task that will complete and trigger a **suggest tool** call at the end
2. When the suggestion appears, **do NOT interact with it** — don't accept or dismiss
3. **Verify**: Check the session status in the UI — it should NOT remain stuck as "busy"/loading while waiting for you to respond to a suggestion. You should still be able to send a new message.
4. Also verify: if you **do** send a new message while the suggestion is pending, the old suggestion should auto-dismiss and the new prompt should work normally.

Test model state is independent for each session:
- Open multiple sessions in agent manager
- Toggle model in one session
- Toggle another model in another session
- Switch in between sessions and observe selected model is different (and ask the model if it really is the model in the chat)

### Permissions




_Single bash call with multiple commands_

- Conditions: Start with git related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured.
- Command:
```
Run 4 different git commands in a single line, like git status && git branch && git log --oneline && git remote
```
- Action: Display the permissions dropdown labeled as "Manage Auto-Approved Rules"
- Expectation: Check the dropdown contains a rule for git, and a rule for each individual command.

---


_Large bash commands_

- Conditions: Start with python related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured.
- Command:
```
Run a large heredoc python command using the bash tool, with any values
```
- Action: Wait until a permission prompt is displayed
- Expectation: The permission prompt should have a reasonable height, and the command should be wrapped. The user should be able to scroll on the command to see it in its full length. Each line in the command should be displayed as a separate line

---

_Webfetch tool_

- Conditions: Start with webfetch related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured.
- Command:
```
Use webfetch to check Wikipedia's home page
```
- Action: Wait until a permission prompt is displayed
- Expectation: The permission prompt should appear, and the dropdown "Manage Auto-Approve rules" should have an option to Allow/Deny webfetch tool for future requests

---

_Subagent permissions_

- Conditions: Start with git related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured.
- Command:
```
Spin 5 different subagents where each of them does git log --oneline and use a different number of lines, from 5 to 15
```
- Action: Approve the rule for `git log` on the first prompt
- Expectation: Should auto-approve all the other subagent permissions. You should only have to handle one permission prompt


---


_Subagent permissions - Denied_

- Conditions: Start with git related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured.
- Command:
```
Spin 5 different subagents where each of them does git log --oneline and use a different number of lines, from 5 to 15
```
- Action: Deny (Click on X) the rule for `git log` on the first prompt
- Expectation: Should auto-deny all the other subagent permissions. You should only have to handle one permission prompt

---


_Subagent permissions - approved/denied_
- Conditions: Start with bash related permissions on "ask" mode or not configured. Webfetch permission on "ask" mode
- Command:
```
Spin up 6 different subagents to do the following:

- Subagent 1: Run git status && npm install in a single command
- S. 2: Run npm install
- S. 3: run git status
- S. 4: Use the webfetch tool to check wikipedia home page, reply with a one-line response on what the home page contains.
- S. 5: run git status
- S. 6: run npm install
```
- Action: Approve/Deny (Click on X) the first rules
- Expectation: Should auto-approve/auto-deny subagent permissions that depend on these first. And still ask for webfetch

---

_Worktree subagent permissions_

- Conditions: Create a fresh worktree in Agent Manager. Set bash/python permissions to `ask`.
- Command:
```
In this worktree, use a subagent to run a python command with any values.
```
- Action: Refresh the webview before answering the permission.
- Expectation: The pending permission should survive the refresh and be recoverable.

---

### UX

_Auto-scrolling_

- Conditions: Open the VS Code sidebar. Make sure `git status && npm install` are not allowed as permissions.
- Command:
```
Use 5 subagents. Read a different file from this folder with each of them, any file. Then, write 3 lines about those files in the chat. If there are no files, create 5 random files.
With subagent number 2, also run git status && npm install
```
- Action: First run, let it stream and approve the permissions. Check that it scrolls down automatically, even if it sometimes takes a bit to settle.

Second run, use the same command, but manually scroll up and down while it is streaming to check that it behaves naturally and there are no weird movements.

Then try the same thing again on Agent Manager.
- Expectation: Auto-scroll should keep following the latest output on the first run. On the second run, manual scrolling should work naturally, without unexpected jumps or forced snap-back to bottom. Agent Manager should behave the same way.

---

_Auto-scroll stability and keyboard navigation_

PRs: #7588, #7607
- Commands (Edit them accordingly):
```
Run `bun test` from <location with tests> and analyze the test results.
Explain which tests pass and which fail.
```
```
Use 5 subagents.
Read a different file from this folder with each of them, any file.
Then, write 3 lines about those files in the chat.
If there are no files, create 5 random files.
With subagent number 2, also run git status && npm install
```
- Action: Run each command separately. Check auto-scrolling works correctly. Also, while the agent is working, try scrolling up using both the mouse wheel and keyboard arrows (Page Up, Arrow Up). Then scroll back down to the bottom.
- Expectation: The scroll should follow the content smoothly without visible jumps or flashes as new content streams in. Scrolling up with both mouse and keyboard should immediately detach auto-follow without the chat fighting back or trembling. Scrolling back to the bottom or clicking the "scroll to bottom" button should re-engage auto-follow. When the agent finishes, the view should not jump unexpectedly.

---

### Login/Logout

- Check Login/Logout works correctly
- Models work fine.
- General app usage (Permissions/Questions/Agent Manager/Agent Modes/Settings) works fine logged in and logged out.

---

### Marketplace

- Check an MCP can be downloaded and used. In my case I tested Notion.
- Check you can download an Agent Mode and use it. It should appear in the list of modes.
- Check you can download skills both at global level and at project level, and the agent can see these

---

### Chat

- Test different models - free and paid ones - On different configurations (None, Low, Medium, High, Max)
- Test the send/stop button.
- Test keyboard actions work (E.g. Enter for sending a prompt).
- Use the "Enhance prompt" funcionality.
- Check "New Task" button works.
- Check "Worktree" button works.
- Check Agent Modes work.

---

### AgentManager

- Test "Local" view works
- Worktrees:
    - Can create/delete a worktree
    - Can edit a worktree name
    - Can move worktrees up/down in the list
    - A working indicator ("Thinking" blur) appears when a worktree is processing a prompt.
    - Check subagents, permissions, questions work in worktrees
    - Close all sessions and check "New Session" works
- Diff viewer:
    - The correct diff is seen
    - Can write comments on the diff and send to the chat

---

### Main Page

- Check you can write on the main page prompt
- Check you can change profile
- Check you can click on Recent Sessions
- Check the "Feedback and Support" button works

---

### Settings

Edit Settings across all views and check these are applied correctly

---

### Autocomplete

Check Autocomplete works when working on a file, and check it can be disabled on Settings page

Test checkpoint systems works independent of each worktree:
- Open multiple sessions in agent manager
- Write message and change a file
- Write message and change a file in second session
- Reset to checkpoint in first session and observe that the state of the second session is not touched (checkpoint state is independent)

Focused regression scenarios from recent merges worth keeping in the manual test list:

### Continue in Worktree end-to-end
PRs: #7541, #7639, #7683
- Conditions: Start from the VS Code sidebar chat on a normal checkout, not inside Agent Manager.
- Command:
```
Make a small visible change in one file in this repo, then stop after the edit is applied.
```
- Action:
  1. Click "Continue in Worktree".
  2. Watch the progress text while the worktree is created.
  3. When Agent Manager opens, reopen the continued session from the recent/local session list.
  4. In that continued worktree session, run `/new` and send a simple follow-up message.
- Expectation: Progress labels are step-specific, the continued session opens in its owning worktree instead of locally, reopening from recents routes to the worktree, and `/new` stays in the same worktree instead of falling back to the repo root.

### Escape closes slash/mention menus without aborting the agent
PR: #7669
- Conditions: Start any prompt that streams for a while.
- Command:
```
Write 80 short bullet points about this repository, one bullet per line.
```
- Action:
  1. While the response is still streaming, type `/` or `@` in the input so the slash or mention menu opens.
  2. Press `Escape`.
- Expectation: The open menu closes, but the running agent continues streaming and is not aborted.

### `apply_patch` tool rendering in the VS Code webview
PR: #7682
- Conditions: Use a prompt likely to make a small single-file edit.
- Command:
```
Make a tiny one-line change in a single file and explain what you changed.
```
- Action:
  1. Let the tool call finish.
  2. Inspect the `apply_patch` tool message in the chat.
- Expectation: The tool row uses the same compact inline meta layout as the edit tool, diff stats stay in the trigger row, and large patch results do not dump full before/after content into the message body.

### Agent Manager keyboard shortcut from the VS Code terminal
PR: #7514
- Conditions: Open a VS Code terminal and focus it.
- Action:
  1. Press `Cmd+Shift+M`.
  2. After Agent Manager opens, verify the prompt input is ready to type in immediately.
- Expectation: The shortcut works even when the terminal has focus, Agent Manager opens, and the input is focused reliably.

- Continue on a very long session
    - Check it doesn't hang on "Considering next steps..."

body:
### Description

I had a problem on my mac because of the kilo of code that caused spotlight to glitch every time, As a result, each time the computer took up 350GB of system data, And no matter how hard you tried to clean the cache and so on, I even reinstalled the system, the problem still remained that my entire disk was clogged on the new laptop, As a result, I solved the problem by restarting spotlight:


### Plugins

Kilo code

### Kilo version

last version

### Steps to reproduce

du -sh ~/* 2>/dev/null| sort -hr | head -20
sudo du -sh /System/Volumes/Data/.Spotlight-V100
sudo mdutil -a -i off
sudo rm -rf /System/Volumes/Data/.Spotlight-V100
sudo rebort // for mac restart

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

_No response_

### Terminal

_No response_

comments.body:
We need a bit more information to triage this issue effectively. Could you please provide additional details?

The user reports that Kilo Code caused macOS Spotlight to malfunction, consuming 350GB of disk space with Spotlight index data, and provides steps they used to resolve it by resetting Spotlight.

*This request was generated by Kilo Auto-Triage.*
Closing per the existing invalid label from triage.

I respectfully dispute the "invalid" label. I would like to provide more details, as this is a real, reproducible bug that causes significant disk space issues on macOS.

Why this is a Kilo Code issue:

    Before installing Kilo Code, my new MacBook had no Spotlight or system data issues.

    After using Kilo Code (which indexed a large codebase), Spotlight started glitching and consuming around 350 GB in .Spotlight-V100.

    The problem persisted even after a full OS reinstallation — until I manually cleared the Spotlight index using the commands I provided.

    As soon as I resumed normal use of Kilo Code, the same issue came back (bloated system data, Spotlight glitches).

Additional details you requested:

    macOS version: Sequoia 15.7.4

    Hardware: MacBook Pro M1 Pro, 2021, 512 GB SSD

    Codebase size: 2–3 GB, but it breaks the Spotlight process regardless of size

    Steps to reproduce (more precise):

        Install Kilo Code (latest version as of March 2025)

        Open a moderately sized codebase - in my case, a complex VueJS + NestJS project with about 150 files

        Ask Kilo Code to perform a complex refactoring task, such as: "Rename a variable across all files where it is used"

        Monitor with:
        sudo du -sh /System/Volumes/Data/.Spotlight-V100
        (size grows beyond 100 GB)

        Observe in System Settings → General → Storage → "System Data" ballooning to 300–400 GB

        Notice that Finder search and Spotlight become unresponsive

This is not a generic macOS issue - other IDEs (VS Code without Kilo, Cursor, etc.) do not trigger this behavior on the same machine with the same codebase. Only Kilo Code does.

Please reopen the issue or let me know what additional logging or diagnostic data you need. I am happy to run any commands to help you resolve this.

Thank you.
@4awel thanks for the details, I will have a look!

title: Bug memory crush Spotlight on MacOS

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

### Summary

Kilo CLI currently does not expose a tokens-per-second (tok/s) performance metric. Latency alone is insufficient for evaluating model performance, especially when comparing different prompts, models, or hardware configurations. You can also add these details to the sidebar within the conversation.

### Proposal

Add token-level performance metrics to the CLI output:

* Input tokens
* Output tokens
* Generation time (seconds)
* Tokens per second (output_tokens / generation_time)

Optional: enable via `--metrics` or `--verbose` flag to avoid cluttering default output.

### Example Output

```
Latency: 4.21s  
Output tokens: 512  
Tokens/sec: 121.6
```

### Rationale

Exposing tok/s enables reliable benchmarking, regression testing, hardware comparison, and optimization analysis. This metric is a de facto standard in LLM tooling.

comments.body:
+1 on this. Two extensions to consider while it's being scoped:

Extend to the VSCode top bar, not just CLI. The same numbers belong next to the existing Tokens ↑ 316 ↓ 351 ↓ cache 144.2K line in the VSCode UI — local-LLM users live in that view and currently have to tail llama-server's stdout to see throughput. Something like:

> Tokens ↑ 316  ↓ 351  ↓ cache 144.2K   PP 412 t/s   TG 38 t/s

Split prompt-processing speed from generation speed. llama.cpp returns both as separate fields (prompt_per_second and predicted_per_second in the timings object on each non-streaming response, also surfaced in streaming events with stream_options: { include_usage: true }). They behave very differently — PP is bottlenecked by batch size and KV cache, TG by memory bandwidth — so one combined number hides exactly the information that's useful for tuning quantization, -ngl GPU offload, and batch settings. Two values, label them PP and TG.

For providers that don't return timings (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini), the row hides — no new config needed, opportunistic detection on the timings field. Bonus if it lands: a session-level rolling average on hover, since power users comparing quants want medians over a turn or two, not just the last value.

Happy to test on Windows + a couple of common llama.cpp builds when there's something to try.

> +1 on this. Two extensions to consider while it's being scoped:
> 
> Extend to the VSCode top bar, not just CLI. The same numbers belong next to the existing Tokens ↑ 316 ↓ 351 ↓ cache 144.2K line in the VSCode UI — local-LLM users live in that view and currently have to tail llama-server's stdout to see throughput. Something like:
> 
> > Tokens ↑ 316  ↓ 351  ↓ cache 144.2K   PP 412 t/s   TG 38 t/s

> Split prompt-processing speed from generation speed. llama.cpp returns both as separate fields (prompt_per_second and predicted_per_second in the timings object on each non-streaming response, also surfaced in streaming events with stream_options: { include_usage: true }). They behave very differently — PP is bottlenecked by batch size and KV cache, TG by memory bandwidth — so one combined number hides exactly the information that's useful for tuning quantization, -ngl GPU offload, and batch settings. Two values, label them PP and TG.

> For providers that don't return timings (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini), the row hides — no new config needed, opportunistic detection on the timings field. Bonus if it lands: a session-level rolling average on hover, since power users comparing quants want medians over a turn or two, not just the last value.

Hello @kilo-code-bot

Node.js 24 is installed on the WSL Instance where the project is and not on the Windows host.

Could the Plugin also use the WSL-Node-JS version that is configured on Project level?
You should have nodejs installed on windows HOST, that's where the plugin runs.

@hyh19962008 the idea is running on windows host, but it loads the project using network path `\\wsl.localhost\`, and this is the problem, extension should run commands in WSL with Linux paths `/home/<user-folder/`.

body:
## Summary

We should consider re-adding Architect mode, or at minimum enhancing Plan mode so it writes plan files as `.md` to a `/plans` directory in the project root. This was a native Kilo behavior that users adopted and now miss.

## Context from internal feedback

- Plan mode currently exists but **cannot write the plan as an `.md` file to `/plans`** in the user's project.
- Plan mode does persist plans, but they're hidden inside the `.opencode` directory — not easily accessible or visible to the user.
- The old Architect mode would always write the plan into the `/plans` folder when finished. Users adopted this workflow and notice it's gone.
- Architect as a separate mode isn't necessarily missed — what's missed is the **behavior** of persisting a readable plan file in the project.
- Suggestion: Allow Plan mode to both persist internally *and* create `.md` files in a `/plans` directory.
- Alternative: It's relatively easy to add a global custom mode, but having this as a built-in behavior is preferred.

## Proposed Options

1. **Enhance Plan mode** to also write `.md` plan files to a `/plans` directory in the project root (in addition to internal `.opencode` persistence).
2. **Re-add Architect mode** as a distinct mode that writes plans to `/plans`.
3. **Prompt Plan mode** to optionally create `.md` files in the plan directory when finishing a plan.

## References

- Internal Slack thread in #new-vscode-feedback

comments.body:
This issue might be related to or a duplicate of existing discussions:
- #1284: "Architect mode" - An existing open issue about Architect mode functionality

Before proceeding, please review that issue to ensure this request isn't already being tracked there or if there's additional context that would help inform this feature request.

My preference is that the plan file is hidden/temporary, but I can see the value of having it for some users' workflows. I think having both Plan and Architect mode would be confusing to users, if the only difference is where they write plan files. Perhaps the option to write plan files in `/plans` could be a user setting? Alternatively, having it as a prompted option.

Please bring back this feature which is crucial for our teams development workflow. Architect mode is the most critical differentiation Kilo has over competitors and is the reason everyone is on Kilo. As we design a feature, we go back and forth with the "architect" iterating over the plan as many times as needed. Once the dev is happy with the plan, other dev in the team does a peer review. Since the context window is usually 170K tokens that were needed for the conversation and iterations, we almost always start a new conversation but in "code" mode this time and the 1-liner we say is to implement that plan. Plans are very thorough so code mode has no deviations from what was designed. Reviewing an MD file is a million times more convenient and faster than going over a long chat to review the different evolutions. The plan is the final output of the design so is a crucial component for us.

Thanks for making this amazing Kilo product!
Gaston

Thanks all. I think I'm inclined to convert `Plan` to `Architect`. In my testing of Plan, it does not regularly write plan files, but will if you ask it. It also is capable of writing elsewhere and making changes (in my experience), which may be a bug but is surprising and I think unexpected. 

You don't want to suddenly start having your agent making changes when in one of these modes. I don't think it's worth having both, as they are very close in behavior. 

Along side this we should support the ability to change modes either automatically or via a permission prompt like the current extension, but that is a separate issue.

To stay organized issues are automatically closed after 60 days of no activity. If the issue is still relevant please reopen it or create a fresh new one.
We are working on this right now, but it will replace plan mode.

The same problem occurs with Kilo CLI/TUI directly on the MacOS terminal. 

From the UX perspective, there are 2 separate issues here:

## Issue 1: There is no way for the user to ENABLE a disabled MCP server from the CLI/TUI

Currently there is no way to ENABLE a disabled MCP server, other than directly editing the config file. The `/mcps` slash command in the TUI does not actually display any MCPs from the config that have `enabled: false`, and there is no `kilo mcp enable` command... 

## Issue 2: Disabling an MCP via `/mcps` in the TUI is not persistent

It feels to the user as if they're disabling the MCP in their global setting, but in fact the setting only seems to hold for the current terminal session — and is not written to the config file. body:
# Issue: Snapshot Git Objects Growing to 68GB - Needs Automatic git gc

## Description

The kilo snapshot storage in `~/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/global/objects/` grew to **68GB** on my system, filling 95% of my 233GB disk. This appears to be caused by git object accumulation without automatic garbage collection.

## Environment
- OS: Linux
- Kilo version: Latest
- Disk: 233GB (95% full before fix)

## Current Behavior

The snapshot directory structure appears to be a git repository:
```
~/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/global/
├── objects/     <- This grew to 68GB
├── refs/
├── hooks/
└── ...
```

## Solution That Worked

Running git garbage collection dramatically reduced the size:

```bash
cd ~/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/global
git gc --aggressive --prune=now
```

**Result:** 68GB → 288MB (99.6% reduction!)

## Related Issues
- #536 - Kilocode takes up a lot of disk space (closed, but focused on task history)
- Discussion #5406 - tasks checkpoints in project as a single repository

## Proposed Solutions

### Option 1: Automatic git gc
Run `git gc --aggressive --prune=now` periodically or when:
- Snapshot size exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5GB)
- On VSCode startup if last gc was > 7 days ago
- Before creating new checkpoints

### Option 2: Git configuration
Set git config to auto-gc more aggressively:
```bash
git config gc.auto 256
git config gc.autoDetach false
```

### Option 3: User-facing feature
Add a "Cleanup Snapshots" button in Settings > Checkpoints similar to the existing task cleanup feature.

## Temporary Workaround for Users

Add this to a weekly cron:
```bash
0 2 * * 0 cd ~/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/global && git gc --aggressive --prune=now
```

Or create a script to monitor and auto-clean:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
SNAPSHOT_DIR="$HOME/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/global"
SIZE_GB=$(du -sg "$SNAPSHOT_DIR" 2>/dev/null | cut -f1)
if [ "$SIZE_GB" -gt 10 ]; then
    cd "$SNAPSHOT_DIR" && git gc --aggressive --prune=now
fi
```

## Impact

This issue can cause:
- Disk space exhaustion
- System instability
- VSCode slowdowns
- User frustration when trying to save files

The fix is simple and safe - git gc is a standard git maintenance operation that compresses loose objects without losing data.

---

Would the maintainers be open to a PR that adds automatic snapshot garbage collection?

comments.body:
The snapshot system already has garbage collection and runs on startup and hourly via the scheduler. The 68GB growth you experienced suggests the existing gc parameters aren't aggressive enough for heavy workloads. Here's what the code reveals about why:

## Existing Snapshot Maintenance

| Commit | What it added |
|--------|--------------|
| [`e2f1f4d`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/commit/e2f1f4d81e) | Scheduler + `git gc --prune=7.days` running hourly |
| [`ac0b37a`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/commit/ac0b37a7b7) | `info/exclude` syncing to respect project-level excludes |

`Snapshot.cleanup()` in `snapshot/index.ts` runs `git gc --prune=7.days` on the snapshot repository. It's registered with the scheduler on startup (runs immediately, then hourly). The snapshot directory lives at `~/.local/share/kilo/snapshot/<project-id>/` — in your case, `global` is the project ID used when git can't derive one from root commits.

## Why 68GB Still Accumulates

**1. 7-day prune window vs. high creation rate:** `Snapshot.track()` is called at both `start-step` and `finish-step` in the session processor. Each call runs `git add .` then `write-tree`, creating loose tree and blob objects. With `--prune=7.days`, all objects from the past week survive gc regardless. For an active user generating hundreds of snapshots per day, a week of accumulated loose objects can be massive.

**2. `write-tree` without commits:** The snapshot system creates tree objects but never creates commits or refs pointing to them. These remain as loose unreferenced objects until the prune window expires. Without commit chains, git's pack optimization (delta compression between similar objects) is less effective during standard gc.

**3. The bulk of your fix was `--prune=now`:** Your manual `gc --aggressive --prune=now` achieved 99.6% reduction (68GB to 288MB). The primary contributor to that reduction is likely `--prune=now` eliminating the entire 7-day backlog of loose objects, rather than the `--aggressive` flag's deeper delta compression. This points to the prune window as the main tuning knob.

## Regarding a PR

Your proposed solutions in the issue align well with what the code analysis suggests. Reducing the prune window (from `7.days` to something shorter) would have the most impact for the least complexity. A size-based trigger for more aggressive gc would handle the edge cases.

The one thing already in place that your proposal assumes is missing: the hourly `git gc` does already run. So the PR scope would be about **tuning the existing gc parameters** rather than adding gc from scratch.
And today I had issue with creating snapshot. Agent works on 13 to-dos. And then after cca. 15 min I saw: Initialize snapshot, but it froze their, initialising took 10 min and then I stop agent.

labels.description: Put on the board of the backend team.

This is required for tools to work correctly, and missing thought_signature may lead to degraded model performance. Additional data, function call `default_api:read_file` , position 2. Please refer to https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/thought-signatures for more details.\",\n    \"status\": \"INVALID_ARGUMENT\"\n  }\n}\n","code":400,"status":"Bad Request"}}

comments.body: Same problem in the VS Code extension
Same issue
This also happens for gemini-3.1-pro-preview-customtools

## Diagnostic Analysis

I investigated the full message pipeline to understand where `thoughtSignature` is lost:

**Pipeline**: `processor.ts` (capture) → SQLite (storage) → `message-v2.ts:toModelMessages()` (replay) → AI SDK `convertToModelMessages` → `@ai-sdk/google` converter → Gemini API

### Findings

1. **Unit tests**: `toModelMessages()` correctly preserves `providerMetadata` (containing `thoughtSignature`) for same-model Gemini sessions. The `differentModel` guard at line 600 evaluates `false` when models match, and metadata flows through.

2. **E2E test**: Ran a live tool-call against `gemini-3.1-pro-preview` using `@ai-sdk/google@2.0.54`. The SDK emits `thoughtSignature` in `providerMetadata` on every `tool-call` event, and the response message includes it in `providerOptions.google.thoughtSignature`. Full round-trip works.

3. **Root cause (likely resolved)**: Commit `021e42c0b` added the `differentModel` guard to strip cross-provider metadata. The original comparison used `model.api.id` on one side but `msg.info.modelID` (set from `model.id`) on the other — a mismatch that could cause `differentModel` to evaluate `true` for same-model Gemini sessions, stripping the `thoughtSignature` metadata.

### This appears fixed in v5.9.0

The JetBrains plugin [v5.9.0 release notes](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/plugin/28350-kilo-code/versions/stable/963706) include:

> fix: preserve extra_content for Gemini 3 thought_signature support

The issue was filed against v5.8.0. **Could you update to v5.9.0 and verify whether the issue persists?**

If it does, I have diagnostic tests and an E2E tracer ready to dig deeper. I've also opened #6368 to add regression test coverage for Gemini `thoughtSignature` metadata preservation — this scenario had zero test coverage before.

body:
## Description

Users are unable to paste text into the Kilo CLI interface. This affects:
- Pasting API keys during `/connect` setup
- Pasting messages/prompts
- Copying and pasting any text

## Environment
- Platform: Linux
- Kilo CLI installed via npm

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run `kilo` to start the TUI
2. Attempt to paste text (API key, message, etc.) using standard paste shortcuts (Ctrl+V, Ctrl+Shift+V, right-click paste)
3. Paste does not work - nothing is pasted into the input field

## Expected Behavior
Text should paste into the CLI input field when using standard paste shortcuts.

## Actual Behavior
Nothing happens when attempting to paste. This makes the CLI unusable for users who need to paste API keys or longer text.

## Additional Context
This has been reported by multiple users. Some workarounds that may or may not work:
- Different paste shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+V, right-click)
- Using `/editor` to open an external editor

## Impact
Critical - users cannot set up the CLI without being able to paste API keys.

comments.body:
## Related Issues

This appears to be part of a broader clipboard/paste issue across Kilo products:

### kilocode repository:

---

Kilo Code requires Node.js to work.
Node.js by default uses its own list of trusted root certificates, ignoring the system certificate store.

**To fix this:**

1. Download your corporate certificates from your internal source.
2. Convert the `.cer` certificate to `.pem`:
   ```bash
   openssl x509 -inform der -in "./root_ca.cer" -out ./root_ca.pem
   ```
3. Add the following to your `.zshrc` or `.bashrc`:
   ```bash
   export NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS="/path/to/root_ca.pem"
   ```
4. In IntelliJ IDEA, go to **Settings > Tools > Server Certificates** and add both original `.cer` corporate certificates to the trusted list.
5. Now the plugin will work, but **only when launching IDEA from the terminal**, since the graphical interface (DE/Snap) doesn't see shell environment variables.

**To make Kilo Code work when launching IDEA from the application icon, additional steps are required:**

6. Find the Snap application desktop file:
   ```
   /var/lib/snapd/desktop/applications/intellij-idea-ultimate_intellij-idea-ultimate.desktop
   ```
7. Copy it to the user applications directory:
   ```bash
   cp /var/lib/snapd/desktop/applications/intellij-idea-ultimate_intellij-idea-ultimate.desktop ~/.local/share/applications/
   ```
8. Open the file in a text editor:
   ```bash
   nano ~/.local/share/applications/intellij-idea-ultimate_intellij-idea-ultimate.desktop
   ```
9. Replace the line:
   ```
   Exec=/snap/bin/intellij-idea-ultimate %f
   ```
   with:
   ```
   Exec=env NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS="/absolute/path/to/root_ca.pem" /snap/bin/intellij-idea-ultimate %f
   ```
   (Make sure to use the absolute path to your certificate file)
10. Save, close the editor, and update the desktop database:
    ```bash
    update-desktop-database ~/.local/share/applications/
    ```
11. Launch IDEA and test the plugin — it should now work.

body:
Currently we only support the codestral-2508 model for autocomplete. We have thoroughly tested this model with both the FIM endpoint which we complete and traditional chat-completions endpoint using the HoleFiller prompt.

We'd like to add more models but have to make sure these other models
1) work well
2) aren't expensive (as autocomplete does a lot of requests)

Therefore we probably do not want to allow full model choice like we do with chat, but probably something more similar to how we do it with Fast Apply.

To implement this we need (at least)

a) an interface to select from a limited set of providers/models
b) a procedure to test whether we deem the model good enough (probably using src/test-llm-completions, and some minimum requirements)

As for people who want this and find this issue, please tell us what models you'd want and why. We did try a lot of models before deciding on this one, and for instance grok-code-fast is free atm so it would seem like a good choice, but it is too slow to use for autocomplete.

i think this link got removed 
the instructions are 
Go to help -> Find Action
Type -> 'Choose Boot Java Runtime for the IDE'
From the dropdow 'New' select a Boot runtime wich support JCEF browser
Select and restart

1. In Android Studio, press Ctrl + Shift + A
2. Search for "Choose Boot Java Runtime for the IDE"
3. Click it
4. Select a runtime from dropdown
5. Click OK
6. Android Studio will restart and download the runtime
7. After restart, Kilo Code should initialize properly.

In Android Studio, press Ctrl + Shift + A or go to Help → Find Action, then search for Choose Boot Java Runtime for the IDE. From the New dropdown, select a boot runtime that supports JCEF browser, apply the change, and restart Android Studio.

if ($env:JEDITERM_SOURCE) {

body: ### Description **Steps to reproduce:** 1. Create a dev container project and start a chat. 2. Rebuild and reopen the dev container. 3. Click existing chat. **Expected result:** Chat history should be opened. **Actual result:** Chat is not found, chat entry has a red background, and an error message is found: > Task file not found for task ID: 0be1e29e-c40a-4bc1-b491-bfbd92068e5b (file /home/vscode/.vscode-server/data/User/globalStorage/kilocode.kilo-code/tasks/0be1e29e-c40a-4bc1-b491-bfbd92068e5b/api_conversation_history.json) ``` ls /home/vscode/.vscode-server/data/User/globalStorage/kilocode.kilo-code/tasks 0be1e29e-c40a-4bc1-b491-bfbd92068e5b 7b36a14e-1784-4fd6-b166-abeb7cc5b82b b257dbbb-bee3-4bee-9383-61107a9ad8ee ls /home/vscode/.vscode-server/data/User/globalStorage/kilocode.kilo-code/tasks/0be1e29e-c40a-4bc1-b491-bfbd92068e5b [EMPTY] ``` The number of task dirs matches the number of chats. Cursor handles this better for Kilo Code, and history is persisted between container rebuilds. As a side note, Cursor and VSC do not share history because Cursor's path is `.cursor-server`. It should be at least mentioned in the docs, and preferably implemented in a way like Cursor handles Kilo Code's and its own history - it's tied to the dev container, but it survives rebuilding. Side note: Cursor also shares Kilo Code settings between dev containers / Windows (like global settings), VSCode does not - each dev container has to be set individually (with pros and cons). **Temporary workaround:** use mounted volume ``` "mounts": [ "source=vscode-user-data,target=/home/vscode/.vscode-server/data/User,type=volume", "source=cursor-user-data,target=/home/vscode/.cursor-server/data/User,type=volume", ```
